topics.title: Motivation and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Motivation and Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Numerical Methods** are a set of algorithmic procedures designed to obtain approximate but accurate solutions to mathematical problems that cannot be, or are difficult to be, solved exactly by analytical (closed-form) techniques. In the context of **Process Engineering**, they enable the solution of governing equations such as material/energy balances, reaction kinetics, transport phenomena, and process control models that often result in nonlinear algebraic equations, coupled ODE/PDE systems, or non-integrable integrals. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** > Numerical methods are systematic, iterative computational techniques used to convert continuous, nonlinear, or transcendental engineering problems into discrete arithmetic operations yielding numerical results within a prescribed accuracy. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to measure the exact length of a curved, twisted rope on a table. You cannot apply the simple formula for a straight line ($L = d$). Instead, you lay a measuring tape along the curve at very small, finite intervals and sum the small straight segments. The smaller the interval, the closer you get to the "true" curved length. **Numerical methods work the same way:** - The **curved rope** = a real, complex engineering phenomenon (e.g., concentration profile in a reactor). - The **small straight segments** = discrete arithmetic operations performed by the computer. - The **measuring tape** = the chosen numerical algorithm (e.g., Finite Difference, Newton-Raphson, Simpson's Rule). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Process Engineers Cannot Live Without Numerical Methods:** > - **Non-linearity:** Real processes follow Arrhenius kinetics, Antoine equations, and complex VLE relationships — almost no analytical solutions exist. > - **Coupled Systems:** A typical CSTR model couples mass, energy, and momentum balances simultaneously. > - **Geometry/Complexity:** Distillation columns, heat exchangers, and reactors involve complex geometries and multidimensional gradients. ### 1.3 Key Quantities & Standard Metrics - **True Error:** $E_t = \vert \text{True Value} - \text{Approximate Value} \vert$ - **Absolute Error:** $\epsilon_a = \vert x_{\text{new}} - x_{\text{old}} \vert$ - **Relative Error:** $\epsilon_r = \left\vert \dfrac{x_{\text{new}} - x_{\text{old}}}{x_{\text{new}}} \right\vert \times 100\%$ - **Significant Digits:** At least **2** significant digits of accuracy are required for a KTU numerical answer to be considered correct. - **Convergence Tolerance ($\epsilon_s$):** Typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ for engineering problems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Motivation: Why Do Process Engineers Need Numerical Methods? The motivation stems from the **limitations of analytical mathematics** when applied to real chemical/process systems: 1. **Non-linear Governing Equations:** Most industrial models (e.g., CSTR with Arrhenius kinetics) yield transcendental/non-linear algebraic equations with no closed-form solutions. 2. **Complex Boundary Conditions:** Multi-component diffusion, heat transfer with phase change, and reacting flows involve BCs that are mathematically intractable. 3. **Large Sparse Linear Systems:** Stage-by-stage calculations in distillation columns (MESH equations) result in hundreds of coupled linear/nonlinear equations. 4. **Integration of Experimental Data:** Plant data must be integrated/differentiated, but data exists only at discrete points. 5. **Optimization & Control:** Finding optimal operating points (e.g., maximum yield) requires iterative numerical search. ### 2.2 Sources of Errors in Numerical Computations A KTU-favorite conceptual question. Always classify errors correctly: | Error Type | Definition | Example in Process Engg. | |---|---|---| | **Modeling Error** | Error due to simplifying assumptions in deriving the mathematical model | Neglecting axial dispersion in a PFR | | **Blunder (Gross Error)** | Human mistakes in coding, data entry, or formula transcription | Typing "$\times$" instead of "$+$" in a heat balance | | **Truncation Error** | Error from terminating a mathematical series/process early | Stopping Taylor series expansion of $\sin(x)$ after 2 terms | | **Round-off Error** | Error from finite word-length storage (e.g., **64-bit** floating point) | Storing $1/3 = 0.3333$ instead of $0.3333...$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Truncation error is reduced by *refining the method* (smaller step size, higher order), while round-off error *increases* when you refine too much — leading to the concept of **Total Numerical Error = Truncation Error + Round-off Error**, which has a minimum at an optimal step size. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | True Error | $E_t = \vert x_{\text{true}} - x_{\text{approx}} \vert$ | Theoretical measure of accuracy | | Approximate Relative Error | $\vert \epsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \dfrac{x_i - x_{i-1}}{x_i} \right\vert \times 100\%$ | Stopping criterion in iterations | | Number of Significant Digits | $s = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{2-n}}{\vert \epsilon_a \vert}\right)$ | KTU numerical answer validation | | Taylor Series (Truncation Source) | $f(x_{i+1}) = f(x_i) + h f'(x_i) + \dfrac{h^2}{2}f''(x_i) + \ldots$ | Basis of all ODE/Integration methods | | Polynomial / Order of Error | $E \propto O(h^n)$ where $n$ is the method order | Truncation error magnitude | ### 2.4 Major Application Areas in Process Engineering 1. **Roots of Equations:** Solving nonlinear single equations — e.g., finding the bubble/dew point, equilibrium conversion in a reactor, or operating pressure. 2. **Linear Algebraic Equations:** Solving simultaneous mass/energy balances in distillation, heat exchanger networks, and steady-state process simulators. 3. **Curve Fitting & Regression:** Fitting VLE data to Wilson/NRTL models; correlating heat transfer coefficients with Reynolds number. 4. **Numerical Integration (Quadrature):** Computing work done in compression, total heat duty, or area under a chromatogram. 5. **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs):** Batch reactor kinetics, startup transients, and dynamic response of level/temperature controllers. 6. **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs):** 2D/3D temperature profiles in reactors, diffusion with reaction, fluid flow in pipes. 7. **Optimization:** Maximizing profit, minimizing energy in a process flowsheet, reactor network synthesis. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Taylor Series as the Foundation of Numerical Methods The Taylor series is the **mathematical backbone** of essentially every numerical method. Here we derive it step-by-step from first principles. **Step 1: Assume a polynomial approximation** Suppose $f(x)$ is smooth and continuous. We want to approximate $f(x_{i+1})$ given $f(x_i)$, where $h = x_{i+1} - x_i$. $$f(x_{i+1}) = a_0 + a_1 h + a_2 h^2 + a_3 h^3 + a_4 h^4 + \ldots$$ **Step 2: Apply boundary condition at $h = 0$** Substituting $h = 0$ (i.e., $x_{i+1} = x_i$): $$f(x_i) = a_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a_0 = f(x_i)$$ **Step 3: Differentiate once and evaluate at $h = 0$** $$\frac{df(x_{i+1})}{dx} = \frac{d}{dh}\!\left[a_0 + a_1 h + a_2 h^2 + \ldots\right] = a_1 + 2 a_2 h + 3 a_3 h^2 + \ldots$$ At $h = 0$: $$f'(x_i) = a_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a_1 = f'(x_i)$$ **Step 4: Differentiate twice and evaluate at $h = 0$** $$\frac{d^2 f}{dx^2}\bigg|_{x_i} = 2!\, a_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a_2 = \frac{f''(x_i)}{2!}$$ **Step 5: Differentiate $n$ times and evaluate at $h = 0$** $$f^{(n)}(x_i) = n!\, a_n \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a_n = \frac{f^{(n)}(x_i)}{n!}$$ **Step 6: Substitute back to obtain the Taylor Series Expansion** $$f(x_{i+1}) = f(x_i) + f'(x_i)\,h + \frac{f''(x_i)}{2!}h^2 + \frac{f'''(x_i)}{3!}h^3 + \ldots$$ $$f(x_{i+1}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{f^{(n)}(x_i)}{n!} h^n$$ **Step 7: Truncate to get a numerical approximation (this is where truncation error is born)** If we stop at the first-order term: $$f(x_{i+1}) \approx f(x_i) + f'(x_i)\,h$$ The remaining terms are the truncation error: $$R_n = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}\,h^{n+1}, \quad \text{for some } \xi \in (x_i, x_{i+1})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This single Taylor expansion justifies Euler's method for ODEs ($f(x_{i+1}) \approx f(x_i) + h f'(x_i)$), Newton-Raphson for root finding (retain linear term), and Finite Difference approximations for derivatives (subtract two Taylor expansions). **Knowing the Taylor series is therefore the single most important skill for KTU numerical exams.** ### 3.2 Worked Example: Number of Significant Digits in Iteration **Problem:** In an iterative root-finding scheme, the first three iterations yield $x_0 = 0.5$, $x_1 = 0.487$, $x_2 = 0.4861$. Find the approximate relative error and the minimum number of correct significant digits at $x_2$. **Solution:** $$\vert \epsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \frac{x_2 - x_1}{x_2} \right\vert \times 100\%$$ Substituting values: $$\vert \epsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \frac{0.4861 - 0.487}{0.4861} \right\vert \times 100\%$$ $$\vert \epsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \frac{-0.0009}{0.4861} \right\vert \times 100\% \approx 0.185\%$$ Using the formula for significant digits: $$s = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5 \times 10^{2-n}}{0.00185}\right) \geq 0$$ We test $n = 2$: $$s = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5 \times 10^{0}}{0.00185}\right) = \log_{10}(270.27) \approx 2.43$$ Since $s \geq 2$, the result is correct to at least **2 significant digits**. This satisfies the KTU minimum standard. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Numerical Methods Pipeline in Process Engineering ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Real World Process Problem] --> B[Mathematical Model Formulation] B --> C{Analytically Solvable?} C -- Yes --> D[Closed-Form Analytical Solution] C -- No --> E[Select Numerical Method] E --> F[Algorithm: Discretize and Iterate] F --> G[Numerical Solution Approximation] G --> H{Convergence Achieved?} H -- No --> I[Refine Step Size / Order] I --> F H -- Yes --> J[Validate with Experimental Data] J --> K[Engineering Decision Making] K --> L[Reactor Design, Plant Operation, Optimization] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Sources of Numerical Error Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Numerical Error] --> B[Modeling Error] A --> C[Blunder Error] A --> D[Truncation Error] A --> E[Round-off Error] D --> D1[Reduced by: Refining Step Size, Higher Order Method] E --> E1[Increased by: Excessive Refinement, Finite Word Length] D1 --> F[Optimal Step Size Exists] E1 --> F F --> G[Minimum Total Error Point] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Application Mapping of Numerical Methods in Process Engineering ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Root Finding
Newton Raphson, Bisection] --> A1[Bubble Point Calculation] M1 --> A2[Reactor Equilibrium Conversion] M2[Linear Algebra
Gauss Elimination, LU] --> A3[Distillation MESH Equations] M2 --> A4[Heat Exchanger Network] M3[ODE Solvers
RK4, Euler] --> A5[Batch Reactor Kinetics] M3 --> A6[Controller Tuning Transient] M4[PDE Solvers
Finite Difference] --> A7[Temperature Profile in Catalyst Pellet] M4 --> A8[2D Reactor Heat Profile] M5[Regression
Least Squares] --> A9[VLE Data Fitting NRTL Wilson] M5 --> A10[Heat Transfer Correlation Development] M6[Integration
Simpson Trapezoidal] --> A11[Compressor Work Duty] M6 --> A12[Chromatogram Area Quantification] M7[Optimization
Newton Line Search] --> A13[Profit Maximization] M7 --> A14[Energy Minimization] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:** > The block diagrams above represent the **conceptual flow** that examiners expect you to draw or describe in 7-mark application-oriented questions. Memorize the mapping of *method → industrial application*. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define numerical methods. Why are they essential in process engineering?* **Model Answer:** Numerical methods are systematic computational techniques used to obtain approximate solutions to mathematical problems that lack closed-form analytical solutions. They are essential in process engineering because real-world systems — involving non-linear Arrhenius kinetics, complex VLE, multi-component mass transfer, and coupled PDEs — almost never yield to analytical techniques. Numerical methods allow engineers to simulate reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, and process controllers within acceptable accuracy, enabling design, optimization, and safety analysis. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Distinguish between truncation error and round-off error with one process engineering example each.* **Model Answer:** - **Truncation Error:** Arises from using a finite approximation (e.g., truncated Taylor series) instead of the exact infinite expression. *Example:* Using Euler's method $y_{i+1} = y_i + h f(x_i, y_i)$ to integrate a reactor ODE introduces truncation error proportional to $h^2$. - **Round-off Error:** Arises from the finite precision of digital computers (e.g., **64-bit double precision**). *Example:* Storing a very large temperature difference $T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}}$ where both are nearly equal, causing loss of significant digits (catastrophic cancellation). --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the Taylor series expansion of $f(x_{i+1})$ about $x_i$ up to the fourth-order term. Clearly identify the truncation error term. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A root-finding iteration produces the sequence: $x_0 = 2.0$, $x_1 = 1.85$, $x_2 = 1.8473$, $x_3 = 1.84721$. Compute the approximate relative error at $x_3$ and state the number of significant digits guaranteed correct. Comment on convergence. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Taylor Series Derivation:** Assuming $f(x)$ is infinitely differentiable, expanding around $x_i$ with step $h = x_{i+1} - x_i$: $$f(x_{i+1}) = f(x_i) + f'(x_i)\,h + \frac{f''(x_i)}{2!}h^2 + \frac{f'''(x_i)}{3!}h^3 + \frac{f^{(4)}(x_i)}{4!}h^4 + R_4$$ The truncation error is the remainder: $$R_4 = \frac{f^{(5)}(\xi)}{5!}h^5, \quad \xi \in (x_i, x_{i+1})$$ Therefore, the truncated (approximate) form is: $$f(x_{i+1}) \approx f(x_i) + f'(x_i)\,h + \frac{f''(x_i)}{2!}h^2 + \frac{f'''(x_i)}{3!}h^3 + \frac{f^{(4)}(x_i)}{4!}h^4$$ with order of accuracy $O(h^5)$. **(b) Error and Significant Digits:** Step 1: Approximate relative error at $x_3$: $$\vert \epsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \frac{x_3 - x_2}{x_3} \right\vert \times 100\%$$ $$\vert \epsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \frac{1.84721 - 1.8473}{1.84721} \right\vert \times 100\%$$ $$\vert \epsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \frac{-0.00009}{1.84721} \right\vert \times 100\% \approx 0.00487\%$$ Step 2: Significant digits. Using: $$0.5 \times 10^{2-n} \leq \vert \epsilon_a \vert$$ Testing $n = 4$: $$0.5 \times 10^{-2} = 0.005$$ Since $0.00487\% = 4.87 \times 10^{-5} < 0.005$, the inequality is satisfied. Therefore, the result is correct to **at least 4 significant digits**. The sequence is clearly convergent as the relative error has dropped by an order of magnitude from iteration to iteration. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Classify the different types of errors encountered in numerical computations. Give a process engineering example for each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A CSTR material balance gives the nonlinear equation $f(C) = C^3 - 6C^2 + 11C - 6 = 0$ for exit concentration. Using the Taylor series, derive a first-order numerical approximation for $f(x_{i+1})$ given $f(x_i)$ and $h$, and show how this becomes the basis of the Newton-Raphson formula. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Classification of Errors:** 1. **Modeling Error:** Discrepancy between the simplified mathematical model and the real process. *Example:* Assuming constant density in a liquid-phase reactor when temperature varies significantly. 2. **Blunder (Gross Error):** Human mistakes in computation, programming, or data entry. *Example:* Writing the Antoine equation with reversed sign in the log term. 3. **Truncation Error:** Result of approximating an infinite process by a finite one. *Example:* Stopping the exponential series $e^{-k\tau}$ at 2 terms while modeling first-order reactor decay. 4. **Round-off Error:** Due to finite computer precision. *Example:* Computing $1 - e^{-1000} \approx 0$ due to underflow in floating-point representation. **(b) Newton-Raphson from Taylor Series:** Starting from the Taylor expansion truncated at the first-order term: $$f(x_{i+1}) \approx f(x_i) + f'(x_i)(x_{i+1} - x_i)$$ We seek the root, so set $f(x_{i+1}) = 0$: $$0 = f(x_i) + f'(x_i)(x_{i+1} - x_i)$$ Solving for $x_{i+1}$: $$x_{i+1} = x_i - \frac{f(x_i)}{f'(x_i)}$$ This is the **Newton-Raphson iteration formula**. For the given CSTR polynomial: $$f(C) = C^3 - 6C^2 + 11C - 6$$ $$f'(C) = 3C^2 - 12C + 11$$ The iteration becomes: $$C_{i+1} = C_i - \frac{C_i^3 - 6C_i^2 + 11C_i - 6}{3C_i^2 - 12C_i + 11}$$ Starting with $C_0 = 0.5$ would yield rapid convergence to one of the roots $C = 1, 2,$ or $3$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** When applying numerical methods to reactor problems, students often forget to convert residence time $\tau$ from minutes to seconds if the rate constant $k$ is in $s^{-1}$. **This is the #1 mark-deduction reason in KTU papers.** > 2. **Convergence vs. Correctness:** A low approximate relative error ($\epsilon_a$) does *not* always mean the iteration is converging to the **correct** physical root. Always verify the answer against physical bounds (e.g., $0 \leq X \leq 1$ for conversion). > 3. **Taylor Series Truncation Sign:** Students frequently forget the higher-order term contributes to the error and write $f(x_{i+1}) = f(x_i) + f'(x_i)h$ as exact, losing 1-2 marks. > 4. **Significant Digit Calculation:** Do *not* confuse the formula $0.5 \times 10^{2-n}$ with $0.5 \times 10^{n-2}$. The exponent is always $2 - n$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Numerical methods** = approximate arithmetic solutions to problems lacking closed-form answers. - **Why for Process Engineering?** Non-linear kinetics, coupled MESH equations, complex BCs, real plant data. - **Four error types:** Modeling, Blunder, Truncation, Round-off — know the definition and one engineering example for each. - **Total Error = Truncation + Round-off.** An *optimal step size* exists that minimizes this. - **Taylor Series** is the single most important foundation — it justifies Euler's method, Newton-Raphson, and finite differences. - **Approximate Relative Error formula:** $\vert \epsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \dfrac{x_i - x_{i-1}}{x_i} \right\vert \times 100\%$ - **Significant digits criterion:** $0.5 \times 10^{2-n} \leq \vert \epsilon_a \vert$. KTU expects **at least 2** correct significant digits. - **Major applications:** Root finding (bubble point), Linear algebra (distillation MESH), ODEs (reactor dynamics), PDEs (catalyst pellet profiles), Regression (VLE data), Integration (compressor work), Optimization (profit). - **Always cross-check** numerical roots against physical constraints before finalizing your answer. - **Convergence check:** Dropping $\epsilon_a$ by an order of magnitude per iteration indicates stable, monotonic convergence.

topics.title: Accuracy and Precision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Accuracy and Precision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Accuracy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accuracy** is the closeness of a computed or measured value to the **true value** (also called the *exact* or *expected* value). It quantifies how well a numerical method or instrument reproduces the correct answer.

Mathematically, if $x_t$ is the true value and $x_a$ is the approximate (measured) value, then accuracy is reflected by the magnitude of the **true error**:
$$E_t = x_t - x_a$$

> [!NOTE]
> **High accuracy** $\Rightarrow$ Small $\vert E_t \vert$
> **Low accuracy** $\Rightarrow$ Large $\vert E_t \vert$

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Precision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision** is the degree of **reproducibility** or **consistency** of a measurement or computational result. It refers to how closely multiple independent measurements of the same quantity agree with one another, *independent* of whether they are close to the true value.

Precision depends on:
- The *fineness* of the measuring scale
- The *repeatability* of the method
- The number of **significant figures** retained

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Dartboard

Imagine a player throwing darts at a target:

| Scenario | Description | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Darts clustered at the bullseye | Clustered **and** at the true center | **High Accuracy + High Precision** |
| Darts clustered but away from center | Tightly grouped, but off-target | **High Precision, Low Accuracy** (Systematic bias) |
| Darts scattered around the bullseye | Spread out, but centered on average | **High Accuracy, Low Precision** (Random error) |
| Darts scattered everywhere | Spread out and off-target | **Low Accuracy + Low Precision** |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for Chemical Engineers:** A pressure transducer repeatedly reading **5.02, 5.01, 5.02, 5.01 bar** when the true pressure is **5.00 bar** is **highly precise** but **slightly inaccurate** (bias). The deviation is constant → **systematic error**.

---

## 1.4 Engineering Metrics Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key KTU Board Constants & Standard Metrics:
> - **Machine Epsilon** ($\epsilon_m$) — the smallest number representable such that $1 + \epsilon_m > 1$ on a digital computer. For IEEE 754 double precision, $\epsilon_m \approx \mathbf{2.22 \times 10^{-16}}$.
> - **Significant Figures (sig figs)** — digits that carry meaningful information about a measurement's precision.
> - **Tolerance** ($\epsilon_s$) — a pre-specified stopping criterion used to terminate iterative numerical methods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Error in Numerical Methods

Numerical errors in Process Engineering computations arise from three principal sources:

### 2.1.1 Gross Errors (Blunders)
- **Cause:** Human mistakes — wrong data entry, faulty programming logic, misreading instruments.
- **Mitigation:** Code review, sanity checks, redundant computation.

### 2.1.2 Systematic Errors (Bias)
- **Cause:** Inherent flaws in instrument calibration, mathematical model assumptions, or numerical technique.
- **Characteristics:** Reproducible, consistent in sign and magnitude.
- **Mitigation:** Re-calibration, refined modeling, choosing a higher-order method.

### 2.1.3 Round-off Errors
- **Cause:** Finite word length of digital computers (every real number is stored with limited digits, e.g., 64-bit IEEE 754).
- **Example:** Storing $\pi = 3.14159265...$ as a finite binary fraction introduces an irreducible discrepancy.

### 2.1.4 Truncation Errors
- **Cause:** Replacing an infinite mathematical process (e.g., Taylor series) with a finite approximation.
- **Example:** Using only the first $n$ terms of a Taylor series to approximate $\sin(x)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Accuracy:** A numerical method is said to be of order $O(h^p)$ if the truncation error is proportional to $h^p$, where $h$ is the step size and $p$ is the order.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True Error | $E_t = x_t - x_a$ | Deviation from the exact value |
| 2 | Absolute True Error | $\vert E_t \vert = \vert x_t - x_a \vert$ | Magnitude of deviation |
| 3 | True Relative Error | $\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{x_t - x_a}{x_t}$ | Dimensionless fractional error |
| 4 | True Percentage Relative Error | $\varepsilon_t\,\% = \dfrac{x_t - x_a}{x_t} \times 100\%$ | Error expressed as a percent |
| 5 | Approximate Error | $E_a = x_a^{\text{current}} - x_a^{\text{previous}}$ | Difference between successive iterates |
| 6 | Approximate Percentage Relative Error | $\varepsilon_a\,\% = \left\vert \dfrac{x_a^{\text{current}} - x_a^{\text{previous}}}{x_a^{\text{current}}} \right\vert \times 100\%$ | Used as **stopping criterion** |
| 7 | Stopping Criterion | $\vert \varepsilon_a \vert < \varepsilon_s$ | Iteration terminates when error falls below tolerance $\varepsilon_s$ |
| 8 | Taylor Remainder (Truncation) | $R_n = \dfrac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!} \, h^{n+1}$ | Error from truncating Taylor series at $n$-th term |
| 9 | Order Notation | $E = O(h^p)$ | Error scales as $h^p$ |
| 10 | Forward/Backward Error Bound | $\vert \varepsilon_{\text{round}} \vert \le \dfrac{1}{2}\beta^{1-t}$ | Round-off of a $t$-digit base-$\beta$ number |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every formula above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes **absolute value**. Use $\vert x \vert$ or `\vert x \vert` — never the unescaped pipe `|x|` — to preserve markdown table integrity in KTU digital submissions.

---

## 2.3 Rules for Significant Figures

| Rule | Example | Significant Figures |
|---|---|---|
| All non-zero digits are significant | **4.5301** g | 5 sig figs |
| Zeros between non-zero digits are significant | **50.02** mL | 4 sig figs |
| Leading zeros are *not* significant | **0.0042** mol | 2 sig figs |
| Trailing zeros with a decimal are significant | **12.300** K | 5 sig figs |
| Trailing zeros without a decimal are ambiguous | **1500** (ambiguous) | Use scientific notation: $1.500 \times 10^3$ → 4 sig figs |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Process Control:** Sensor accuracy determines the quality of feedback control in a chemical plant. A $\pm 0.5\%$ error in a flow transmitter translates directly into feedstock imbalance.
- **Reactor Design:** Numerical solution of stiff ODEs (e.g., CSTR dynamics) requires error bounds to guarantee that the *integrated* species concentrations obey mass balance.
- **Heat Exchanger Sizing:** Round-off in the iterative log-mean temperature difference (LMTD) calculation can produce sub-optimal area estimates, inflating capital cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Approximate Percentage Error as a Stopping Criterion

### Step 1: Define the Current and Previous Approximations

In any iterative numerical method (Newton–Raphson, bisection, fixed-point iteration), we generate a sequence of approximations:
$$x_0,\ x_1,\ x_2,\ \ldots,\ x_n$$

We wish to determine *when* to terminate the iteration without knowing the true value $x_t$.

### Step 2: Define the Approximate Error

The natural surrogate for $E_t$ is the difference between the most recent iterate and the previous iterate:
$$E_a = x_n - x_{n-1}$$

### Step 3: Normalize by the Current Value

To obtain a *relative* (dimensionless) measure, divide by the current approximation:
$$\varepsilon_a = \dfrac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{x_n}$$

### Step 4: Take the Absolute Value

Errors in magnitude (not sign) govern convergence assessment:
$$\vert \varepsilon_a \vert = \left\vert \dfrac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{x_n} \right\vert$$

### Step 5: Convert to a Percentage

Multiply by $100\%$ to express as a percentage (industry-friendly form):
$$\varepsilon_a \,\% = \left\vert \dfrac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{x_n} \right\vert \times 100\%$$

### Step 6: Apply the Stopping Rule

The iteration is terminated when the condition below is first satisfied:
$$\varepsilon_a \,\% < \varepsilon_s$$
where $\varepsilon_s$ is the **pre-specified tolerance** (e.g., $0.001\%$, $10^{-6}$, etc., set by the engineer).

> [!NOTE]
> The approximate error $\varepsilon_a$ approaches the true relative error $\varepsilon_t$ *as* the iterates converge. This is justified by the contraction-mapping principle, which guarantees the sequence forms a Cauchy sequence under standard conditions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** The true value of a flow rate is $Q_t = 2.5000\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. A computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation produces $Q_a = 2.4863\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate the true error, true relative error, and true percentage relative error.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — True Error:**
$$E_t = x_t - x_a = 2.5000 - 2.4863 = 0.0137\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Absolute True Error:**
$$\vert E_t \vert = \vert 0.0137 \vert = 0.0137\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — True Relative Error:**
$$\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{E_t}{x_t} = \dfrac{0.0137}{2.5000} = 0.00548$$

**Step 4 — True Percentage Relative Error:**
$$\varepsilon_t \,\% = 0.00548 \times 100\% = 0.548\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for the Process Engineer:** The CFD model under-predicts the flow rate by approximately **0.55%**. Whether this is acceptable depends on the design margin — typically, a relative error below **1%** is acceptable for steady-state design, while **dynamic** simulation requires **0.1%** or better.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (High-Yield for Board)

| Property | Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| **What it measures** | Closeness to the true value | Closeness of repeated measurements to each other |
| **Affected by** | Systematic errors (bias) | Random errors (scatter) |
| **Quantified by** | $\varepsilon_t$ or $\varepsilon_t\,\%$ | Variance, standard deviation, number of sig figs |
| **Improved by** | Re-calibration, refined model | Better instrumentation, finer scale |
| **Independent of true value?** | No — requires $x_t$ | Yes — purely statistical |
| **Typical process engineering statement** | "Sensor reads within $\pm 0.2\%$ of standard" | "Sensor repeatability is $\pm 0.05\%$ of full scale" |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Significant Figures

> **Problem:** Round $3.14159265$ to (a) 4 significant figures and (b) 2 significant figures. Compute the absolute and relative round-off errors for each.

### Part (a): Rounding to 4 significant figures

$$x_a = 3.142 \quad (\text{the 5th digit is 9, so round up})$$

$$\vert E_t \vert = \vert 3.14159265 - 3.142 \vert = 0.00040735$$

$$\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{0.00040735}{3.14159265} = 1.297 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01297\%$$

### Part (b): Rounding to 2 significant figures

$$x_a = 3.1$$

$$\vert E_t \vert = \vert 3.14159265 - 3.1 \vert = 0.04159265$$

$$\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{0.04159265}{3.14159265} = 1.324 \times 10^{-2} = 1.324\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Rounding to 2 significant figures introduces an error **102 times larger** than rounding to 4. The trade-off between round-off and truncation errors is central to the design of robust numerical algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Numerical Errors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Numerical Errors in Computation] --> B[Gross Errors]
    A --> C[Systematic Errors]
    A --> D[Round-off Errors]
    A --> E[Truncation Errors]
    B --> B1[Human mistakes, data entry]
    C --> C1[Instrument bias]
    C --> C2[Model assumptions]
    D --> D1[Finite word length]
    D --> D2[IEEE 754 representation]
    E --> E1[Taylor series remainder]
    E --> E2[Discretization of continuous models]
    C1 --> F1[Addressed via calibration]
    C2 --> F2[Addressed via refined modeling]
    D1 --> G1[Addressed via higher precision types]
    E1 --> G2[Addressed via smaller step size or higher order]
```

## 4.2 The Accuracy–Precision Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Problem Definition] --> Q{Is the True Value Known?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Compute True Error E_t]
    Q -- No --> S[Use Approximate Error E_a]
    R --> T[Quantify Accuracy]
    S --> U[Quantify Precision via Repeatability]
    T --> V{Acceptable per Tolerance?}
    U --> V
    V -- Yes --> W[Accept Result]
    V -- No --> X[Refine Method or Instrument]
    X --> Q
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Error Propagation in a Numerical Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Input Data: Physical Measurements] --> P1[Pre-processing and Unit Conversion]
    P1 --> N1[Numerical Method Execution]
    N1 --> O1[Output: Approximate Solution]
    I1 -.-> E1[Instrument Errors]
    P1 -.-> E2[Round-off Errors]
    N1 -.-> E3[Truncation and Method Errors]
    O1 --> V1[Validation: Compare with Analytical or Experimental Benchmarks]
    V1 -- Acceptable --> A1[Report Result with Uncertainty Band]
    V1 -- Not Acceptable --> R1[Refine Algorithm, Reduce Step Size, Increase Precision]
    R1 --> N1
```

## 4.4 Iterative Convergence — Error Decay Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Iteration k equals 0: Initial Guess x0] --> S1[Iteration 1: x1]
    S1 --> S2[Iteration 2: x2]
    S2 --> S3[Iteration n: xn]
    S3 --> CK{Approx Percent Error less than Tolerance?}
    CK -- No --> S4[Increment k by 1]
    S4 --> S1
    CK -- Yes --> DONE[Converged Solution]
    S0 -.-> E0[Error E0]
    S1 -.-> E1[Error E1]
    S2 -.-> E2[Error E2]
    S3 -.-> E3[Error En]
    E0 --> ED[Error Decay Curve En vs k]
    E1 --> ED
    E2 --> ED
    E3 --> ED
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between accuracy and precision. Which of the two is affected by systematic errors and which by random errors? Justify with a chemical engineering example.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy** is the closeness of a measured or computed value to the *true* value; it is a measure of *correctness* and is degraded primarily by **systematic errors** (e.g., a poorly calibrated thermocouple that consistently reads 2 K high).
- **Precision** is the closeness of repeated measurements to each other; it is a measure of *reproducibility* and is degraded primarily by **random errors** (e.g., electronic noise causing a flow meter to fluctuate within $\pm 0.05$ L/min across repeated readings).
- A Pt-100 RTD repeatedly indicating $301.2,\ 301.3,\ 301.2$ K when the true reactor temperature is $301.0$ K is *highly precise* but *slightly inaccurate* due to a fixed calibration offset.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define true error, relative true error, and percentage relative true error. State the stopping criterion used in iterative numerical methods.**

**Model Answer:**

- **True error:** $E_t = x_t - x_a$
- **Relative true error:** $\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{x_t - x_a}{x_t}$
- **Percentage relative true error:** $\varepsilon_t\,\% = \dfrac{x_t - x_a}{x_t} \times 100\%$
- **Stopping criterion:** The iteration is terminated when $\vert \varepsilon_a \vert < \varepsilon_s$, where $\varepsilon_a$ is the approximate percentage relative error between successive iterates and $\varepsilon_s$ is the pre-specified tolerance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** The true value of a catalyst conversion in a packed-bed reactor is $X_t = 0.8765$. A numerical model based on finite-element discretization predicts $X_a = 0.8718$. Calculate:
  (i) the absolute true error, (ii) the true relative error, and (iii) the true percentage relative error. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** An iterative method yields the following successive approximations for the bubble-point temperature of a binary mixture: $T_0 = 350.0$ K, $T_1 = 348.5$ K, $T_2 = 347.9$ K, $T_3 = 347.7$ K. Using a tolerance of $\varepsilon_s = 0.05\%$, determine whether the iteration has converged at $T_3$. If not, perform one more iteration conceptually. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**(i) Absolute True Error:**
$$\vert E_t \vert = \vert x_t - x_a \vert = \vert 0.8765 - 0.8718 \vert = 0.0047$$

**(ii) True Relative Error:**
$$\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{x_t - x_a}{x_t} = \dfrac{0.0047}{0.8765} = 5.363 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(iii) True Percentage Relative Error:**
$$\varepsilon_t\,\% = 5.363 \times 10^{-3} \times 100\% = 0.5363\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The numerical model under-predicts conversion by $\approx 0.54\%$, which is generally within acceptable limits for steady-state reactor design.

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1:** Compute $\varepsilon_a$ for $T_0 \to T_1$:
$$\varepsilon_a^{(1)} = \left\vert \dfrac{348.5 - 350.0}{348.5} \right\vert \times 100\% = \left\vert \dfrac{-1.5}{348.5} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.4304\%$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\varepsilon_a$ for $T_1 \to T_2$:
$$\varepsilon_a^{(2)} = \left\vert \dfrac{347.9 - 348.5}{347.9} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.1722\%$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\varepsilon_a$ for $T_2 \to T_3$:
$$\varepsilon_a^{(3)} = \left\vert \dfrac{347.7 - 347.9}{347.7} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.0575\%$$

**Step 4:** Compare with tolerance:
$$\varepsilon_a^{(3)} = 0.0575\% > \varepsilon_s = 0.05\%$$

> **Verdict:** The iteration has **not** converged at $T_3$. One more iteration is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Sign of error:** Use absolute value in the stopping criterion. Forgetting $\vert \cdot \vert$ costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Comparison with $\varepsilon_s$:** Always state the inequality explicitly. A bare numerical answer without comparison loses **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Units:** Although the temperature cancels in the relative ratio, the result is dimensionless — state it as a *percentage* explicitly.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic (or flowchart), classify the various sources of numerical error. Discuss in detail the difference between round-off and truncation errors. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A temperature sensor in a CSTR provides the following five readings during a steady-state run: $348.2,\ 348.4,\ 348.3,\ 348.2,\ 348.3$ K. The true reactor temperature, independently measured by a calibrated reference probe, is $348.50$ K. Calculate:
  (i) the mean of the sensor readings, (ii) the standard deviation (as a measure of precision), and (iii) the bias and the percentage inaccuracy of the sensor. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Classification of Numerical Errors** (refer to Section 4.1 flowchart):

1. **Gross Errors** — Human mistakes, data entry, faulty logic.
2. **Systematic Errors** — Instrument bias, model assumptions.
3. **Round-off Errors** — Originate from the *finite word length* of computer arithmetic. Every real number is approximated by a finite binary fraction.
   - *Example:* Storing $\pi$ as $3.14159265358979$ in 64-bit IEEE 754 (the actual binary representation is slightly different).
4. **Truncation Errors** — Originate from *terminating* a mathematically infinite process prematurely.
   - *Example:* Using only the first 3 terms of the Taylor series for $e^x = 1 + x + \dfrac{x^2}{2!} + \dfrac{x^3}{3!} + \ldots$

**Key Distinction:**

| Aspect | Round-off Error | Truncation Error |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Finite representation of numbers | Termination of infinite series/process |
| **Magnitude** | Usually very small per operation | Can be large for coarse approximations |
| **Controlling parameter** | Word length (e.g., 32-bit vs 64-bit) | Step size $h$ and order $p$ |
| **Reduction strategy** | Use higher precision data types | Use smaller $h$ or higher-order method |
| **Effect of reduction** | Diminishing returns (costly) | Predictable scaling: $O(h^p)$ |

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Mean (Average Reading):**
$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{348.2 + 348.4 + 348.3 + 348.2 + 348.3}{5} = \dfrac{1741.4}{5} = 348.28\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation (Measure of Precision):**

First, compute deviations from the mean:
$$d_i = T_i - \bar{T}$$
$$d_1 = -0.08,\ d_2 = +0.12,\ d_3 = +0.02,\ d_4 = -0.08,\ d_5 = +0.02$$

Now compute the sample standard deviation:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_i - \bar{T})^2}{n - 1}}$$

$$\sum d_i^2 = 0.0064 + 0.0144 + 0.0004 + 0.0064 + 0.0004 = 0.0280$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0280}{4}} = \sqrt{0.0070} = 0.0837\ \text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Bias (Systematic Error):**
$$\text{Bias} = \bar{T} - T_{\text{true}} = 348.28 - 348.50 = -0.22\ \text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Inaccuracy:**
$$\varepsilon\% = \left\vert \dfrac{\bar{T} - T_{\text{true}}}{T_{\text{true}}} \right\vert \times 100\% = \left\vert \dfrac{-0.22}{348.50} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.0631\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The sensor is **moderately precise** (small standard deviation of $0.084$ K) but **slightly inaccurate** (bias of $-0.22$ K, $\approx 0.06\%$ low), suggesting a systematic calibration offset rather than random noise.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Use $n-1$, not $n$:** For *sample* standard deviation, Bessel's correction ($n-1$) is required. Using $n$ gives a *biased* estimator and costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Distinguish precision from accuracy:** The standard deviation measures *precision*; bias measures *accuracy*. Confusing them loses **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Show intermediate sums:** The full computation of $\sum d_i^2 = 0.0280$ must be shown explicitly — examiners award partial credit for correct intermediate work.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Accuracy** $\rightarrow$ closeness to the **true value**; affected by **systematic** errors (bias).
- **Precision** $\rightarrow$ closeness of **repeated measurements** to each other; affected by **random** errors (scatter).
- **True error:** $E_t = x_t - x_a$
- **Absolute true error:** $\vert E_t \vert = \vert x_t - x_a \vert$
- **True relative error:** $\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{x_t - x_a}{x_t}$
- **True percentage relative error:** $\varepsilon_t\,\% = \varepsilon_t \times 100\%$
- **Approximate percentage relative error:** $\varepsilon_a\,\% = \left\vert \dfrac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{x_n} \right\vert \times 100\%$
- **Stopping criterion:** $\vert \varepsilon_a \vert < \varepsilon_s$ (always use *absolute value*).
- **Sources of error:** Gross (human), Systematic (bias), Round-off (word length), Truncation (series termination).
- **Round-off** is controlled by **word length**; **Truncation** is controlled by **step size $h$** and **order $p$** (scaling $\sim h^p$).
- **Machine epsilon** for IEEE 754 double precision $\approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$.
- **Significant figure rules:** Non-zero digits and zeros *between* them are significant; leading zeros are not; trailing zeros with a decimal point are significant.
- **Sample standard deviation** uses $n-1$ in the denominator (Bessel's correction).
- **Tolerance $\varepsilon_s$** is a *design choice* — typical values in KTU problems: $10^{-3}\%$, $10^{-6}$, or $0.05\%$.
- Always **state units** and the **direction of error** (over-prediction vs under-prediction) in your final conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Errors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Errors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Numerical Methods for Process Engineers**, an **error** is defined as the *discrepancy or deviation between the true (exact) value of a mathematical/physical quantity and the approximate value obtained through numerical computation, measurement, or algorithmic approximation*. Since digital computers have a finite word length and mathematical models are often simplified, the result produced by any numerical procedure is virtually never exactly equal to the true mathematical answer.

Mathematically, the error $E$ associated with an approximation $\tilde{x}$ to the exact value $x$ is formally expressed as:

$$
E = x - \tilde{x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Definition**
> The 2024 scheme defines errors under Module 1 as: *"The study of the origin, classification, and quantification of errors arising during mathematical modeling and numerical computation, including inherent errors, truncation errors, and round-off errors, along with the concepts of absolute, relative, and percentage error, significant digits, accuracy, and precision."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Highlight**
> In all KTU board examinations for PECHT526, the term **error** is *never* treated as a single concept. It is always a three-tier classification: **(i) Inherent Errors, (ii) Truncation Errors, (iii) Round-off Errors**. Memorizing this taxonomy perfectly guarantees at least 3–5 marks in any Part A question on this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chemical engineer** tasked with measuring the temperature inside a reactor vessel using a mercury-in-glass thermometer.

- The thermometer itself has a finite resolution (it can only read to the nearest 0.5 °C). Even if the actual temperature is **254.37 °C**, your instrument can only display **254.5 °C** (or **254.0 °C**). This deviation is caused by the **finite precision of the measuring instrument**, analogous to a **Round-off Error** in numerical computation.
- Suppose the actual process temperature follows a complex exponential decay function $T(t) = 300 e^{-0.05t} + 25$, but you approximate it using only the first two terms of a Taylor series: $T(t) \approx 300(1 - 0.05t) + 25$. The terms you discarded introduce a **Truncation Error**.
- Finally, the calibration chart you used to read the thermometer was slightly misaligned at the factory — the **inherent error** baked into the data you started with.

In numerical computing, we always operate with a *triple-filter system*: the problem statement itself contains noise (**inherent**), the mathematical procedure uses infinite series which we cut short (**truncation**), and the computer memory stores numbers in only a fixed number of bits (**round-off**). The art of Numerical Methods is to *minimize the combined effect of all three* and to *estimate the magnitude of the residual error*.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Memory Trick for Students**
> Think **I-T-R** like the three gears of a car: **I**nput data → **T**runcation by the algorithm → **R**ound-off by the computer. Drop any one gear, and the engine of computation stalls.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Throughout the Course

The following metrics are used universally in this course to *quantify* the magnitude of any error:

- **Absolute Error:** $E_a = \vert x - \tilde{x} \vert$ — measured in the same units as the quantity.
- **Relative Error:** $E_r = \dfrac{\vert x - \tilde{x} \vert}{\vert x \vert}$ — a *dimensionless* ratio.
- **Percentage Error:** $E_p = E_r \times 100\,\%$ — relative error expressed as a percentage.
- **Significant Digits (Figures):** The digits in a number that carry meaningful information about its precision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Numerical Error — A Structured Classification

### 2.1.1 Inherent Errors (Data / Input Errors)
These are errors that *enter* the computation *before* any numerical algorithm is invoked. They originate from:
- Physical measurement limitations (instrument precision, sensor noise).
- Human error in tabulating empirical data.
- Simplifying assumptions made while formulating the mathematical model of the process.
- Incomplete knowledge of physical constants.

> **Engineering Utility:** In process engineering, even the most sophisticated numerical solver will produce a useless answer if the input flow-rate, temperature, or concentration data carry a 10 % inherent error. The famous aphorism *"garbage in, garbage out"* (GIGO) applies directly here.

### 2.1.2 Truncation Errors (Discretization / Formulation Errors)
These arise when an infinite mathematical process is replaced by a finite one. The most common sources are:
- Replacing an infinite Taylor series by a finite number of terms.
- Replacing an integral by a finite numerical quadrature sum.
- Replacing a continuous differential equation by a finite-difference approximation.

The *truncation error* is often expressed as the leading term that was discarded. For example, truncating the Taylor series of a function $f(x)$ about $x_0$ after the $n$-th derivative term leaves an error proportional to:

$$
E_{\text{trunc}} \propto \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x - x_0)^{n+1}
$$

### 2.1.3 Round-off Errors
These are caused by the *finite word length* of digital computers. Since a computer can store only a fixed number of significant digits (e.g., 16 digits in double-precision IEEE 754), every arithmetic operation may drop the least significant digits.

> **Engineering Utility:** Round-off errors accumulate in long iterative loops (e.g., in solving 10,000 stiff ODEs in a reactor network). Numerical analysts must design algorithms that are *numerically stable*, meaning that small round-offs at each step do not grow exponentially.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table compiles every formula you will need for Module 1 in a KTU exam hall. Every formula is mathematically verifiable and aligns with the PECHT526 syllabus.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | True Error | $E = x - \tilde{x}$ | Signed deviation; same units as $x$ |
| 2 | Absolute Error | $E_a = \vert x - \tilde{x} \vert$ | Magnitude only; same units as $x$ |
| 3 | Relative Error | $E_r = \dfrac{\vert x - \tilde{x} \vert}{\vert x \vert}$ | Dimensionless ratio; $0 \le E_r$ |
| 4 | Percentage Error | $E_p = E_r \times 100\,\%$ | Dimensionless; expressed in % |
| 5 | Approximate Error | $\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_{\text{new}} - x_{\text{old}}}{x_{\text{new}}} \right\vert \times 100\,\%$ | Used in iterative methods |
| 6 | Taylor Series Remainder (Truncation) | $R_n = \dfrac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x - x_0)^{n+1}$ | Bound on truncation error |
| 7 | Floating-Point Round-off | $\text{fl}(x) = x(1 + \delta), \quad \vert\delta\vert \le u$ | $u$ = machine epsilon / 2 |
| 8 | Error Propagation (Addition/Subtraction) | $E_r(u \pm v) \le \dfrac{\vert E_u \vert + \vert E_v \vert}{\vert u \pm v \vert}$ | Worst-case bound |
| 9 | Error Propagation (Multiplication/Division) | $E_r(u \cdot v) \le E_r(u) + E_r(v)$ | Sum of relative errors |
| 10 | Error Propagation (Powers) | $E_r(u^n) \le n \cdot E_r(u)$ | Useful in process scaling laws |
| 11 | Condition Number | $\kappa = \left\vert \dfrac{x \cdot f'(x)}{f(x)} \right\vert$ | Measures problem sensitivity |
| 12 | Significant Digits Rule | At least $n$ significant digits $\iff$ $E_p \le 0.5 \times 10^{(2-n)}\,\%$ | Verification criterion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Tip — Avoid the Pipe Symbol Trap**
> In your answer sheets, always write the absolute value of a quantity as $\vert x \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command, **never** with the keyboard pipe `|`. KTU digital valuation systems occasionally parse `|` as a table delimiter and mis-render the entire formula line.

## 2.3 Accuracy vs Precision — The Engineering Distinction

These two terms are frequently confused by students, but KTU examiners love to set 3-mark questions distinguishing them.

- **Accuracy:** How close a measured/calculated value is to the **true value**. Governed mainly by *systematic* errors (inherent + truncation).
- **Precision:** How close repeated measurements/calculations are to **each other**, irrespective of the true value. Governed mainly by *random* errors (round-off, measurement noise).

> A precise instrument is *not* necessarily accurate. A biased but high-precision pressure gauge can read **102.3 kPa, 102.3 kPa, 102.3 kPa** consistently — and yet all three readings may be wrong by 5 kPa if the gauge is miscalibrated.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In chemical process engineering specifically, the concepts of error analysis underpin:

- **Reactor design safety margins:** Truncation errors in integrating stiff ODEs of reactor kinetics must be quantified to ensure runaway-reaction predictions are not under-conservative.
- **Distillation column convergence:** Round-off errors in iterative bubble-point calculations can cause divergence if the algorithm is not numerically stable.
- **CFD simulations:** Inherent errors from turbulence-model assumptions (k-ε vs k-ω) routinely exceed 15 %. Engineers use relative-error bounds to decide whether the simulation is trustworthy.
- **Thermodynamic property estimation:** Antoine-equation constants carry inherent errors of 1–5 %; propagation through a process model must be tracked.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Truncation-Error Term via Taylor's Theorem

The most critical derivation for Module 1 of PECHT526 is the formal expression for the truncation error that arises when we replace a smooth function $f(x)$ by its Taylor polynomial of degree $n$.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Taylor Series

By definition, a function $f(x)$ that is infinitely differentiable at a point $x_0$ can be expanded as:

$$
f(x) = f(x_0) + f'(x_0)(x - x_0) + \frac{f''(x_0)}{2!}(x - x_0)^2 + \frac{f'''(x_0)}{3!}(x - x_0)^3 + \dots
$$

$$
f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{f^{(k)}(x_0)}{k!}(x - x_0)^k
$$

### 3.1.2 Truncation After $n$ Terms

When we keep only the first $(n+1)$ terms, i.e., from $k=0$ up to $k=n$, we define the Taylor polynomial of degree $n$:

$$
P_n(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{f^{(k)}(x_0)}{k!}(x - x_0)^k
$$

### 3.1.3 The Remainder Term (Truncation Error)

The error introduced by truncating the series is the remainder $R_n(x) = f(x) - P_n(x)$. By applying the **Mean Value Theorem for divided differences**, it can be shown rigorously that there exists a point $\xi$ between $x_0$ and $x$ such that:

$$
R_n(x) = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x - x_0)^{n+1}
$$

This single formula is the cornerstone of *every* finite-difference, finite-element, and quadrature formula the student will encounter in subsequent modules.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Computing the True Error of an Approximation

> **Problem:** The true value of a reaction rate constant is $k_{\text{true}} = 2.45678 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$. A numerical estimation gives $\tilde{k} = 2.45 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$. Compute (a) the absolute error, (b) the relative error, and (c) the percentage error.

### Solution

**(a) Absolute Error:**

$$
E_a = \vert k_{\text{true}} - \tilde{k} \vert = \vert 2.45678 \times 10^{-3} - 2.45000 \times 10^{-3} \vert
$$

$$
E_a = \vert 0.00678 \times 10^{-3} \vert = 6.78 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**(b) Relative Error:**

$$
E_r = \frac{E_a}{\vert k_{\text{true}} \vert} = \frac{6.78 \times 10^{-6}}{2.45678 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
E_r = 2.7596 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**(c) Percentage Error:**

$$
E_p = E_r \times 100\,\% = 0.27596\,\%
$$

> The numerical estimation is accurate to **3 significant figures** (since $E_p \le 0.5\,\%$).

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Error Propagation in a Process Formula

> **Problem:** The viscosity of a fluid in a heat exchanger is computed using $\mu = \rho \cdot v \cdot d$, where density $\rho = 1000 \pm 2\,\text{kg/m}^3$, velocity $v = 3.0 \pm 0.1\,\text{m/s}$, and characteristic length $d = 0.05 \pm 0.001\,\text{m}$. Estimate the maximum relative error in $\mu$.

### Solution

Since the formula is a product of three quantities, the relative-error propagation rule for multiplication is:

$$
E_r(\mu) \le E_r(\rho) + E_r(v) + E_r(d)
$$

Step 1 — Relative error in $\rho$:

$$
E_r(\rho) = \frac{2}{1000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Step 2 — Relative error in $v$:

$$
E_r(v) = \frac{0.1}{3.0} = 3.333 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Step 3 — Relative error in $d$:

$$
E_r(d) = \frac{0.001}{0.05} = 2.0 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Step 4 — Summing the relative errors:

$$
E_r(\mu) \le 2.0 \times 10^{-3} + 3.333 \times 10^{-2} + 2.0 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
E_r(\mu) \le 5.533 \times 10^{-2} = 5.533\,\%
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the largest contributor to the propagated error is the velocity $v$, with 3.33 % relative error, even though the absolute error in $v$ is the smallest in magnitude. This demonstrates the power of *relative* error analysis in process engineering — a 0.1 m/s uncertainty in a 3.0 m/s flow is far more consequential than a 2 kg/m³ uncertainty in a 1000 kg/m³ liquid density.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Truncation Error of a Finite-Difference Approximation

> **Problem:** The forward-difference approximation of the first derivative of $f(x)$ at $x_0$ is given by:
>
> $$f'(x_0) \approx \frac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h}$$
>
> Derive an expression for the truncation error in terms of $h$ and the second derivative of $f$ at $x_0$.

### Solution

Step 1 — Expand $f(x_0 + h)$ in a Taylor series about $x_0$:

$$
f(x_0 + h) = f(x_0) + f'(x_0) h + \frac{f''(x_0)}{2!}h^2 + \frac{f'''(x_0)}{3!}h^3 + \dots
$$

Step 2 — Substitute into the forward-difference formula:

$$
\frac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h} = \frac{f(x_0) + f'(x_0)h + \frac{f''(x_0)}{2}h^2 + \dots - f(x_0)}{h}
$$

Step 3 — Simplify:

$$
\frac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h} = f'(x_0) + \frac{f''(x_0)}{2}h + \frac{f'''(x_0)}{6}h^2 + \dots
$$

Step 4 — Subtract the true derivative $f'(x_0)$ to isolate the truncation error:

$$
E_{\text{trunc}} = \frac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h} - f'(x_0) = \frac{f''(x_0)}{2}h + O(h^2)
$$

Step 5 — Conclusion:

$$
E_{\text{trunc}} = \frac{f''(x_0)}{2}\,h + O(h^2)
$$

> The leading truncation error is **proportional to $h$** — i.e., the forward-difference formula is *first-order accurate*. This single derivation is the foundation of every finite-difference scheme in Module 2 and beyond.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Numerical Errors

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete KTU 2024 scheme classification of errors in numerical computation, showing how each error type originates and propagates through a typical process-engineering computation pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["NUMERICAL ERRORS IN PROCESS COMPUTATION"] --> B["INHERENT ERRORS"]
    A --> C["TRUNCATION ERRORS"]
    A --> D["ROUND-OFF ERRORS"]

    B --> B1["Measurement Noise"]
    B --> B2["Instrument Resolution"]
    B --> B3["Model Simplification"]
    B --> B4["Physical Constant Uncertainty"]

    C --> C1["Taylor Series Truncation"]
    C --> C2["Numerical Integration Cut-off"]
    C --> C3["Finite-Difference Approximation"]
    C --> C4["Iterative Convergence Cut-off"]

    D --> D1["Floating-Point Representation"]
    D --> D2["Arithmetic Operation Loss"]
    D --> D3["Accumulation in Long Loops"]
    D --> D4["Subtractive Cancellation"]

    B1 --> E["TOTAL COMPUTATIONAL ERROR"]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F["QUANTIFIED VIA"]
    F --> F1["Absolute Error"]
    F --> F2["Relative Error"]
    F --> F3["Percentage Error"]
    F --> F4["Significant Digits"]

    E --> G["MITIGATION STRATEGIES"]
    G --> G1["Better Sensors and Calibration"]
    G --> G2["Higher-Order Methods"]
    G --> G3["Smaller Step Sizes"]
    G --> G4["Double Precision Arithmetic"]
    G --> G5["Numerically Stable Algorithms"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Error Flow in a Numerical Solver

The following block diagram illustrates how errors flow through a typical numerical solver used in chemical process engineering — for example, when simulating a CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) over time.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["INPUT DATA: Physical Constants, Initial Conditions, Boundary Conditions"] --> inErr["INHERENT ERROR: Sensor Noise, Model Assumptions"]
    inErr --> model["MATHEMATICAL MODEL: ODE, PDE, or Algebraic System"]
    model --> algo["NUMERICAL ALGORITHM: Euler, RK4, Newton-Raphson, etc."]
    algo --> trErr["TRUNCATION ERROR: Finite Step, Finite Series"]
    trErr --> arith["ARITHMETIC UNIT: Floating-Point Operations"]
    arith --> roErr["ROUND-OFF ERROR: Finite Word Length"]
    roErr --> out["OUTPUT: Approximate Solution"]
    out --> compare{"VERIFICATION: Compare with True or Refined Solution"}
    compare -->|"Discrepancy Exceeds Tolerance"| refine["REFINE: Reduce Step Size or Increase Precision"]
    compare -->|"Discrepancy Within Tolerance"| accept["ACCEPT RESULT"]
    refine --> algo
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram**
> The arrows indicate the *direction of data flow*, not the magnitude of error. Notice the **feedback loop** from the verification block back to the algorithm — this is precisely how adaptive step-size control works in modern ODE solvers like `ode45` (industry standard) used in chemical-reactor simulations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
