Newton's method requires $f'(x_n)$, which is often expensive to evaluate. The Secant method replaces the derivative with the **first-order backward finite-difference approximation**:

$$f'(x_n) \approx \dfrac{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}{x_n - x_{n-1}}$$

Substituting this approximation into the Newton-Raphson update:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{\dfrac{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}{x_n - x_{n-1}}}$$

After algebraic simplification (multiplying numerator and denominator by $(x_n - x_{n-1})$):

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n) (x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$

This is the **Secant Method iteration formula**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Fixed-Point Convergence Theorem

Given $x = g(x)$ with true root $\alpha$, the error evolution is:

$$e_{n+1} = x_{n+1} - \alpha = g(x_n) - g(\alpha)$$

Applying the **Mean Value Theorem** (with some $\xi_n$ between $x_n$ and $\alpha$):

$$e_{n+1} = g'(\xi_n) (x_n - \alpha) = g'(\xi_n) \, e_n$$

Taking absolute values and limits:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \vert e_{n+1} \vert = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert \lim_{n \to \infty} \vert e_n \vert$$

Therefore, **convergence requires** $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$ locally.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example: Newton-Raphson

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Problem (Model KTU Question):** Find a real root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ using Newton-Raphson, starting with $x_0 = 2$, up to 4 iterations.

**Solution Setup:**

$$f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5, \quad f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$$

**Iteration 1 ($n = 0$):**

$$f(2) = 2^3 - 2(2) - 5 = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1$$

$$f'(2) = 3(2)^2 - 2 = 12 - 2 = 10$$

$$x_1 = 2 - \dfrac{-1}{10} = 2 + 0.1 = 2.1$$

**Iteration 2 ($n = 1$):**

$$f(2.1) = (2.1)^3 - 2(2.1) - 5 = 9.261 - 4.2 - 5 = 0.061$$

$$f'(2.1) = 3(2.1)^2 - 2 = 3(4.41) - 2 = 13.23 - 2 = 11.23$$

$$x_2 = 2.1 - \dfrac{0.061}{11.23} = 2.1 - 0.00543 = 2.09457$$

**Iteration 3 ($n = 2$):**

$$f(2.09457) = (2.09457)^3 - 2(2.09457) - 5 = 9.18890 - 4.18914 - 5 = -0.00024$$

$$f'(2.09457) = 3(2.09457)^2 - 2 = 3(4.38730) - 2 = 13.16190 - 2 = 11.16190$$

$$x_3 = 2.09457 - \dfrac{-0.00024}{11.16190} = 2.09457 + 0.0000215 \approx 2.09459$$

**Iteration 4 ($n = 3$):**

$$x_4 \approx 2.09459 \quad (\text{stabilized to 5 decimal places})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **True root:** $\alpha \approx 2.0945514815$. The Newton-Raphson method has converged rapidly in 3 iterations — illustrating **quadratic convergence** in action.

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example: Bisection Method

**Example:** Find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - x - 1 = 0$ in $[1, 2]$ with $\varepsilon = 0.0625$.

**Step 0 — Verify Sign Change:**

$$f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0, \quad f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5 > 0$$

So $f(1) \cdot f(2) < 0$. Bracket is valid.

**Iteration Table:**

| $n$ | $a$ | $b$ | $c = (a+b)/2$ | $f(c)$ | Sign | New Interval |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 1.5 | $3.375 - 1.5 - 1 = 0.875$ | $+$ | $[1.0, 1.5]$ |
| 2 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 1.25 | $1.953 - 1.25 - 1 = -0.297$ | $-$ | $[1.25, 1.5]$ |
| 3 | 1.25 | 1.5 | 1.375 | $2.600 - 1.375 - 1 = 0.225$ | $+$ | $[1.25, 1.375]$ |
| 4 | 1.25 | 1.375 | 1.3125 | $2.260 - 1.3125 - 1 = -0.052$ | $-$ | $[1.3125, 1.375]$ |

After 4 iterations, interval width is $0.0625 < \varepsilon$. Root: $x \approx 1.3247$ (true: $\approx 1.32472$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Comparative Convergence Geometry (Block Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **decision logic** that an engineer traverses when selecting a root-finding method for a given process equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Equation f of x equals 0"] --> B["Is f of x continuous and sign change bracketed?"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Bracketing Methods Available"]
    B -- No --> D["Use Open Methods with Initial Guess x0"]
    C --> C1["Bisection Method: c is average of a and b"]
    C --> C2["Regula Falsi: c by linear interpolation of a f b"]
    C1 --> E1["Order 1: Linear, Guaranteed"]
    C2 --> E2["Order 1: Linear, Faster than Bisection"]
    D --> D1["Newton Raphson: needs f prime of x"]
    D --> D2["Secant Method: approximates f prime of x"]
    D --> D3["Fixed Point: rewrite as x equals g of x"]
    D1 --> F1["Order 2: Quadratic, Fastest"]
    D2 --> F2["Order approx 1.618: Superlinear"]
    D3 --> F3["Order 1 if g prime alpha less than 1"]
    E1 --> G["Apply Convergence Check"]
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H{"Absolute Error less than epsilon?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Return Approximate Root xn plus 1"]
    H -- No --> J["Iteration n equals n plus 1, Continue"]
    J --> D1
    J --> C1
    J --> C2
    J --> D2
    J --> D3
    I --> K["Process Engineering Result Obtained"]
```

## 4.2 Newton-Raphson Geometric Construction (Flowchart)

This schematic captures the **geometric construction** underlying the Newton-Raphson update: draw tangent, project to x-axis, repeat.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Current point xn, f of xn on curve"] --> T["Draw tangent line at xn"]
    T --> T1["Slope equals f prime of xn"]
    T1 --> P1["Tangent meets x axis at xn plus 1"]
    P1 --> P2["Evaluate f of xn plus 1"]
    P2 --> Q{"f of xn plus 1 close to zero?"}
    Q -- No --> P0
    Q -- Yes --> R["Root approximated by xn plus 1"]
```

## 4.3 Bisection Interval Halving Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Initial Bracket a, b with f of a times f of b less than 0"] --> S1["Compute midpoint c is average of a and b"]
    S1 --> S2["Evaluate f of c"]
    S2 --> D1{"Sign of f of a times f of c"}
    D1 -- Less than 0 --> U1["Update: b equals c"]
    D1 -- Greater than 0 --> U2["Update: a equals c"]
    U1 --> CHK{"Interval half width less than epsilon?"}
    U2 --> CHK
    CHK -- Yes --> OUT["Root equals midpoint c"]
    CHK -- No --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Distinguish between a polynomial equation and a transcendental equation. Give one example of each as encountered in chemical engineering.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **polynomial equation** consists of a finite sum of non-negative integer powers of the variable, e.g., $x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6 = 0$. A **transcendental equation** involves transcendental functions like $\sin$, $\cos$, $\exp$, $\log$, etc. **Examples in process engineering:**
> - *Polynomial:* Cubic EOS compressibility equation $Z^3 - Z^2 + (A - B - B^2)Z - AB = 0$ (van der Waals form).
> - *Transcendental:* Antoine equation rearranged for boiling point, $\log_{10}(P) = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$, where $T$ is implicit if $P$ is specified.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark definition, 1 mark distinction, 1 mark process engineering example.)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**State the convergence criterion for the Fixed-Point Iteration method. What happens if $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert > 1$?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Fixed-Point Iteration $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ converges to a root $\alpha$ of $x = g(x)$ if and only if $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$ in a neighborhood of $\alpha$. This is called the **contraction mapping condition**. If $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert > 1$, the iteration **diverges** — successive iterates move progressively away from the true root, and the sequence $\{x_n\}$ oscillates or grows without bound. The case $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert = 1$ is marginal and convergence is not guaranteed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Pattern]

**Solve the equation $x^3 - 4x - 9 = 0$ using the Newton-Raphson method, taking $x_0 = 2.5$. Perform iterations until $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < 0.001$. Also state the order of convergence.**

#### Part (a) — Setup and First Three Iterations (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The function is $f(x) = x^3 - 4x - 9$ and its derivative is $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 4$.

**Iteration 1 ($n = 0$):**

$$f(2.5) = (2.5)^3 - 4(2.5) - 9 = 15.625 - 10 - 9 = -3.375$$

$$f'(2.5) = 3(2.5)^2 - 4 = 18.75 - 4 = 14.75$$

$$x_1 = 2.5 - \dfrac{-3.375}{14.75} = 2.5 + 0.22881 = 2.72881$$

**Iteration 2 ($n = 1$):**

$$f(2.72881) = (2.72881)^3 - 4(2.72881) - 9$$

$$(2.72881)^3 = 20.3235 \quad (\text{calculator expansion})$$

$$f(2.72881) = 20.3235 - 10.91524 - 9 = 0.40826$$

$$f'(2.72881) = 3(2.72881)^2 - 4 = 3(7.44670) - 4 = 22.34010 - 4 = 18.34010$$

$$x_2 = 2.72881 - \dfrac{0.40826}{18.34010} = 2.72881 - 0.02226 = 2.70655$$

**Iteration 3 ($n = 2$):**

$$f(2.70655) = (2.70655)^3 - 4(2.70655) - 9$$

$$(2.70655)^3 \approx 19.8236$$

$$f(2.70655) = 19.8236 - 10.8262 - 9 = -0.0026$$

$$f'(2.70655) = 3(2.70655)^2 - 4 = 3(7.32542) - 4 = 21.97626 - 4 = 17.97626$$

$$x_3 = 2.70655 - \dfrac{-0.0026}{17.97626} = 2.70655 + 0.000145 \approx 2.70670$$

**Check Convergence:** $\vert x_3 - x_2 \vert = \vert 2.70670 - 2.70655 \vert = 0.00015 < 0.001$. **Converged.**

#### Part (b) — Order of Convergence and Verification (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **order of convergence** of Newton-Raphson is **quadratic (order 2)** when $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$ and $f''(\alpha)$ is bounded.

**Verification via error ratio test:**

$$\text{Error at } x_0: \quad e_0 = 2.5 - 2.7065 = -0.2065$$

$$\text{Error at } x_1: \quad e_1 = 2.72881 - 2.7065 = 0.02231$$

$$\text{Error at } x_2: \quad e_2 = 2.70655 - 2.7065 = 0.00005$$

**Ratio check** (for $p = 2$): $\dfrac{\vert e_2 \vert}{\vert e_1 \vert^2} = \dfrac{0.00005}{(0.02231)^2} = \dfrac{0.00005}{0.000498} \approx 0.1004$

This is approximately constant, confirming **quadratic convergence**. The true root is $\alpha \approx 2.70653$.

---

### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Using the Bisection method, find the root of $f(x) = x^3 - x - 1 = 0$ accurate to $\varepsilon = 0.01$ in the interval $[1, 2]$.**

#### Part (a) — Convergence Theory and Tabulation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence theory for Bisection:**
> After $n$ iterations, the interval length is $\dfrac{b - a}{2^n}$. Stopping condition: $\dfrac{b - a}{2^n} < \varepsilon$.

For $\varepsilon = 0.01$ with $b - a = 1$:

$$\dfrac{1}{2^n} < 0.01 \implies 2^n > 100 \implies n \geq 7$$

**Tabulation of Bisection Iterations:**

| $n$ | $a$ | $b$ | $c = (a+b)/2$ | $f(c)$ | Sign | New Interval |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0000 | 2.0000 | 1.5000 | $0.8750$ | $+$ | $[1.0000, 1.5000]$ |
| 2 | 1.0000 | 1.5000 | 1.2500 | $-0.2969$ | $-$ | $[1.2500, 1.5000]$ |
| 3 | 1.2500 | 1.5000 | 1.3750 | $0.2246$ | $+$ | $[1.2500, 1.3750]$ |
| 4 | 1.2500 | 1.3750 | 1.3125 | $-0.0515$ | $-$ | $[1.3125, 1.3750]$ |
| 5 | 1.3125 | 1.3750 | 1.3438 | $0.0826$ | $+$ | $[1.3125, 1.3438]$ |
| 6 | 1.3125 | 1.3438 | 1.3281 | $0.0147$ | $+$ | $[1.3125, 1.3281]$ |
| 7 | 1.3125 | 1.3281 | 1.3203 | $-0.0185$ | $-$ | $[1.3203, 1.3281]$ |

#### Part (b) — Final Approximation and Error Analysis (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

After 7 iterations, the interval is $[1.3203, 1.3281]$ with half-width $0.0039 < 0.01$. **Convergence achieved.**

**Best estimate:**

$$x \approx \dfrac{1.3203 + 1.3281}{2} = 1.3242$$

**Error analysis:** The maximum possible error is half the final interval width:

$$E_{\max} = \dfrac{1.3281 - 1.3203}{2} = 0.0039 < 0.01 \quad \checkmark$$

**True root comparison:** $\alpha = 1.3247179572$. Approximate error $= 0.0005 < 0.01$. **Result accepted.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Bisection Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign-test error:** Forgetting to update only the *bracket side* with the same sign. Always check $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$ first.
> 2. **Iteration count mismatch:** Examiners often ask "minimum iterations to achieve tolerance." Use $n \geq \log_2\left(\dfrac{b-a}{\varepsilon}\right)$ and round up.
> 3. **Premature stopping:** Stop only when $(b-a)/2 < \varepsilon$, not when $f(c) = 0$ (this rarely happens in floating-point).
> 4. **Wrong initial bracket:** If $f(a) \cdot f(b) > 0$, the bracket is invalid and the method will fail silently.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Newton-Raphson Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Derivative arithmetic:** Losing 2 marks for miscalculating $f'(x_n)$.
> 2. **Stopping criterion ambiguity:** Examiners are strict — state explicitly which convergence criterion is used.
> 3. **Convergence failure:** If iteration diverges, students often continue blindly. Always state "diverges" if $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$ grows.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Bisection:** Always converges (linear, $p = 1$); needs $f(a) f(b) < 0$; slow but reliable. Bracket halves each step.

- **Regula Falsi:** Linear convergence (order 1); faster than Bisection; uses linear interpolation between $(a, f(a))$ and $(b, f(b))$.

- **Newton-Raphson:** **Quadratic convergence** (order 2) — the gold standard. Requires $f'(x) \neq 0$. Formula: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$.

- **Secant Method:** Superlinear convergence (order $\approx 1.618$ — the **golden ratio**); no derivative needed; uses two initial guesses.

- **Fixed-Point Iteration:** Linear convergence **only if** $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$. Rewrite $f(x) = 0$ as $x = g(x)$.

- **Convergence Orders Hierarchy:** Bisection ≈ Regula Falsi ≈ Fixed-Point (order 1) $<$ Secant (order 1.618) $<$ Newton-Raphson (order 2).

- **Process Engineering Hot Spots:** Bubble/dew point calculations, CSTR design equations, cubic EOS ($Z$-factor), flash calculations, Antoine equation implicit solves.

- **Common KTU 2024 Trap:** Students confuse the **Secant** method (two-point, no derivative) with **Regula Falsi** (two-point, bracketing). Remember: Secant discards one point each step; Regula Falsi always retains the bracket.

- **Formula Recap (Memorize):**
  - Newton-Raphson: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$
  - Secant: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$
  - Regula Falsi: $c = \dfrac{a f(b) - b f(a)}{f(b) - f(a)}$
  - Bisection: $c = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$
  - Fixed-Point: $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$

- **Convergence Theorem Conditions:**
  - Bisection: continuous $f$ + sign change.
  - Newton-Raphson: $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$ (simple root).
  - Fixed-Point: $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bracketing Methods: Bisection Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bracketing Methods: Bisection Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bracketing Method (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *bracketing method* is a class of root-finding algorithms used to determine the real root of a continuous non-linear equation $f(x) = 0$, where the algorithm systematically narrows down (or "brackets") the root within an interval $[a, b]$ by repeatedly reducing the search space based on sign-change analysis of the function values.

The **Bisection Method** (also called the *Interval Halving Method* or *Binary Search Method*) is the most fundamental, robust, and pedagogically significant bracketing technique. It relies on the cornerstone **Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)** of real analysis and guarantees convergence provided the function is continuous on the chosen interval and the function values at the endpoints exhibit opposite signs.

The formal mathematical statement of the algorithm is:

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Convergence of Bisection):**
> If $f(x)$ is a continuous function on the closed interval $[a, b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, then there exists at least one root $\alpha \in (a, b)$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$. The bisection method generates a sequence of midpoints $\{c_n\}$ that converges monotonically to $\alpha$ with an error bound reduced by a factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ at every iteration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are searching for a particular page number in a thick 1000-page textbook, and you know the page exists somewhere between page 200 and page 800. What is the smartest strategy?

1. Open the book exactly in the middle (page 500).
2. Check if your target page is before or after page 500.
3. Eliminate the half that does **not** contain the target.
4. Repeat the same procedure on the remaining half.

This is *exactly* how the Bisection Method works. The "page number" is the **root** $\alpha$, the "200 to 800 range" is the **bracket** $[a, b]$, and each "middle opening" is the **midpoint** $c = \tfrac{a+b}{2}$. The function $f(x)$ plays the role of a "compass" — it tells you on which side of the midpoint the root lies by checking the sign of $f(c)$ relative to $f(a)$ or $f(b)$.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Bisection Metrics used in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
> - **Convergence Rate:** Linear (order of convergence $n = 1$)
> - **Error Reduction Factor per Iteration:** $\tfrac{1}{2}$ (i.e., 50% reduction)
> - **Required Condition (Bracket):** $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$
> - **Standard Stopping Criteria:** $|f(c_n)| < \varepsilon$ **OR** $\tfrac{b-a}{2^n} < \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is the prescribed **tolerance** (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ in process engineering contexts).

### 1.4 Why Process Engineers Need This Method

In **chemical process engineering**, the bisection method is heavily used for:
- Solving non-linear equations arising in **thermodynamic equilibrium** calculations.
- Finding the **bubble-point and dew-point temperatures** of multi-component mixtures.
- Determining the **fugacity coefficient roots** in equations of state (e.g., Peng-Robinson, van der Waals).
- Solving **Antoine equation** for saturation pressure of pure components.

The robustness of the method (it *cannot* diverge under proper bracketing) makes it the first choice for safety-critical engineering computations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Decomposition

The Bisection Method follows a strict, deterministic, six-step operational cycle at every iteration $n$:

- **Step 1 — Initialization (Bracket Setup):**
  Identify an initial interval $[a_0, b_0]$ such that the function is continuous and $f(a_0) \cdot f(b_0) < 0$. This sign difference guarantees a root exists inside the interval by IVT.

- **Step 2 — Midpoint Computation:**
  Compute the midpoint $c_n$ of the current interval using the arithmetic mean:
  $$c_n = \frac{a_n + b_n}{2}$$
  This is the *current best estimate* of the root.

- **Step 3 — Function Evaluation at Midpoint:**
  Compute $f(c_n)$. This single evaluation dictates the entire future of the iteration.

- **Step 4 — Decision Branch (The "How"):**
  Compare the sign of $f(c_n)$ with the signs of $f(a_n)$ and $f(b_n)$:
  - If $f(a_n) \cdot f(c_n) < 0$, the root lies in the *left sub-interval* — set $b_{n+1} = c_n$ and $a_{n+1} = a_n$.
  - If $f(c_n) \cdot f(b_n) < 0$, the root lies in the *right sub-interval* — set $a_{n+1} = c_n$ and $b_{n+1} = b_n$.
  - If $f(c_n) = 0$, the root is found exactly — terminate the algorithm.

- **Step 5 — Convergence Check (The "When to Stop"):**
  Test any one (or both) of the following stopping criteria:
  - **Function value criterion:** $\vert f(c_n) \vert < \varepsilon$
  - **Interval width criterion:** $\tfrac{b_n - a_n}{2} < \varepsilon$
  
  where $\varepsilon$ is a pre-specified tolerance.

- **Step 6 — Iteration Counter Increment:**
  Set $n \leftarrow n+1$ and return to Step 2 with the new (halved) interval.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Bisection Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the bracket always halve?**
> Because the midpoint $c_n$ is mathematically guaranteed to be *inside* the current interval $[a_n, b_n]$. Once we determine on which side of $c_n$ the root actually lies, we discard the half that contains no root. Since the discarded half is exactly half of the parent interval, the new interval length is $\tfrac{b_n - a_n}{2}$. This halving is **independent of the function's complexity**, which is why the method is so robust.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MUST MEMORIZE FOR KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Midpoint (Iteration $n$) | $c_n = \tfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$ | New root estimate at iteration $n$ |
| 2 | Bracket Condition (IVT) | $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ | Required precondition for bisection to start |
| 3 | Error Bound (after $n$ iters) | $E_n = \tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$ | Maximum possible error after $n$ iterations |
| 4 | Interval Length (after $n$ iters) | $L_n = \tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n}}$ | Length of bracket after $n$ iterations |
| 5 | Stopping Criterion (Width) | $\tfrac{b_n - a_n}{2} \leq \varepsilon$ | Interval half-width must be $\leq$ tolerance |
| 6 | Stopping Criterion (Function) | $\vert f(c_n) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ | Function value at midpoint must be $\leq$ tolerance |
| 7 | Minimum Iterations Formula | $n \geq \tfrac{\log\!\left(\tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log 2}$ | Number of iterations *guaranteed* to achieve tolerance $\varepsilon$ |
| 8 | Order of Convergence | $p = 1$ (linear) | Bisection converges *linearly* |
| 9 | Convergence Rate (Asymptotic) | $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}$ | Per-iteration error multiplier |
| 10 | Sign Decision Rule | $\text{sign}\big(f(a_n) \cdot f(c_n)\big)$ | Determines left or right half-discard |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **distillation column** design, the engineer must solve the implicit Antoine equation:

$$\ln P^{sat} = A - \frac{B}{T + C}$$

for the bubble-point temperature $T$ given a saturation pressure $P^{sat}$. Since $T$ appears implicitly (inside a denominator of a non-linear function), the equation is rearranged as $f(T) = 0$ and solved using bisection. The chemical engineer brackets the temperature between the normal boiling point and the critical temperature — a natural, physically meaningful interval where IVT is guaranteed to hold.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Error Bound Formula

The error bound of the bisection method is one of its most prized features — it can be **calculated *before* running the algorithm**. This is the source of its absolute reliability.

**Derivation:**

At iteration $n = 0$, the initial interval is $[a_0, b_0]$ of length $L_0 = b_0 - a_0$. The midpoint is $c_0 = \tfrac{a_0 + b_0}{2}$, and the true root $\alpha$ is somewhere inside the interval. Therefore, the maximum possible distance between $c_0$ and $\alpha$ is half the interval length:

$$\vert \alpha - c_0 \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2}$$

This establishes the base case $n = 0$ of the error bound.

**Inductive step:** Assume the error bound holds at iteration $n$:

$$\vert \alpha - c_n \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$$

At iteration $n+1$, the algorithm selects either the left or right sub-interval, each of length exactly $\tfrac{b_n - a_n}{2}$. Since $b_n - a_n = \tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$ (each iteration halves the interval), the new interval length is:

$$L_{n+1} = \frac{L_n}{2} = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$$

The new midpoint $c_{n+1}$ is at the center of this halved interval, so the worst-case distance from the root is half the new interval length:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert \alpha - c_{n+1} \vert &\leq \frac{L_{n+1}}{2} \\
&\leq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}} \\
&\leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+2}}
\end{aligned}$$

This proves the induction step. By the principle of mathematical induction, the bound holds for all $n \geq 0$:

$$\boxed{\;\vert \alpha - c_n \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}\;}$$

This is the **a priori error estimate** that makes bisection mathematically bulletproof.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Iteration Formula

To find the minimum number of iterations $n$ required to guarantee the error is less than $\varepsilon$, we set the error bound equal to $\varepsilon$ and solve for $n$:

$$\frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}} \leq \varepsilon$$

Rearranging:

$$2^{n+1} \geq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}$$

Taking binary logarithm on both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
(n+1) \log 2 &\geq \log\!\left(\frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right) \\
n+1 &\geq \frac{\log\!\left(\tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log 2} \\
n &\geq \frac{\log\!\left(\tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log 2} - 1
\end{aligned}$$

Equivalently, with a slightly looser but commonly-used form:

$$n \geq \frac{\log\!\left(\tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log 2}$$

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Problem (Model Question Pattern):**
> Find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - 4x - 9 = 0$ using the Bisection Method, taking the initial bracket as $[a, b] = [2, 3]$ and tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.01$. Perform **4 iterations** and report the approximate root.

**Step 0 — Verification of Bracket:**
Compute $f(2) = 2^3 - 4(2) - 9 = 8 - 8 - 9 = -9 < 0$ and $f(3) = 27 - 12 - 9 = +6 > 0$. Therefore $f(2) \cdot f(3) = -54 < 0$. The bracket is valid.

**Iteration 1:**
Midpoint: $c_1 = \tfrac{2+3}{2} = 2.5$
Function value: $f(2.5) = (2.5)^3 - 4(2.5) - 9 = 15.625 - 10 - 9 = -3.375$
Sign check: $f(2.5) = -3.375 < 0$, same sign as $f(2)$. Therefore the root lies in $[2.5, 3]$.
Update: $a_2 = 2.5$, $b_2 = 3.0$.

**Iteration 2:**
Midpoint: $c_2 = \tfrac{2.5 + 3.0}{2} = 2.75$
Function value: $f(2.75) = (2.75)^3 - 4(2.75) - 9 = 20.7969 - 11 - 9 = 0.7969$
Sign check: $f(2.75) > 0$, same sign as $f(3)$. Root lies in $[2.5, 2.75]$.
Update: $a_3 = 2.5$, $b_3 = 2.75$.

**Iteration 3:**
Midpoint: $c_3 = \tfrac{2.5 + 2.75}{2} = 2.625$
Function value: $f(2.625) = (2.625)^3 - 4(2.625) - 9 = 18.0879 - 10.5 - 9 = -1.4121$
Sign check: $f(2.625) < 0$, same sign as $f(2.5)$. Root lies in $[2.625, 2.75]$.
Update: $a_4 = 2.625$, $b_4 = 2.75$.

**Iteration 4:**
Midpoint: $c_4 = \tfrac{2.625 + 2.75}{2} = 2.6875$
Function value: $f(2.6875) = (2.6875)^3 - 4(2.6875) - 9 = 19.4126 - 10.75 - 9 = -0.3374$
Sign check: $f(2.6875) < 0$, same sign as $f(2.625)$. Root lies in $[2.6875, 2.75]$.

**Convergence Verification:** Interval half-width is $\tfrac{2.75 - 2.625}{2} = 0.0625$. Tolerance was $\varepsilon = 0.01$, so we are not yet within tolerance. However, after 4 iterations our best estimate is $c_4 = 2.6875$, with the guarantee that $\vert \alpha - 2.6875 \vert \leq 0.0625$.

**Summary Table (Analytical Matrix):**

| Iteration $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n$ | $f(c_n)$ | Updated Interval |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0000 | 3.0000 | 2.5000 | $-3.3750$ | $[2.5000, 3.0000]$ |
| 2 | 2.5000 | 3.0000 | 2.7500 | $+0.7969$ | $[2.5000, 2.7500]$ |
| 3 | 2.5000 | 2.7500 | 2.6250 | $-1.4121$ | $[2.6250, 2.7500]$ |
| 4 | 2.6250 | 2.7500 | 2.6875 | $-0.3374$ | $[2.6875, 2.7500]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **True root check:** The exact root is $\alpha \approx 2.7065$. After 4 iterations our estimate $2.6875$ has an absolute error of $\vert 2.7065 - 2.6875 \vert = 0.0190$, which is consistent with the theoretical bound of $0.0625$. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Initialize bracket a0 b0] --> B{Step2: Verify f a times f b less than 0}
    B -- No --> Z[STOP: Invalid bracket, choose new interval]
    B -- Yes --> C[Step3: Compute midpoint c equals a plus b over 2]
    C --> D[Step4: Evaluate f at c]
    D --> E{f at c equals 0}
    E -- Yes --> Y[STOP: Exact root found at c]
    E -- No --> F{f at a times f at c less than 0}
    F -- Yes --> G[Step5a: Set a new equals a, b new equals c]
    F -- No --> H[Step5b: Set a new equals c, b new equals b]
    G --> I[Step6: Increment n, n plus 1]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Step7: Half width less than epsilon}
    J -- Yes --> X[STOP: Converged, report c as root]
    J -- No --> C
```

### 4.2 Visual Schematic of the Halving Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Initial bracket a0 to b0, length L] --> P2[Iteration 1: midpoint c1, length L over 2]
    P2 --> P3[Iteration 2: midpoint c2, length L over 4]
    P3 --> P4[Iteration 3: midpoint c3, length L over 8]
    P4 --> P5[Iteration n: midpoint cn, length L over 2 to the power n]
    P5 --> P6[Root alpha bracketed within epsilon tolerance]
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic Block Diagram (Sign-Change Branching)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given f a and f b with opposite signs] --> Q2[Compute c equals a plus b over 2]
    Q2 --> Q3[Evaluate f c]
    Q3 --> Q4{Is f a times f c negative}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R1[Root in LEFT half: replace b with c]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is f c times f b negative}
    Q5 -- Yes --> R2[Root in RIGHT half: replace a with c]
    Q5 -- No --> R3[Error: numerical instability, recompute]
    R1 --> S[New bracket ready for next iteration]
    R2 --> S
```

### 4.4 Engineering Application Flow (Process Engineering Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Process Model yields implicit equation] --> E2[Define f x equals LHS minus RHS]
    E2 --> E3[Identify physical bracket from domain knowledge]
    E3 --> E4[Apply Bisection Algorithm]
    E4 --> E5{Has bracket converged to epsilon}
    E5 -- No --> E4
    E5 -- Yes --> E6[Report root: bubble point T, fugacity, etc]
    E6 --> E7[Use root in downstream process design]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define the Bisection Method. State the necessary and sufficient condition for its convergence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Bisection Method is a numerical root-finding technique that repeatedly halves a given interval $[a, b]$ containing a root of $f(x) = 0$ and selects the sub-interval in which the function changes sign. The **necessary and sufficient condition** for convergence is that $f(x)$ must be **continuous on the closed interval** $[a, b]$ and the function values at the endpoints must have **opposite signs**, i.e.,
$$f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$$
This is the direct application of the Intermediate Value Theorem, which guarantees at least one root $\alpha \in (a, b)$.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, May 2024 Style]**

**Q2. What is the order of convergence of the Bisection Method? Justify with the error bound formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Bisection Method exhibits **linear convergence** (order of convergence $p = 1$). The error bound after $n$ iterations is given by:
$$E_n = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$$
This means the error is reduced by a constant factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ at every iteration, which is the defining characteristic of linear convergence. The convergence rate is independent of the function's derivative, making the method extremely reliable but slower than Newton-Raphson (which is quadratic) or secant (superlinear).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to verify $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ before starting — **lose 2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Stopping criterion is mis-stated as $\tfrac{b-a}{2^n} < \varepsilon$ instead of the correct $\tfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$ — **lose 1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Rounding the intermediate $c_n$ values prematurely — full marks only if **at least 4 decimal places** are retained until final reporting.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to tabulate the iterations properly — KTU examiners award marks *only* for the table entries, not narrative alone.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the error bound formula for the Bisection Method and show that the method has linear convergence. Use a clearly labeled diagram or table to justify your steps.

**Model Solution:**

We begin with the initial bracket $[a_0, b_0]$ containing the root $\alpha$, with interval length $L_0 = b_0 - a_0$. The first midpoint is:
$$c_0 = \frac{a_0 + b_0}{2}$$

Since $\alpha \in [a_0, b_0]$, the maximum possible distance from $c_0$ to $\alpha$ is half the interval:
$$|\alpha - c_0| \leq \frac{L_0}{2} = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2}$$

**Inductive hypothesis:** Assume after $n$ iterations the error satisfies:
$$|\alpha - c_n| \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Inductive step:** At iteration $n+1$, the algorithm replaces the interval with one of length $L_{n+1} = L_n/2 = \tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$. The new midpoint $c_{n+1}$ is at the center of this halved interval, so:
$$|\alpha - c_{n+1}| \leq \frac{L_{n+1}}{2} = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+2}}$$

This completes the induction. Hence the **a priori error bound** is:
$$\boxed{E_n = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}}$$

**Order of convergence:** Since $E_{n+1} = \tfrac{1}{2} E_n$, the error is multiplied by a constant factor $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}$ at every step. By the formal definition of order of convergence $p$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{|E_{n+1}|}{|E_n|^p} = \lambda$$

With $p = 1$ and $\lambda = 1/2$, the limit is $1/2 \neq 0$, confirming linear convergence.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - x - 1 = 0$ accurate to $\varepsilon = 0.01$ using the Bisection Method, with initial bracket $[a, b] = [1, 2]$. Perform iterations until the stopping criterion is satisfied.

**Model Solution:**

**Bracket verification:** $f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0$ and $f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = +5 > 0$. Since $f(1) \cdot f(2) = -5 < 0$, the bracket is valid.

**Iterations Tabulated:**

| Iter $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n$ | $f(c_n)$ | Sign | New Interval | Half-Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0000 | 2.0000 | 1.5000 | $+0.8750$ | $+$ | $[1.0000, 1.5000]$ | 0.2500 |
| 2 | 1.0000 | 1.5000 | 1.2500 | $-0.2969$ | $-$ | $[1.2500, 1.5000]$ | 0.1250 |
| 3 | 1.2500 | 1.5000 | 1.3750 | $+0.2246$ | $+$ | $[1.2500, 1.3750]$ | 0.0625 |
| 4 | 1.2500 | 1.3750 | 1.3125 | $-0.0510$ | $-$ | $[1.3125, 1.3750]$ | 0.0313 |
| 5 | 1.3125 | 1.3750 | 1.3438 | $+0.0827$ | $+$ | $[1.3125, 1.3438]$ | 0.0156 |
| 6 | 1.3125 | 1.3438 | 1.3281 | $+0.0152$ | $+$ | $[1.3125, 1.3281]$ | 0.0078 |

**Detailed calculation for Iteration 1:** $c_1 = \tfrac{1+2}{2} = 1.5$, $f(1.5) = (1.5)^3 - 1.5 - 1 = 3.375 - 2.5 = 0.875$. Since $f(1) \cdot f(1.5) = -0.875 < 0$, the root lies in $[1, 1.5]$, so we update $b_1 = 1.5$.

**Detailed calculation for Iteration 6:** $c_6 = \tfrac{1.3125 + 1.3438}{2} = 1.3281$, $f(1.3281) = (1.3281)^3 - 1.3281 - 1 = 2.3427 - 2.3281 = 0.0146$. The half-width $\tfrac{b_6 - a_6}{2} = \tfrac{1.3281 - 1.3125}{2} = 0.0078 < 0.01 = \varepsilon$. **Convergence achieved.**

**Final Answer:** The approximate root is $\alpha \approx 1.3281$ (true value $\approx 1.3247$, absolute error $\approx 0.0034 \leq \varepsilon$). ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State the Intermediate Value Theorem and explain its role in the Bisection Method. Compute the minimum number of iterations required to reduce the error below $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$ when the initial bracket is $[1, 4]$.

**Model Solution:**

**Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT):** If $f(x)$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $k$ is any value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ (i.e., $f(a) \leq k \leq f(b)$ or vice versa), then there exists at least one $c \in (a, b)$ such that $f(c) = k$.

**Role in Bisection:** By taking $k = 0$, the IVT guarantees that a root $\alpha$ exists in $[a, b]$ whenever $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ have opposite signs. The bisection method uses this guarantee iteratively — at every step, the chosen sub-interval is guaranteed to still contain a root, so the algorithm never "loses" the root during halving.

**Minimum iterations calculation:**
Using the formula:
$$n \geq \frac{\log\!\left(\frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log 2}$$

Substituting $b_0 - a_0 = 4 - 1 = 3$ and $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$:
$$n \geq \frac{\log(3 \times 10^5)}{\log 2} = \frac{\log 3 + 5}{0.30103} = \frac{0.4771 + 5}{0.30103} = \frac{5.4771}{0.30103} \approx 18.19$$

Therefore, the minimum number of iterations is $\boxed{n = 19}$ (rounding up to the next integer, since $n$ must be a whole number).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Bisection Method, find the root of $f(x) = e^{-x} - x$ in the interval $[0, 1]$ with a tolerance of $\varepsilon = 0.05$. Show all iterations in a tabular form.

**Model Solution:**

**Bracket verification:** $f(0) = e^0 - 0 = 1 > 0$ and $f(1) = e^{-1} - 1 = 0.3679 - 1 = -0.6321 < 0$. Sign change confirmed.

**Iterations:**

| Iter $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n$ | $f(c_n) = e^{-c_n} - c_n$ | New Interval | Half-Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.5000 | $0.6065 - 0.5 = +0.1065$ | $[0.5000, 1.0000]$ | 0.2500 |
| 2 | 0.5000 | 1.0000 | 0.7500 | $0.4724 - 0.75 = -0.2776$ | $[0.5000, 0.7500]$ | 0.1250 |
| 3 | 0.5000 | 0.7500 | 0.6250 | $0.5353 - 0.625 = -0.0897$ | $[0.5000, 0.6250]$ | 0.0625 |
| 4 | 0.5000 | 0.6250 | 0.5625 | $0.5698 - 0.5625 = +0.0073$ | $[0.5625, 0.6250]$ | 0.0313 |

**Stopping check at Iteration 4:** Half-width $\tfrac{0.6250 - 0.5625}{2} = 0.0313 < 0.05 = \varepsilon$. **Algorithm terminates.**

**Final Answer:** The approximate root is $\alpha \approx c_4 = 0.5625$ (true value $\approx 0.5671$, error $\approx 0.0046$, well within tolerance). ✓

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 Module 1: Bisection Method**

- ⭐ **Bracketing method** = root-finding technique that confines the root within an interval; **always converges** if the bracket condition is satisfied.
- ⭐ **Bisection** = simplest bracketing method; uses *interval halving* strategy based on the **Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)**.
- ⭐ **Pre-condition (MANDATORY):** $f$ must be **continuous on** $[a, b]$ **AND** $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.
- ⭐ **Midpoint Formula:** $c_n = \tfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$ — this is the next approximation to the root.
- ⭐ **Sign-based decision:** If $f(a_n) \cdot f(c_n) < 0$ → keep left half (replace $b$); else → keep right half (replace $a$).
- ⭐ **Error Bound:** $E_n = \tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}$ — **a priori**, computable *before* running iterations.
- ⭐ **Minimum Iterations:** $n \geq \tfrac{\log\!\left(\tfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log 2}$ — always round **UP** to the next integer.
- ⭐ **Convergence Order:** $p = 1$ (**linear**); rate factor $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}$.
- ⭐ **Stopping Criteria:** (i) Half-width $\leq \varepsilon$ OR (ii) $\vert f(c_n) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ — KTU accepts either.
- ⭐ **Key Limitation:** Bisection is *slow* compared to Newton-Raphson but **cannot diverge** — best for **safety-critical** process engineering calculations.
- ⭐ **Tabulation is essential** in KTU exams: every $a_n$, $b_n$, $c_n$, $f(c_n)$ must be tabulated to fetch full marks.
- ⭐ **Engineering uses:** Bubble-point/dew-point temperature, fugacity coefficients, Antoine equation inversions, and equilibrium-stage calculations in distillation.
- ⭐ **Remember:** Bisection **halves the error** every step — after $k$ iterations, the interval shrinks by a factor of $2^k$, a clean, predictable, and dependable rate of convergence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regula-Falsi Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regula-Falsi Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regula-Falsi Method (Method of False Position):** The Regula-Falsi method, also known as the *Method of Linear Interpolation* or *Method of False Position*, is a **bracketing-type root-finding algorithm** used in numerical analysis to determine the root of a non-linear equation $f(x) = 0$. Unlike the Bisection method which simply halves the interval, the Regula-Falsi method estimates the root by constructing a **straight line (chord)** joining the two bracketing points $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$ on the curve, and then finds where this chord intersects the x-axis. The intersection point is taken as the new approximation to the root.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme notation, given an initial bracketing interval $[x_0, x_1]$ where $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$, the iterative formula is:

$$x_2 = x_0 - \frac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)} = \frac{x_0 f(x_1) - x_1 f(x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Mountain Hike Problem:**
> Imagine you are standing on a mountain ridge, with one foot on the left slope (altitude **+500 m**) and the other foot on the right slope (altitude **−300 m**). You know the valley floor (the root where altitude = **0**) lies somewhere between your two feet. The **Bisection method** would tell you to step exactly to the midpoint — a safe but inefficient choice. The **Regula-Falsi method**, however, is smarter: it *stretches a tightrope* between your two feet, and because the right slope is much steeper, the tightrope crosses the ground (zero altitude) much closer to the right foot. The Regula-Falsi formula essentially predicts that intersection point, which is almost always a *better guess* than the midpoint.

- **$f(x_0)$ and $f(x_1)$**: The function values at the two bracketing points (analogous to the altitudes of your two feet).
- **$x_2$**: The new estimate of the root (the spot on the ground where the tightrope touches).
- **Linear interpolation assumption**: The curve is approximated by a straight line locally between $x_0$ and $x_1$ — this is the *linear approximation* core idea of the method.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "False Position"?** The Latin *regula* means "rule" and *falsi* means "false." The name arises because the method deliberately assumes a *false* (linear) position for the curve in order to compute a new, generally more accurate position of the true root.

## 1.3 Significance in Process Engineering

The Regula-Falsi method is heavily applied in chemical and process engineering for:
- Solving **non-linear material and energy balance equations** (e.g., Antoine equation iterations for vapor pressure).
- Determining **reaction equilibrium constants** where no closed-form solution exists.
- Computing **pipe-flow friction factors** from the implicit Colebrook-White equation.
- Resolving **multicomponent vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) relations** in separator design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Regula-Falsi method follows a **deterministic bracketing logic** with these operational steps:

- **Step 1 — Bracket the Root:** Identify two initial guesses $x_0$ and $x_1$ such that $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$. This guarantees, by the **Intermediate Value Theorem**, that at least one root exists in the open interval $(x_0, x_1)$.

- **Step 2 — Compute the False Position:** Using the linear interpolation formula, compute the new estimate $x_2$ on the x-axis where the chord crosses zero.

- **Step 3 — Evaluate the Function at the New Point:** Calculate $f(x_2)$.

- **Step 4 — Update the Bracket (Asymmetric Update Rule):**
  - If $f(x_2) \cdot f(x_0) < 0$ → The root lies in $(x_0, x_2)$, so set $x_1 = x_2$.
  - If $f(x_2) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$ → The root lies in $(x_2, x_1)$, so set $x_0 = x_2$.
  - **The point whose function value has the *opposite sign* to the new value is replaced.** This is what makes Regula-Falsi *asymmetric* — one of the original bracketing points remains fixed until convergence is achieved.

- **Step 5 — Check for Convergence:** Stop when either:
  - $\vert f(x_2) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ (function-value tolerance)
  - $\vert x_2^{(k+1)} - x_2^{(k)} \vert \leq \varepsilon$ (interval/norm tolerance)
  - A maximum number of iterations is reached.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Linear Interpolation

The method exploits the **first-order Taylor-like geometric insight**: locally, between two close points $x_0$ and $x_1$, any smooth continuous function $f(x)$ can be approximated by the straight line passing through $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$. The x-intercept of this line is the simplest algebraic solution to the "where does this line hit zero?" question. The Regula-Falsi formula is mathematically equivalent to *inverse linear interpolation* on the function values.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every formula you will need for a KTU 2024 Scheme ESE / CE on this topic.

| **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description / Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Regula-Falsi iteration (one-point form) | $x_2 = x_0 - \dfrac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$ | Standard form using bracketing points $x_0$ and $x_1$ |
| Regula-Falsi iteration (symmetric form) | $x_2 = \dfrac{x_0 \, f(x_1) - x_1 \, f(x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$ | Numerically stable form, equivalent to the one above |
| Bracket condition | $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$ | Required for guaranteed root existence in $[x_0, x_1]$ |
| Convergence criterion (function) | $\vert f(x_2) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ | Stop when function value is sufficiently close to zero |
| Convergence criterion (argument) | $\vert x_2^{(k+1)} - x_2^{(k)} \vert \leq \varepsilon$ | Stop when successive approximations are sufficiently close |
| Order of convergence | $\alpha \approx 1$ (linear convergence) | Slower than Newton-Raphson ($\alpha = 2$) but more robust |
| Comparison to Bisection | $x_{\text{bisect}} = \dfrac{x_0 + x_1}{2}$ | Bisection is Regula-Falsi with constant weight $1/2$ on each side |
| Modified Regula-Falsi (Illinois) | $f(x_0) \to \dfrac{f(x_0)}{2}$ if same side retained twice | Prevents one-sided stagnation in concave regions |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In process engineering, the Regula-Falsi method is the workhorse for solving the **Colebrook-White equation** for turbulent flow friction factor $f$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

Since $f$ appears on *both sides* implicitly, iterative bracketing methods like Regula-Falsi are the de-facto numerical solvers used in **ASPEN, ChemCAD, and DWSIM** process simulators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Regula-Falsi Formula

We start with the **equation of the straight line** passing through two given points $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$. Using the two-point form of a line:

$$\frac{y - f(x_0)}{x - x_0} = \frac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}$$

We seek the value of $x$ where this chord intersects the x-axis, i.e., where $y = 0$. Substituting $y = 0$:

$$\frac{0 - f(x_0)}{x - x_0} = \frac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}$$

Rearranging the left-hand side:

$$\frac{-f(x_0)}{x - x_0} = \frac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}$$

Cross-multiplying both sides:

$$-f(x_0) \cdot (x_1 - x_0) = [f(x_1) - f(x_0)] \cdot (x - x_0)$$

Dividing both sides by $[f(x_1) - f(x_0)]$:

$$x - x_0 = \frac{-f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$$

Renaming $x$ as $x_2$ (the new approximation) and moving $x_0$ to the right:

$$x_2 = x_0 - \frac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$$

This is the **canonical Regula-Falsi formula** taught in KTU 2024 Scheme textbooks.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Full Analytical Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Find a root of the equation $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ using the Regula-Falsi method, starting with $x_0 = 2$ and $x_1 = 3$, up to **4 iterations** (or until convergence, whichever is sooner). Use tolerance $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$ on $\vert f(x) \vert$.

### Iteration 0 — Verification of Bracket

Evaluate the function at the initial points:

$$f(x_0) = f(2) = 2^3 - 2(2) - 5 = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1$$

$$f(x_1) = f(3) = 3^3 - 2(3) - 5 = 27 - 6 - 5 = 16$$

Since $f(2) \cdot f(3) = (-1)(16) = -16 < 0$, the root is confirmed in $[2, 3]$. ✓

### Iteration 1

Apply the Regula-Falsi formula:

$$x_2 = 2 - \frac{(-1)(3 - 2)}{16 - (-1)} = 2 - \frac{-1}{17} = 2 + \frac{1}{17} = \frac{34 + 1}{17} = \frac{35}{17} \approx 2.058824$$

Evaluate the function at $x_2$:

$$f(2.058824) = (2.058824)^3 - 2(2.058824) - 5$$

$$= 8.7259 - 4.1176 - 5 = -0.3917$$

Since $f(2.058824) \cdot f(3) = (-0.3917)(16) < 0$, replace $x_0$ with $x_2$:

$$x_0 \leftarrow 2.058824, \quad x_1 = 3 \text{ (retained)}$$

### Iteration 2

$$x_2 = 2.058824 - \frac{(-0.3917)(3 - 2.058824)}{16 - (-0.3917)}$$

$$= 2.058824 - \frac{(-0.3917)(0.941176)}{16.3917}$$

$$= 2.058824 - \frac{-0.3686}{16.3917}$$

$$= 2.058824 + 0.02249 = 2.08132$$

Evaluate:

$$f(2.08132) = (2.08132)^3 - 2(2.08132) - 5 = 9.0159 - 4.1626 - 5 = -0.1467$$

Since $f(2.08132) \cdot f(3) < 0$, again replace $x_0$:

$$x_0 \leftarrow 2.08132, \quad x_1 = 3 \text{ (still retained)}$$

### Iteration 3

$$x_2 = 2.08132 - \frac{(-0.1467)(3 - 2.08132)}{16 - (-0.1467)}$$

$$= 2.08132 - \frac{(-0.1467)(0.91868)}{16.1467}$$

$$= 2.08132 - \frac{-0.13478}{16.1467}$$

$$= 2.08132 + 0.008347 = 2.08967$$

Evaluate:

$$f(2.08967) = (2.08967)^3 - 2(2.08967) - 5 = 9.1245 - 4.1793 - 5 = -0.0548$$

Again $f(2.08967) \cdot f(3) < 0$, replace $x_0$:

$$x_0 \leftarrow 2.08967$$

### Iteration 4

$$x_2 = 2.08967 - \frac{(-0.0548)(3 - 2.08967)}{16 - (-0.0548)}$$

$$= 2.08967 - \frac{(-0.0548)(0.91033)}{16.0548}$$

$$= 2.08967 - \frac{-0.04989}{16.0548}$$

$$= 2.08967 + 0.003108 = 2.09278$$

Evaluate:

$$f(2.09278) = (2.09278)^3 - 2(2.09278) - 5 = 9.1673 - 4.1856 - 5 = -0.0183$$

### Convergence Summary Table

| **Iteration $k$** | $x_0$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ (new root) | $f(x_2)$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.000000 | 3.000000 | 2.058824 | −0.3917 | Replace $x_0$ |
| 2 | 2.058824 | 3.000000 | 2.081320 | −0.1467 | Replace $x_0$ |
| 3 | 2.081320 | 3.000000 | 2.089670 | −0.0548 | Replace $x_0$ |
| 4 | 2.089670 | 3.000000 | 2.092780 | −0.0183 | Replace $x_0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The endpoint $x_1 = 3$ is *never* replaced — this is the **"one-sided stagnation"** problem of the classical Regula-Falsi method when the function is highly concave on one side. The true root (≈ 2.094551) is being approached, but convergence is **linear** and slow. The **Illinois algorithm** (modified Regula-Falsi) halves $f(x_0)$ whenever the same side is retained twice to break this stagnation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Interpretation — Chord Construction

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of the geometric chord construction step. It shows how the Regula-Falsi method operates conceptually at each iteration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Initial Bracket x0, x1] --> B{f x0 times f x1 less than 0?}
    B -- No --> Z[Reject: Pick New Bracket]
    B -- Yes --> C[Evaluate f of x0 and f of x1]
    C --> D[Draw Chord Joining x0, f x0 and x1, f x1]
    D --> E[Find x-intercept of Chord]
    E --> F[Compute x2 using Regula-Falsi Formula]
    F --> G[Evaluate f of x2]
    G --> H{f x2 times f x0 less than 0?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Set x1 = x2, Keep x0]
    H -- No --> J{f x2 times f x1 less than 0?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Set x0 = x2, Keep x1]
    J -- No --> L[Error: Numerical Instability]
    I --> M{Convergence Met?}
    K --> M
    M -- No --> D
    M -- Yes --> N[Output: Approximate Root = x2]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Iteration Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input: Function f, Initial Guess x0 x1, Tolerance eps] --> P2[Bracket Verification]
    P2 --> P3[Linear Interpolation Engine]
    P3 --> P4[Root Estimator x2]
    P4 --> P5[Convergence Checker]
    P5 -- Not Converged --> P2
    P5 -- Converged --> P6[Output: Root Approximation]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Architecture — Regula-Falsi vs. Bisection vs. Newton-Raphson

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Nature of Function} --> Q2[Smooth and Derivative Known]
    Q1 --> Q3[Continuous but Derivative Difficult]
    Q1 --> Q4[Noisy or Discontinuous]

    Q2 --> R1[Newton-Raphson: Quadratic Convergence]
    Q3 --> R2[Regula-Falsi: Linear but Robust]
    Q4 --> R3[Bisection: Slowest but Guaranteed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Reading Tip:** Each block in the Mermaid diagrams represents a *logical operation* in the algorithm. The directional arrows (showing `flowchart TD` or `flowchart LR`) indicate the *flow of execution*. Convergence loops in the first diagram are denoted by the arrow returning from the **Convergence Checker** to **Bracket Verification** when the tolerance condition is not yet satisfied.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** What is the Regula-Falsi method? State the iterative formula and the bracketing condition required for its convergence.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Regula-Falsi method, also called the *Method of False Position*, is a **bracketing root-finding algorithm** for solving the non-linear equation $f(x) = 0$. It estimates the root by drawing a chord between two bracketing points and finding its x-intercept. The iterative formula is:
> $$x_2 = x_0 - \frac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$$
> The bracketing condition is $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$, which guarantees (by the Intermediate Value Theorem) that at least one root lies in the open interval $(x_0, x_1)$.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Compare the Regula-Falsi method with the Bisection method in terms of convergence rate and bracketing strategy.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Bracketing strategy:** Both methods require an initial interval $[x_0, x_1]$ such that $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$. However, Bisection always halves the interval symmetrically, while Regula-Falsi replaces *only one* endpoint — the one whose function value shares the same sign as $f(x_2)$.
> - **Convergence rate:** Bisection converges **linearly** with rate $\frac{1}{2}$ per iteration. Regula-Falsi also converges linearly, but the rate depends on the function shape and is generally faster than Bisection.
> - **Robustness:** Bisection is *guaranteed* to converge; classical Regula-Falsi can suffer **one-sided stagnation** in concave functions, requiring the Illinois modification for full robustness.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Choice]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Derive the iterative formula of the Regula-Falsi method from the two-point form of a straight line.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Using the Regula-Falsi method, find the root of $x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ correct to 4 decimal places, starting with $x_0 = 2$ and $x_1 = 3$. Perform **5 iterations**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

We begin with the two-point line equation through $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$:

$$\frac{y - f(x_0)}{x - x_0} = \frac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}$$

To find the x-intercept, we set $y = 0$:

$$\frac{-f(x_0)}{x - x_0} = \frac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$-f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0) = [f(x_1) - f(x_0)](x - x_0)$$

Dividing both sides by $[f(x_1) - f(x_0)]$ and solving for $x$:

$$x - x_0 = \frac{-f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$$

$$x = x_0 - \frac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$$

Renaming $x$ as the new approximation $x_2$:

$$x_2 = x_0 - \frac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Iter $k$ | $x_0$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $f(x_2)$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.000000 | 3.000000 | 2.058824 | −0.3917 | $x_0 \leftarrow x_2$ |
| 2 | 2.058824 | 3.000000 | 2.081320 | −0.1467 | $x_0 \leftarrow x_2$ |
| 3 | 2.081320 | 3.000000 | 2.089670 | −0.0548 | $x_0 \leftarrow x_2$ |
| 4 | 2.089670 | 3.000000 | 2.092780 | −0.0183 | $x_0 \leftarrow x_2$ |
| 5 | 2.092780 | 3.000000 | 2.094270 | −0.0014 | $x_0 \leftarrow x_2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sample Calculation (Iteration 1):** $x_2 = 2 - \dfrac{(-1)(3-2)}{16-(-1)} = 2 - \dfrac{-1}{17} = 2.058824$. Full credit is awarded only when the bracket-update decision is shown explicitly, as in the "Action" column above.

**Conclusion:** After 5 iterations, the root is approximately $x \approx 2.0943$ (true value: $x \approx 2.0946$).

---

### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the geometric interpretation of the Regula-Falsi method. Why is it considered superior to the Bisection method despite both being bracketing methods?

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Apply the Regula-Falsi method to find the root of $\cos(x) - x e^x = 0$ in the interval $[0, 1]$ to 4 decimal places.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Geometric Interpretation:** The Regula-Falsi method draws a **straight line (chord)** joining the two function points $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$ that lie on opposite sides of the x-axis. It then computes the x-coordinate where this chord meets the x-axis. This is essentially an *inverse linear interpolation*: instead of evaluating the function at the midpoint (as in Bisection), it exploits the relative magnitudes of $f(x_0)$ and $f(x_1)$ to estimate a more likely location of the root.

**Why Superior to Bisection:**
- **Adaptive position:** Regula-Falsi weighs the new position inversely with the function magnitudes; Bisection always chooses the midpoint regardless of the function shape.
- **Faster practical convergence** in functions that are well-approximated by chords (e.g., nearly linear near the root).
- **Same robustness:** Both are bracketing methods, so neither will diverge — they both guarantee convergence if $f(x_0) f(x_1) < 0$.

**Drawback:** Classical Regula-Falsi can exhibit *one-sided stagnation* if the function is strongly concave. The Illinois or Pegasus modifications address this by halving the function value at the stagnant endpoint.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Define $f(x) = \cos(x) - x e^x$. Initial values: $x_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 1$.

- $f(0) = \cos(0) - 0 \cdot e^0 = 1 - 0 = 1$
- $f(1) = \cos(1) - 1 \cdot e^1 = 0.5403 - 2.7183 = -2.1780$

Since $f(0) \cdot f(1) < 0$, root exists in $[0, 1]$. ✓

| Iter $k$ | $x_0$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $f(x_2)$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.000000 | 1.000000 | 0.31469 | 0.5197 | $x_1 \leftarrow x_2$ |
| 2 | 0.000000 | 0.31469 | 0.44673 | 0.2034 | $x_1 \leftarrow x_2$ |
| 3 | 0.000000 | 0.44673 | 0.49404 | 0.0702 | $x_1 \leftarrow x_2$ |
| 4 | 0.000000 | 0.49404 | 0.50996 | 0.0099 | $x_1 \leftarrow x_2$ |
| 5 | 0.000000 | 0.50996 | 0.51520 | 0.0004 | Converged |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sample Calculation (Iteration 1):** $x_2 = 0 - \dfrac{1 \cdot (1-0)}{-2.1780 - 1} = 0 - \dfrac{1}{-3.1780} = 0.31469$.

**Conclusion:** The root of $\cos(x) - x e^x = 0$ is approximately $x \approx 0.5177$ (true value: $x \approx 0.5177$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Regula-Falsi Questions:**
> - **Wrong bracket update:** Students often mistakenly update *both* endpoints or update the wrong one. **Rule:** Replace the endpoint whose function value has the *same sign* as $f(x_2)$.
> - **Forgetting to verify the bracket condition** $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$ at the start — failure to do so costs **1 full mark** in KTU valuation.
> - **Sign errors in the numerator:** The formula is $x_0 - \dfrac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$, NOT $x_0 + \dfrac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$.
> - **Omitting the convergence statement:** End the solution with a clear statement such as *"The root after $n$ iterations is $x \approx \ldots$"*. Examiners allot 1 mark specifically for the conclusion line.
> - **Rounding too early:** Always carry at least 6 significant digits during intermediate steps; only round the final answer to the requested precision.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The 10-Point Regula-Falsi Cheat Sheet for Last-Minute Revision:**
> - **Definition:** Bracketing method using linear interpolation (chord) between $f(x_0)$ and $f(x_1)$.
> - **Iterative formula:** $x_2 = x_0 - \dfrac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$.
> - **Pre-condition:** $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$ (mandatory bracket check).
> - **Asymmetric update:** Only the endpoint sharing the same sign with $f(x_2)$ is replaced.
> - **Convergence order:** Linear, $\alpha \approx 1$; slower than Newton-Raphson but guaranteed.
> - **Stagnation risk:** One endpoint can become "stuck" in highly concave functions — use the Illinois modification to halve $f$ at the stagnant side.
> - **Convergence tests:** $\vert f(x_2) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ OR $\vert x_2^{(k+1)} - x_2^{(k)} \vert \leq \varepsilon$.
> - **Comparison:** Bisection = midpoint; Regula-Falsi = weighted by inverse function values; Newton-Raphson = tangent-based.
> - **Engineering use:** Colebrook-White friction factor, Antoine equation roots, VLE equilibrium.
> - **Sign convention:** Always subtract the second fraction — *never* add it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Methods: Newton-Raphson Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Methods: Newton-Raphson Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Newton-Raphson Method** (also known as the Newton-Raphson iterative scheme or Newton's Method) is a premier **open root-finding algorithm** used in numerical analysis to determine successively better approximations to the real roots of a real-valued function $f(x) = 0$. 

Unlike bracketing methods (e.g., Bisection or False Position), the Newton-Raphson method requires only a **single initial guess** ($x_0$) and utilizes the function value $f(x)$ as well as its first derivative $f'(x)$ to construct a tangent line approximation at each iteration. The method falls under the category of **fixed-point iteration schemes** with **quadratic convergence** in the neighborhood of a simple root.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Open methods do not bracket the root. They may diverge if the initial guess is poor, but when they converge, they do so *much faster* than bracketing methods. This is a high-weightage topic in Module 1 of PECHT526.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Hiker

Imagine you are **blindfolded on a mountain** and you must find the valley floor (the root, where elevation = 0). You cannot see the bottom, but you can feel the **slope** of the ground beneath your feet.

- You stand at point $x_0$ and ask: *"What is the steepness here?"* (This is the derivative $f'(x_0)$).
- You also ask: *"How high am I above sea level?"* (This is the function value $f(x_0)$).
- Using slope and height, you mathematically project a **straight line** (the tangent) downward until it hits sea level.
- You then step to that landing point. This becomes your new $x_1$.
- You repeat — each time drawing the slope at your current position — and you get closer to the valley at a **dramatically accelerating** pace.

This geometric picture of *"replacing the curve with its tangent"* is the very heart of the Newton-Raphson Method.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are central to the method:

- **Tolerance ($\varepsilon$):** Typically chosen as **$\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$** or **$\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$** in KTU examination numericals.
- **Maximum Iterations ($N_{max}$):** Usually capped at **50** or **100** to prevent infinite loops in non-converging cases.
- **Stopping Criterion:** $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ or $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$.

> [!NOTE]
> The Newton-Raphson method is the **workhorse of chemical process engineering** — used in solving nonlinear equations of state (e.g., Peng-Robinson, Redlich-Kwong), flash calculations, equilibrium compositions, and reactor design equations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Method Steps Forward

The Newton-Raphson method executes the following structured logic at every iteration:

1. **Initialization:** Choose a starting value $x_0$ that is "reasonably close" to the actual root. Compute $f(x_0)$ and $f'(x_0)$.

2. **Tangent Line Construction:** At the point $(x_n, f(x_n))$, construct the equation of the tangent line to the curve $y = f(x)$:
$$y - f(x_n) = f'(x_n)(x - x_n)$$

3. **x-Intercept Computation:** The new approximation $x_{n+1}$ is the value of $x$ where this tangent line crosses the x-axis (i.e., $y = 0$):
$$0 - f(x_n) = f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

4. **Iterative Update:** Solve the linear equation for $x_{n+1}$:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

5. **Convergence Check:** Compare $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$ with the prescribed tolerance $\varepsilon$. If satisfied, terminate; else, return to Step 2.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Quadratic Convergence

The "Why" of Newton-Raphson's superior speed lies in the **Taylor series expansion** of $f(x)$ around the true root $\alpha$. If we let $e_n = x_n - \alpha$ denote the error at iteration $n$, then for a simple root:

$$e_{n+1} \approx \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \, e_n^{\,2}$$

This means the **error is squared at each step** — a property called **quadratic convergence**. The number of correct digits roughly **doubles** per iteration once you are close to the root.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadratic convergence requires a "good" initial guess** and a **simple root** (where $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$). Near multiple roots, the method degrades to *linear* convergence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Iterative Formula** | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | The heart of the Newton-Raphson update |
| **Stopping Criterion (a)** | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ | Successive approximation error test |
| **Stopping Criterion (b)** | $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$ | Residual error test |
| **Stopping Criterion (c)** | $\dfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} < \varepsilon$ | Relative error test (preferred) |
| **Error Recurrence** | $e_{n+1} = -\dfrac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(\xi)} \, e_n^{\,2}$ | Quadratic convergence law |
| **Order of Convergence** | $p = 2$ (quadratic) | For simple roots |
| **Divergence Condition** | $f'(x_n) = 0$ or near zero | Tangent becomes horizontal, blows up |
| **Oscillation Condition** | $f(x_0) \cdot f''(x_0) \leq 0$ at initial guess | Can cause non-converging cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in PECHT526:** The Newton-Raphson method is the standard workhorse for solving nonlinear algebraic equations arising in chemical engineering thermodynamics (vapor-liquid equilibrium, equation-of-state cubic solves), reaction engineering (CSTR/PFR nonlinear algebraic balances), and mass transfer (implicit finite-difference solutions).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Newton-Raphson Formula from Taylor Series

We begin with the first-order Taylor expansion of $f(x)$ about the point $x_n$:

$$f(x_{n+1}) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n) + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2!}(x_{n+1} - x_n)^2$$

We desire that $x_{n+1}$ be a root, so we set $f(x_{n+1}) = 0$:

$$0 = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n) + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2!}(x_{n+1} - x_n)^2$$

**Step 1 — Truncation to First Order:**
If $x_{n+1}$ is close to $x_n$, then $(x_{n+1} - x_n)^2$ is negligibly small. We drop the second-order term:

$$0 \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the Iterative Term:**
Move $f(x_n)$ to the right-hand side:

$$f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n) = -f(x_n)$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the Derivative:**
Assuming $f'(x_n) \neq 0$, divide both sides by $f'(x_n)$:

$$x_{n+1} - x_n = -\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Update:**
Add $x_n$ to both sides to obtain the **Newton-Raphson update formula**:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Quadratic Convergence Law

We want to show the order of convergence is $p = 2$. Let $\alpha$ be the true root so that $f(\alpha) = 0$. Expand $f(\alpha)$ as a Taylor series around $x_n$:

$$f(\alpha) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(\alpha - x_n) + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2!}(\alpha - x_n)^2$$

Since $f(\alpha) = 0$:

$$0 = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(\alpha - x_n) + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2!}(\alpha - x_n)^2$$

Divide throughout by $f'(x_n)$:

$$0 = \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} + (\alpha - x_n) + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)}(\alpha - x_n)^2$$

From the Newton-Raphson formula, $\dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = x_n - x_{n+1}$, so:

$$0 = (x_n - x_{n+1}) + (\alpha - x_n) + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)}(\alpha - x_n)^2$$

**Step 1 — Group the First Two Terms:**
$$(x_n - x_{n+1}) + (\alpha - x_n) = \alpha - x_{n+1}$$

So we obtain:
$$\alpha - x_{n+1} = -\frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)}(\alpha - x_n)^2$$

**Step 2 — Define the Error:**
Let $e_n = x_n - \alpha$, so $\alpha - x_n = -e_n$ and $\alpha - x_{n+1} = -e_{n+1}$:

$$-e_{n+1} = -\frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)}(-e_n)^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**
$$e_{n+1} = \frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)} e_n^2$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
This is the **quadratic convergence relation**:
$$e_{n+1} \approx C \cdot e_n^2, \quad \text{where } C = \frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $e_{n+1}$ is proportional to $e_n^2$, the order of convergence is exactly $p = 2$. This is why KTU examiners emphasize that "the number of correct decimal places approximately doubles per iteration" once the iterates are near the root.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Worked-Out KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Find the positive root of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6 = 0$ using the Newton-Raphson method with $x_0 = 0$ and tolerance $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$.

**Solution Setup:**
- $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6$
- $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 11$
- Iteration formula: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{x_n^3 - 6x_n^2 + 11x_n - 6}{3x_n^2 - 12x_n + 11}$

**Iteration 1 ($n=0$):**
$$f(0) = 0 - 0 + 0 - 6 = -6, \quad f'(0) = 0 - 0 + 11 = 11$$
$$x_1 = 0 - \frac{-6}{11} = 0.5454545454\ldots$$
Error: $\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert = 0.5455$ (not converged)

**Iteration 2 ($n=1$):**
$$f(0.5455) = 0.1623 - 1.7859 + 5.9999 - 6 = -1.6237$$
$$f'(0.5455) = 0.8925 - 6.5455 + 11 = 5.3470$$
$$x_2 = 0.5455 - \frac{-1.6237}{5.3470} = 0.5455 + 0.3037 = 0.8492$$
Error: $\vert x_2 - x_1 \vert = 0.3037$ (not converged)

**Iteration 3 ($n=2$):**
$$f(0.8492) = 0.6124 - 4.3281 + 9.3412 - 6 = -0.3745$$
$$f'(0.8492) = 2.1633 - 10.1904 + 11 = 2.9729$$
$$x_3 = 0.8492 - \frac{-0.3745}{2.9729} = 0.8492 + 0.1260 = 0.9752$$
Error: $\vert x_3 - x_2 \vert = 0.1260$ (not converged)

**Iteration 4 ($n=3$):**
$$f(0.9752) \approx -0.0247, \quad f'(0.9752) \approx 2.1437$$
$$x_4 = 0.9752 + \frac{0.0247}{2.1437} = 0.9868$$
Error: $\vert x_4 - x_3 \vert = 0.0116$ (not converged)

**Iteration 5 ($n=4$):**
$$f(0.9868) \approx -0.0004, \quad f'(0.9868) \approx 2.0816$$
$$x_5 = 0.9868 + \frac{0.0004}{2.0816} \approx 0.9870$$
Error: $\vert x_5 - x_4 \vert = 0.0002 < 10^{-4}$... approximately converged.

**Note:** The actual positive root is $x = 1.0000$. The method is approaching it quadratically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Iterative Process Flow of the Newton-Raphson Method

The following block-level architecture diagram illustrates the complete control flow of the algorithm, including the convergence decision gate:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[INPUT: Initial guess x0, tolerance eps, max iterations Nmax]
    B --> C[COMPUTE f of x0 and f prime of x0]
    C --> D{Is f prime of x0 approx 0?}
    D -- YES --> E([ERROR: Method fails - tangent is horizontal])
    D -- NO --> F[ITERATION LOOP n = 1 to Nmax]
    F --> G[Compute new approximation using formula x of n plus 1 equals x of n minus f of x of n over f prime of x of n]
    G --> H[Compute function value f of x of n plus 1]
    H --> I{Is absolute f of x of n plus 1 less than eps}
    I -- YES --> J([CONVERGED: Return x of n plus 1 as root])
    I -- NO --> K{Is absolute x of n plus 1 minus x of n less than eps}
    K -- YES --> J
    K -- NO --> L{Is n greater than Nmax}
    L -- NO --> M[Increment iteration counter n to n plus 1]
    M --> F
    L -- YES --> N([DIVERGENCE: Maximum iterations reached])
    E --> O([END])
    J --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Block Diagram

The following diagram maps the geometric relationship between the curve $y = f(x)$, the tangent line at $x_n$, and the new approximation $x_{n+1}$:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Point on Curve: xn, f of xn] --> B[Draw Tangent Line at this Point with slope f prime of xn]
    B --> C[Find x intercept of Tangent Line: set y equals 0]
    C --> D[New Approximation: x of n plus 1 equals xn minus f of xn over f prime of xn]
    D --> E[Move to new Point on Curve: x of n plus 1, f of x of n plus 1]
    E --> F{Tangent crosses closer to True Root alpha}
    F -- YES --> G[Quadratic Convergence: Error squares each iteration]
    F -- NO --> H[Check for Divergence or Oscillation]
```

## 4.3 Convergence vs. Divergence Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Geometric Cause | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| $f'(x_n)$ is large | Steep tangent — large correction | Converges rapidly |
| $f'(x_n)$ is small | Nearly horizontal tangent | Overshoots — may diverge |
| $x_0$ is far from root | Tangent lands far from true root | Possible divergence |
| $f(x_0) \cdot f''(x_0) < 0$ | Inflection point near guess | Possible oscillation |
| Multiple root $f'(\alpha) = 0$ | Tangent is horizontal at root | Linear convergence only |
| $x_0$ lies in monotonic region of $f(x)$ | Well-behaved curve | Reliable quadratic convergence |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**State the Newton-Raphson iterative formula for finding the root of $f(x) = 0$. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of the method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Newton-Raphson iterative formula is:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Advantage (1 Mark):** The method has **quadratic convergence** ($p = 2$), meaning the number of correct decimal places approximately doubles per iteration once the iterates are close to the root. This makes it significantly faster than bracketing methods such as Bisection (which has only linear convergence).

**Disadvantage (1 Mark):** The method requires the **evaluation of the derivative** $f'(x_n)$ at every step, which may be computationally expensive or even impossible for functions with complex, implicit, or noisy derivatives. Additionally, the method can **diverge** if the initial guess is poor or if $f'(x_n) \approx 0$ at any iteration.

**Formula statement (1 Mark):** Correct expression as above.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**What is the order of convergence of the Newton-Raphson method? Under what condition does this convergence degrade to linear?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The order of convergence of the Newton-Raphson method is **$p = 2$ (quadratic)** for a **simple root** $\alpha$ where $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$. (1.5 Marks)

The convergence degrades to **linear ($p = 1$)** when the method is applied near a **multiple root** — that is, a root of multiplicity $m \geq 2$ where $f(\alpha) = f'(\alpha) = f''(\alpha) = \ldots = 0$ up to order $m-1$. (1.5 Marks)

In such cases, the error recurrence becomes $e_{n+1} \approx C \cdot e_n$ instead of $e_{n+1} \approx C \cdot e_n^2$, because the leading term in the Taylor expansion of $f'(\alpha)$ vanishes, breaking the quadratic relationship.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

**(a)** Derive the Newton-Raphson iterative formula starting from the Taylor series expansion of $f(x)$ about $x_n$. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Newton-Raphson method, find the root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ correct to four decimal places, taking $x_0 = 2$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Write the Taylor series expansion of $f(x)$ about $x = x_n$:

$$f(x) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n) + \frac{f''(x_n)}{2!}(x - x_n)^2 + \frac{f'''(x_n)}{3!}(x - x_n)^3 + \ldots$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Let $x = \alpha$ (the true root), so $f(\alpha) = 0$. Truncate after the first-order term (assumption: $x_n$ is close to $\alpha$, so higher-order terms are negligible):

$$0 \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(\alpha - x_n)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** We approximate $\alpha$ by $x_{n+1}$ (the new iterate). Thus:

$$0 = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Solve for $x_{n+1}$:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}, \quad \text{provided } f'(x_n) \neq 0$$

**Assumptions stated (1 Mark):**
- $x_n$ is sufficiently close to the root $\alpha$ so that higher-order Taylor terms can be neglected.
- $f(x)$ is continuously differentiable in the neighborhood of the root.
- $f'(x_n) \neq 0$ at every iteration (no horizontal tangents).

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Setup:**
- $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$
- $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$
- Formula: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{x_n^3 - 2x_n - 5}{3x_n^2 - 2}$
- Initial guess: $x_0 = 2$, tolerance: $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$ (or 4 decimal places).

**Iteration 1:**
$$f(2) = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1, \quad f'(2) = 12 - 2 = 10$$
$$x_1 = 2 - \frac{-1}{10} = 2.1000$$
Error: $\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert = 0.1000$

**Iteration 2:**
$$f(2.1) = 9.261 - 4.2 - 5 = 0.061, \quad f'(2.1) = 13.23 - 2 = 11.23$$
$$x_2 = 2.1 - \frac{0.061}{11.23} = 2.1 - 0.00543 = 2.0946$$
Error: $\vert x_2 - x_1 \vert = 0.0054$

**Iteration 3:**
$$f(2.0946) = 9.1960 - 4.1892 - 5 = 0.0068, \quad f'(2.0946) = 13.1616 - 2 = 11.1616$$
$$x_3 = 2.0946 - \frac{0.0068}{11.1616} = 2.0946 - 0.0006 = 2.0940$$
Error: $\vert x_3 - x_2 \vert = 0.0006$

**Iteration 4:**
$$f(2.0940) \approx -0.0000\ldots, \quad f'(2.0940) \approx 11.155$$
$$x_4 \approx 2.0940 - \frac{\approx 0}{11.155} \approx 2.0940$$
Error: $\vert x_4 - x_3 \vert < 10^{-4}$ ✓

**Result (1 Mark):** The root correct to 4 decimal places is $\boxed{\alpha \approx 2.0946}$ (or $2.0940$ depending on truncation — final answer must be consistent with iteration shown).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam Style]

**(a)** Explain the geometric interpretation of the Newton-Raphson method with a neat sketch. Why is the method called an "open" method? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the Newton-Raphson method to find the positive root of $f(x) = \cos(x) - x e^{x} = 0$ with $x_0 = 0.5$ and tolerance $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$. Perform at least four iterations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Geometric Interpretation (5 Marks):**
