## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
Differentiate between the Gauss Elimination method and the Gauss-Jordan method. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Gauss Elimination | Gauss-Jordan |
|---|---|---|
| Final form | Upper triangular $[U \mid b']$ | Identity augmented $[I \mid x]$ |
| Back substitution | **Required** | **Not required** |
| Operation count | $\approx n^3 / 3$ FLOPs | $\approx n^3$ FLOPs |
| Computational efficiency | **Higher** (less arithmetic) | **Lower** (more arithmetic) |
| Ease of solution extraction | Requires careful reverse loop | Direct reading |

**Advantage / Disadvantage (1 mark each):**
- Gauss Elimination: *Advantage* — computationally cheaper; *Disadvantage* — needs separate back-substitution step.
- Gauss-Jordan: *Advantage* — solution obtained directly in one pass; *Disadvantage* — roughly **3× the arithmetic**, hence slower for large $n$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
What is **partial pivoting**? Why is it employed in the Gauss Elimination algorithm?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Partial pivoting is a row-interchange strategy in which, before performing the elimination at step $k$, the $k$-th row is swapped with a row $i$ ($i \geq k$) that has the **largest absolute value** in the $k$-th column. The pivot element becomes $a_{pk}^{(k-1)} = \max_{i \geq k} \vert a_{ik}^{(k-1)} \vert$.

**Reason for use (1.5 Marks):**
1. To **avoid division by zero** when the natural pivot $a_{kk} = 0$.
2. To **reduce round-off error amplification**: small pivots magnify numerical errors in subsequent subtractions. Choosing the largest available pivot minimises the magnitude of the multipliers $m_{ik}$, keeping intermediate values well-conditioned.
3. To improve the **stability and accuracy** of the computed solution in finite-precision arithmetic (e.g., IEEE 754 double precision, $\varepsilon \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE convention: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-parts typically carry **7 + 7 marks** distribution.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Gauss Elimination Focus

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Solve the following system of linear equations using the **Gauss Elimination method** with partial pivoting. Show all steps of forward elimination and back substitution. (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 + 2x_2 + 3x_3 &= 14 \\
2x_1 + x_2 + 2x_3 &= 10 \\
3x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 &= 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Compute the **determinant** of the coefficient matrix using the product of pivots. Also verify by direct expansion. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Gauss Elimination with partial pivoting (7 Marks):**

Augmented matrix:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 & \mid & 14 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 & \mid & 10 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 & \mid & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 1 — Pivot selection:* $\max(\vert 1 \vert, \vert 2 \vert, \vert 3 \vert) = 3$ in row 3. Swap $R_1 \leftrightarrow R_3$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 1 & \mid & 9 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 & \mid & 10 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & \mid & 14 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 2 — Eliminate column 1:*

Multipliers $m_{21} = 2/3$, $m_{31} = 1/3$.

$R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - \tfrac{2}{3} R_1$:

$$
(2-2, \; 1-\tfrac{4}{3}, \; 2-\tfrac{2}{3}, \; \mid \; 10-6) = (0, -\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{4}{3}, \mid 4)
$$

$R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - \tfrac{1}{3} R_1$:

$$
(1-1, \; 2-\tfrac{2}{3}, \; 3-\tfrac{1}{3}, \; \mid \; 14-3) = (0, \tfrac{4}{3}, \tfrac{8}{3}, \mid 11)
$$

Matrix after step 2:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 1 & \mid & 9 \\ 0 & -\tfrac{1}{3} & \tfrac{4}{3} & \mid & 4 \\ 0 & \tfrac{4}{3} & \tfrac{8}{3} & \mid & 11 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 3 — Pivot on row 2:* $\max(\vert -\tfrac{1}{3} \vert, \vert \tfrac{4}{3} \vert) = \tfrac{4}{3}$ in row 3. Swap $R_2 \leftrightarrow R_3$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 1 & \mid & 9 \\ 0 & \tfrac{4}{3} & \tfrac{8}{3} & \mid & 11 \\ 0 & -\tfrac{1}{3} & \tfrac{4}{3} & \mid & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplier $m_{32} = (-1/3)/(4/3) = -1/4$.

$R_3 \leftarrow R_3 + \tfrac{1}{4} R_2$:

$$
(0, \; 0, \; \tfrac{4}{3} + \tfrac{1}{4} \cdot \tfrac{8}{3}, \; \mid \; 4 + \tfrac{1}{4}(11)) = (0, 0, \tfrac{4}{3} + \tfrac{2}{3}, \mid 4 + \tfrac{11}{4}) = (0, 0, 2, \mid \tfrac{27}{4})
$$

Upper triangular matrix:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 1 & \mid & 9 \\ 0 & \tfrac{4}{3} & \tfrac{8}{3} & \mid & 11 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & \mid & \tfrac{27}{4} \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Back substitution:*

$$
x_3 = \frac{27/4}{2} = \frac{27}{8}
$$

$$
x_2 = \frac{11 - (8/3)(27/8)}{4/3} = \frac{11 - 9}{4/3} = \frac{2 \cdot 3}{4} = \frac{3}{2}
$$

$$
x_1 = \frac{9 - 2(3/2) - 1(27/8)}{3} = \frac{9 - 3 - 27/8}{3} = \frac{6 - 27/8}{3} = \frac{48/8 - 27/8}{3} = \frac{21/8}{3} = \frac{21}{24} = \frac{7}{8}
$$

$$
\boxed{x_1 = \frac{7}{8}, \quad x_2 = \frac{3}{2}, \quad x_3 = \frac{27}{8}}
$$

**(b) Determinant (7 Marks):**

By product of pivots (with row swap sign corrections):

Original $A$ before pivoting was:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

We performed **two row swaps** ($R_1 \leftrightarrow R_3$ and then $R_2 \leftrightarrow R_3$). Each swap multiplies the determinant by $-1$, so the net sign factor is $(-1)^2 = 1$.

The pivots (diagonal of final triangular matrix) are: $3, \tfrac{4}{3}, 2$.

$$
\det(A) = (+1) \cdot 3 \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot 2 = 8
$$

**Verification by cofactor expansion along row 1:**

$$
\det(A) = 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{vmatrix} - 2 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{vmatrix} + 3 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{vmatrix}
$$

$$
= 1 \cdot (1 - 4) - 2 \cdot (2 - 6) + 3 \cdot (4 - 3)
$$

$$
= 1 \cdot (-3) - 2 \cdot (-4) + 3 \cdot (1) = -3 + 8 + 3 = 8 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{\det(A) = 8}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Gauss-Jordan Focus

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Solve the following system using the **Gauss-Jordan method** with complete working. (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x_1 - 2x_2 + x_3 &= 7 \\
x_1 + x_2 - x_3 &= 1 \\
2x_1 - x_2 + 2x_3 &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Comment on the **numerical stability** of this system using the determinant and condition number concept. What is the practical implication? (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Gauss-Jordan method (7 Marks):**

Augmented matrix:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 & 1 & \mid & 7 \\ 1 & 1 & -1 & \mid & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & 2 & \mid & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 1:* Swap $R_1 \leftrightarrow R_2$ (pivot choice):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & -1 & \mid & 1 \\ 3 & -2 & 1 & \mid & 7 \\ 2 & -1 & 2 & \mid & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 2:* Eliminate column 1 in rows 2 and 3. $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - 3 R_1$, $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - 2 R_1$:

$$
R_2 = (0, -5, 4, \mid 4), \quad R_3 = (0, -3, 4, \mid 3)
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & -1 & \mid & 1 \\ 0 & -5 & 4 & \mid & 4 \\ 0 & -3 & 4 & \mid & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 3:* Normalize $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 / (-5)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & -1 & \mid & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -4/5 & \mid & -4/5 \\ 0 & -3 & 4 & \mid & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 4:* Eliminate column 2 in rows 1 and 3. $R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2$, $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 + 3 R_2$:

$$
R_1 = (1, 0, -1 + 4/5, \mid 1 + 4/5) = (1, 0, -1/5, \mid 9/5)
$$

$$
R_3 = (0, 0, 4 - 12/5, \mid 3 - 12/5) = (0, 0, 8/5, \mid 3/5)
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -1/5 & \mid & 9/5 \\ 0 & 1 & -4/5 & \mid & -4/5 \\ 0 & 0 & 8/5 & \mid & 3/5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 5:* Normalize $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 \cdot (5/8)$:

$$
R_3 = (0, 0, 1, \mid 3/8)
$$

*Step 6:* Eliminate column 3 in rows 1 and 2. $R_1 \leftarrow R_1 + (1/5) R_3$, $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 + (4/5) R_3$:

$$
R_1 = (1, 0, 0, \mid 9/5 + 3/40) = (1, 0, 0, \mid 75/40) = (1, 0, 0, \mid 15/8)
$$

$$
R_2 = (0, 1, 0, \mid -4/5 + 12/40) = (0, 1, 0, \mid -32/40 + 12/40) = (0, 1, 0, \mid -20/40) = (0, 1, 0, \mid -1/2)
$$

Final reduced matrix:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & \mid & 15/8 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \mid & -1/2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \mid & 3/8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Direct Solution:**

$$
\boxed{x_1 = \frac{15}{8}, \quad x_2 = -\frac{1}{2}, \quad x_3 = \frac{3}{8}}
$$

**(b) Numerical stability and condition number (7 Marks):**

**Determinant:**

$$
\det(A) = 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{vmatrix} - 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \end{vmatrix} + (-1) \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{vmatrix}
$$

$$
= 1 \cdot (-4 + 1) - 1 \cdot (6 - 2) - 1 \cdot (-3 + 4) = -3 - 4 - 1 = -8
$$

Wait — let us recompute the determinant of the *original* (un-pivoted) matrix:

$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = 3(2-1) - (-2)(2+2) + 1(-1-2) = 3 + 8 - 3 = 8
$$

After one row swap in Step 1 of part (a), the sign flipped. Pivots product = $1 \cdot (-5) \cdot (8/5) = -8$ (using pivots of the swapped matrix); with one swap sign correction, the original determinant is $\det(A) = 8$. **Since $\det(A) \neq 0$, the system is non-singular and has a unique solution.**

**Condition number (using 1-norm):**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Vert A \Vert_1 = \max_j \sum_i \vert a_{ij} \vert = \max(6, 4, 4) = 6
$$

Computing $A^{-1}$ by the adjugate formula:

$$
A^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(A)} \text{adj}(A), \quad \det(A) = 8
$$

$$
\text{adj}(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ -4 & 4 & 4 \\ -3 & -1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A^{-1} = \frac{1}{8}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ -4 & 4 & 4 \\ -3 & -1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\Vert A^{-1} \Vert_1 = \max_j \sum_i \vert a^{-1}_{ij} \vert \cdot (1/8) = \frac{1}{8}\max(8, 8, 10) = \frac{10}{8} = \frac{5}{4}
$$

$$
\kappa_1(A) = \Vert A \Vert_1 \cdot \Vert A^{-1} \Vert_1 = 6 \cdot \tfrac{5}{4} = \tfrac{30}{4} = 7.5
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\kappa_1(A) = 7.5$ is **small** (well below the ill-conditioned threshold of $\mathbf{10^6}$).
- The system is **well-conditioned**, and a relative perturbation in the input data of size $\varepsilon$ leads to a relative perturbation in the solution of approximately $7.5\,\varepsilon$.
- **Practical implication:** The Gauss-Jordan solution obtained is numerically reliable to nearly machine precision ($\sim 15$ significant digits in IEEE 754 double). No pivoting-induced rescaling is strictly mandatory, though partial pivoting is still recommended for round-off protection in larger systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Sign error in pivoting-based determinant** — forgetting to multiply by $(-1)^s$ where $s$ is the number of row swaps. (Lose up to 2 marks.)
> 2. **Confusing the pivot in the swapped matrix with the original pivot** when computing the determinant. Always track the swap count.
> 3. **Omitting the residual/verification step** in Gauss Elimination problems. (Lose 1 mark for missing verification.)
> 4. **Misapplying the back-substitution loop range** — ensure $i$ runs from $n$ down to $1$ with $j$ from $i+1$ to $n$.
> 5. **In Gauss-Jordan, forgetting to normalise the pivot row** before elimination — leading to a non-identity final form. (Lose up to 3 marks.)
> 6. **Not stating units / not commenting on physical meaning** in process engineering questions — KTU values the engineering interpretation, not just the number.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Gauss Elimination** = forward elimination to **upper triangular form** + back-substitution. Operation count $\approx n^3/3$.
- **Gauss-Jordan** = forward + backward elimination to **identity matrix**. Solution read directly. Operation count $\approx n^3$ (≈ 3× costlier).
- Both are **direct methods** — give exact solution in finite steps (in exact arithmetic).
- **Multipliers** $m_{ik} = a_{ik}^{(k-1)} / a_{kk}^{(k-1)}$ drive the row update $R_i \leftarrow R_i - m_{ik} R_k$.
- **Back substitution** formula: $x_i = \frac{1}{a_{ii}'}\left(b_i' - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij}' x_j\right)$.
- **Gauss-Jordan final form:** $[I \mid x]$.
- **Partial pivoting** is mandatory when $a_{kk} \approx 0$ or $\vert a_{kk} \vert$ is small relative to entries below. Swap with row of largest $\vert a_{ik} \vert$.
- **Determinant** is the product of pivots multiplied by $(-1)^s$ (number of row swaps).
- **Operation count** is the basis for the LU decomposition perspective: $A = LU$, with $L$ holding the multipliers and $U$ the upper triangular result.
- **Residual check** $r = Ax - b$ should be within tolerance $\varepsilon \sim 10^{-6}$ for engineering work.
- **Condition number** $\kappa(A)$ governs stability: $\kappa \sim 1$ ⇒ well-conditioned; $\kappa \gg 1$ ⇒ ill-conditioned.
- **Engineering applications** include distillation MESH, pipe network analysis, reactor balances, state-space control, and linearised process models.
- **Common mistakes:** (a) skipping sign correction in determinant; (b) missing row swap count; (c) forgetting residual verification; (d) failing to normalise pivot row in Gauss-Jordan.
- **Remember:** Direct methods are preferred over iterative methods (Gauss-Seidel, Jacobi) when the matrix is small-to-medium sized and well-conditioned. For large sparse systems, iterative methods typically dominate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crout's Reduction Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crout's Reduction Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Crout's Reduction Method** is a direct (non-iterative) numerical technique used to solve a system of $n$ simultaneous linear algebraic equations in $n$ unknowns, $\mathbf{Ax = b}$, by first decomposing the coefficient matrix $\mathbf{A}$ into a product of a **lower triangular matrix $\mathbf{L}$** and a **unit upper triangular matrix $\mathbf{U}$** (i.e., a matrix whose diagonal entries are all equal to $\mathbf{1}$). Once $\mathbf{A = LU}$ is established, the system is solved in two efficient substitution stages: **forward substitution** to solve $\mathbf{Ly = b}$, followed by **backward substitution** to solve $\mathbf{Ux = y}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Doolittle vs Crout):**
> - In **Doolittle's** method: $\mathbf{L}$ is unit lower triangular ($l_{ii}=1$) and $\mathbf{U}$ is general upper triangular.
> - In **Crout's** method: $\mathbf{L}$ is general lower triangular (diagonal entries $l_{ii} \neq 1$) and $\mathbf{U}$ is unit upper triangular ($u_{ii}=1$).
> This is a frequent **short-answer question** in KTU exams.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Locker and Key Analogy 🔐**
> Imagine you want to open a complicated $3$-combination lock (the vector $\mathbf{x}$). The combination lock has three interlocking discs where each disc's rotation depends on the previous one. You could try every combination blindly (this is the *Gaussian Elimination* approach, slow and messy).
>
> **Crout's method**, however, separates the problem into two simpler locks:
> - **First Lock (Lower L):** A "forward-looking" lock where disc 1 turns first, then disc 2, then disc 3 — each depending only on the *previous* ones. This is the **forward substitution**.
> - **Second Lock (Upper U):** A "backward-looking" lock where disc 3 turns first, then disc 2, then disc 1 — each depending only on the *later* ones. This is the **backward substitution**.
>
> Once you've broken the lock into two simpler ones ($\mathbf{L}$ and $\mathbf{U}$), solving becomes systematic and **reusable** — if you change only the right-hand side $\mathbf{b}$ (say, a new load condition in a process plant), you don't have to re-factor the matrix!

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

In process engineering, Crout's method is the engine behind solving:
- **Steady-state material and energy balances** in chemical plants.
- **Pipe network flow problems** (Hardy-Cross linearized form).
- **Heat exchanger rating problems** (LMTD discretized systems).
- **Reaction kinetics** in multiple CSTRs simultaneously.

The decomposition $\mathbf{A = LU}$ is computed **once**, and for **multiple load vectors $\mathbf{b}$** (e.g., different feed compositions), only the cheap substitution steps are repeated — saving massive computational cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Decomposition

Given the system $\mathbf{Ax = b}$ of order $n$, we seek matrices $\mathbf{L}$ and $\mathbf{U}$ such that:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{U}
$$

where:

$$
\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{11} & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ l_{21} & l_{22} & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ l_{31} & l_{32} & l_{33} & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ l_{n1} & l_{n2} & l_{n3} & \cdots & l_{nn} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & u_{12} & u_{13} & \cdots & u_{1n} \\ 0 & 1 & u_{23} & \cdots & u_{2n} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & u_{3n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that $\mathbf{L}$ has **$n(n+1)/2$** unknowns (full lower triangle) and $\mathbf{U}$ has **$n(n-1)/2$** unknowns (strict upper triangle, diagonals fixed at $1$). Total unknowns = $n^2$, exactly matching the $n^2$ equations in $\mathbf{A = LU}$. The system is **square, linear, and well-posed** when $l_{ii} \neq 0$ for all $i$.

## 2.2 The Recursive Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

By equating the product $\mathbf{LU}$ element-by-element with $\mathbf{A}$, the following compact recurrence relations emerge:

### Case 1: Diagonal entries of $\mathbf{L}$ (computed first)

$$
l_{ii} = a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} \, u_{ki}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

### Case 2: Off-diagonal entries of $\mathbf{U}$ (strict upper triangle)

$$
u_{ij} = \frac{a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} \, u_{kj}}{l_{ii}}, \quad 1 \le i < j \le n
$$

### Case 3: Off-diagonal entries of $\mathbf{L}$ (strict lower triangle)

$$
l_{ij} = a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{ik} \, u_{kj}, \quad 1 \le j < i \le n
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of evaluation in Crout's method:**
> 1. First, the first **row of $\mathbf{U}$** and first **column of $\mathbf{L}$** are computed.
> 2. Then the second diagonal element $l_{22}$ is computed.
> 3. Then the second row of $\mathbf{U}$ and second column of $\mathbf{L}$ are completed.
> 4. This "row-by-row" sweep continues.

## 2.3 The Two Substitution Stages

Once $\mathbf{L}$ and $\mathbf{U}$ are known, the original system $\mathbf{Ax = b}$ becomes $\mathbf{LUx = b}$. Let $\mathbf{y = Ux}$, then:

### Stage I — Forward Substitution (solve $\mathbf{Ly = b}$)

$$
y_i = \frac{1}{l_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} \, y_k \right), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

### Stage II — Backward Substitution (solve $\mathbf{Ux = y}$)

$$
x_i = y_i - \sum_{k=i+1}^{n} u_{ik} \, x_k, \quad i = n, n-1, \ldots, 1
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete formula toolkit** required for any Crout's method problem in the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination.

| Step | Formula | Range / Constraint | Purpose |
|------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| First column of $\mathbf{L}$ | $l_{i1} = a_{i1}$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ | $i \ge 1$ | Seed the decomposition |
| First row of $\mathbf{U}$ | $u_{1j} = a_{1j}/l_{11}$ for $j = 2, 3, \ldots, n$ | $l_{11} \neq 0$ | Seed the decomposition |
| General diagonal of $\mathbf{L}$ | $l_{ii} = a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} u_{ki}$ | $i = 2, 3, \ldots, n$ | Compute diagonal |
| General $u_{ij}$ (above diag.) | $u_{ij} = \left( a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} u_{kj} \right) / l_{ii}$ | $i < j$ | Compute upper off-diagonal |
| General $l_{ij}$ (below diag.) | $l_{ij} = a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{ik} u_{kj}$ | $i > j$ | Compute lower off-diagonal |
| Forward substitution | $y_i = \left( b_i - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} y_k \right) / l_{ii}$ | $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ | Solve $\mathbf{Ly = b}$ |
| Backward substitution | $x_i = y_i - \sum_{k=i+1}^{n} u_{ik} x_k$ | $i = n, n-1, \ldots, 1$ | Solve $\mathbf{Ux = y}$ |
| Final answer check | $\mathbf{Ax - b \approx 0}$ | — | Verification |
| Operation count | $\approx \frac{n^3}{3}$ multiplications/divisions | Same as Gauss elim. | Computational cost |
| Existence condition | All pivots $l_{ii} \neq 0$ | — | Validity |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** In process simulation software like **Aspen Plus** or **MATLAB's `\` operator**, the actual workhorse is exactly this LU factorization. Whenever you see "matrix left-divided" in MATLAB, an LU decomposition is happening behind the scenes!

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Crout's Recurrence from First Principles

Starting from the matrix equation $\mathbf{A = LU}$ and writing out the $(i,j)$-th element:

$$
a_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{\min(i,j)} l_{ik} \, u_{kj}
$$

### Why this sum goes up to $\min(i,j)$?

- If $i < j$ (upper triangle of $\mathbf{A}$), then $l_{ik}$ has a non-zero range only for $k \le i$ (lower triangle of $\mathbf{L}$), so the sum stops at $k = i$.
- If $i > j$ (lower triangle of $\mathbf{A}$), then $u_{kj}$ has a non-zero range only for $k \le j$ (since the diagonal of $\mathbf{U}$ is $1$, but the strict upper triangle exists), so the sum stops at $k = j$.
- If $i = j$ (diagonal), the sum goes up to $k = i = j$.

### Case A: When $i \le j$ (Computing $u_{ij}$)

Separating the last term $k = i$ from the rest:

$$
a_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} \, u_{kj} + l_{ii} \, u_{ij}
$$

Solving for $u_{ij}$ (since the $k = i$ term contains $u_{ij}$):

$$
u_{ij} = \frac{1}{l_{ii}}\left( a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} \, u_{kj} \right), \quad i < j
$$

For $i = 1$, the sum is empty (vacuous), so $u_{1j} = a_{1j}/l_{11}$.

### Case B: When $i > j$ (Computing $l_{ij}$)

Now we separate the last term $k = j$:

$$
a_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{ik} \, u_{kj} + l_{ij} \, u_{jj}
$$

Since $u_{jj} = 1$ (unit upper triangular), this simplifies to:

$$
l_{ij} = a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{ik} \, u_{kj}, \quad i > j
$$

For $j = 1$, the sum is empty, so $l_{i1} = a_{i1}$.

### Case C: Diagonal of $\mathbf{L}$ (Setting $i = j$ in Case B)

$$
l_{ii} = a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} \, u_{ki}
$$

This completes the full derivation. ✓

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example: Full 3×3 Crout's Reduction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Solve the following system using Crout's reduction method:
> $$x_1 + 2x_2 + 3x_3 = 14$$
> $$2x_1 + 5x_2 + 8x_3 = 30$$
> $$3x_1 + 8x_2 + 14x_3 = 53$$

The augmented data is $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 & 8 \\ 3 & 8 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 30 \\ 53 \end{bmatrix}$.

### Step 1: Decompose $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{LU}$

Let us write $\mathbf{L}$ and $\mathbf{U}$ as:

$$
\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{11} & 0 & 0 \\ l_{21} & l_{22} & 0 \\ l_{31} & l_{32} & l_{33} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & u_{12} & u_{13} \\ 0 & 1 & u_{23} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

There are **9 unknowns** in total: $l_{11}, l_{21}, l_{22}, l_{31}, l_{32}, l_{33}, u_{12}, u_{13}, u_{23}$.

### Step 1a: First column of $\mathbf{L}$ (using $l_{i1} = a_{i1}$)

$$
l_{11} = a_{11} = 1, \quad l_{21} = a_{21} = 2, \quad l_{31} = a_{31} = 3
$$

### Step 1b: First row of $\mathbf{U}$ (using $u_{1j} = a_{1j}/l_{11}$)

$$
u_{12} = \frac{a_{12}}{l_{11}} = \frac{2}{1} = 2, \quad u_{13} = \frac{a_{13}}{l_{11}} = \frac{3}{1} = 3
$$

### Step 1c: Diagonal entry $l_{22}$ (using $l_{ii} = a_{ii} - \sum l_{ik} u_{ki}$)

$$
l_{22} = a_{22} - l_{21} \, u_{12} = 5 - (2)(2) = 5 - 4 = 1
$$

### Step 1d: Off-diagonal $u_{23}$ (using $u_{ij}$ formula with $i=2, j=3$)

$$
u_{23} = \frac{a_{23} - l_{21} \, u_{13}}{l_{22}} = \frac{8 - (2)(3)}{1} = \frac{8 - 6}{1} = 2
$$

### Step 1e: Off-diagonal $l_{32}$ (using $l_{ij}$ formula with $i=3, j=2$)

$$
l_{32} = a_{32} - l_{31} \, u_{12} = 8 - (3)(2) = 8 - 6 = 2
$$

### Step 1f: Final diagonal $l_{33}$

$$
l_{33} = a_{33} - l_{31} \, u_{13} - l_{32} \, u_{23} = 14 - (3)(3) - (2)(2) = 14 - 9 - 4 = 1
$$

### Step 1g: Assemble the matrices

$$
\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1h: Verify the decomposition $\mathbf{LU} = \mathbf{A}$

Computing the product:

$$
\mathbf{LU} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 1 & 1\cdot 2 & 1\cdot 3 \\ 2\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 0 & 2\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1 & 2\cdot 3 + 1\cdot 2 \\ 3\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 0 + 1\cdot 0 & 3\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 0 & 3\cdot 3 + 2\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 & 8 \\ 3 & 8 & 14 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A} \checkmark
$$

### Step 2: Forward Substitution — Solve $\mathbf{Ly = b}$

The system $\mathbf{Ly = b}$ expands as:

$$
y_1 = 14
$$
$$
2 y_1 + y_2 = 30
$$
$$
3 y_1 + 2 y_2 + y_3 = 53
$$

Solving sequentially:

**From Row 1:**

$$
y_1 = \frac{b_1}{l_{11}} = \frac{14}{1} = 14
$$

**From Row 2:**

$$
y_2 = \frac{b_2 - l_{21} \, y_1}{l_{22}} = \frac{30 - (2)(14)}{1} = \frac{30 - 28}{1} = 2
$$

**From Row 3:**

$$
y_3 = \frac{b_3 - l_{31} \, y_1 - l_{32} \, y_2}{l_{33}} = \frac{53 - (3)(14) - (2)(2)}{1} = \frac{53 - 42 - 4}{1} = 7
$$

Therefore, $\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 2 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}$.

### Step 3: Backward Substitution — Solve $\mathbf{Ux = y}$

The system $\mathbf{Ux = y}$ expands as:

$$
x_1 + 2 x_2 + 3 x_3 = 14
$$
$$
x_2 + 2 x_3 = 2
$$
$$
x_3 = 7
$$

Solving from the bottom up:

**From Row 3:**

$$
x_3 = y_3 = 7
$$

**From Row 2:**

$$
x_2 = y_2 - u_{23} \, x_3 = 2 - (2)(7) = 2 - 14 = -12
$$

**From Row 1:**

$$
x_1 = y_1 - u_{12} \, x_2 - u_{13} \, x_3 = 14 - (2)(-12) - (3)(7) = 14 + 24 - 21 = 17
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;x_1 = 17, \quad x_2 = -12, \quad x_3 = 7\;}
$$

### Step 4: Verification (Mandatory in KTU valuation)

Substituting back into the original system:

**Equation 1:** $(1)(17) + (2)(-12) + (3)(7) = 17 - 24 + 21 = 14$ ✓
**Equation 2:** $(2)(17) + (5)(-12) + (8)(7) = 34 - 60 + 56 = 30$ ✓
**Equation 3:** $(3)(17) + (8)(-12) + (14)(7) = 51 - 96 + 98 = 53$ ✓

All three equations are satisfied exactly, confirming the solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Algorithmic Pipeline of Crout's Method

The following diagram captures the **block-level functional architecture** of the Crout's reduction procedure, mapping every interaction between the $\mathbf{L}$, $\mathbf{U}$, and the right-hand-side vector $\mathbf{b}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Matrix A and vector b] --> B[Initialize: Set L lower triangular, U unit upper triangular]
    B --> C[Compute First Column of L: l_i1 = a_i1 for i=1 to n]
    C --> D[Compute First Row of U: u_1j = a_1j divided by l_11]
    D --> E[Loop i from 2 to n]
    E --> F[Compute diagonal l_ii = a_ii minus sum of l_ik times u_ki]
    F --> G[Compute Row i of U: u_ij for j greater than i]
    G --> H[Compute Column i of L: l_ji for j greater than i]
    H --> I{Is i equal to n?}
    I -->|No| E
    I -->|Yes| J[Decomposition Complete: L and U matrices ready]
    J --> K[Stage 1: Forward Substitution: Solve L y equals b]
    K --> L[Compute y_i from b_i minus sum of l_ik times y_k, divide by l_ii]
    L --> M[Stage 2: Backward Substitution: Solve U x equals y]
    M --> N[Compute x_i from y_i minus sum of u_ik times x_k, start from i=n]
    N --> O[Output: Solution vector x]
    O --> P[Verification: Substitute Ax equals b]
    P --> Q[End]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style J fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style K fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C
    style M fill:#FDEDEC,stroke:#D62728
    style O fill:#F4ECF7,stroke:#8E44AD
    style Q fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Two-Stage Substitution Pipeline

This diagram visualizes how the intermediate vector $\mathbf{y}$ acts as a **bridge** between the lower and upper triangular systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageA[Stage I: Forward Substitution]
        A1[L matrix - lower triangular] --> A2[Ly equals b]
        A2 --> A3[Compute y1 from b1 divided by l_11]
        A3 --> A4[Compute y2 from b2 and l_21 times y1]
        A4 --> A5[Compute y3 from b3 and l_31 times y1 plus l_32 times y2]
        A5 --> A6[Intermediate vector y]
    end

    subgraph StageB[Stage II: Backward Substitution]
        B1[U matrix - unit upper triangular] --> B2[Ux equals y]
        B2 --> B3[Compute x_n from y_n]
        B3 --> B4[Compute x_n-1 from y_n-1 and u_n-1,n times x_n]
        B4 --> B5[Compute x_n-2 from y_n-2 and u_n-2,n-1 times x_n-1 plus u_n-2,n times x_n]
        B5 --> B6[Final solution vector x]
    end

    A6 --> B1
    B6 --> C[Output: x is the solution to Ax equals b]

    style StageA fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#27AE60
    style StageB fill:#FDEDEC,stroke:#C0392B
    style C fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#F39C12
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Element-wise Dependency Map for a 3×3 System

This diagram shows the **dependency tree** — which $l_{ij}$ or $u_{ij}$ value depends on which previously-computed values.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L11[l_11 = a_11] --> U12[u_12 = a_12 divided by l_11]
    L11 --> U13[u_13 = a_13 divided by l_11]
    L21[l_21 = a_21] --> L22[l_22 = a_22 minus l_21 times u_12]
    L21 --> U23[u_23 = a_23 minus l_21 times u_13, divided by l_22]
    L21 --> U13
    L21 --> U12
    L31[l_31 = a_31] --> L32[l_32 = a_32 minus l_31 times u_12]
    L31 --> L33[l_33 = a_33 minus l_31 times u_13 minus l_32 times u_23]
    L31 --> U13
    L31 --> U12
    L22 --> L33
    L32 --> L33
    U12 --> L32
    U13 --> L33
    U23 --> L33

    style L11 fill:#D6EAF8
    style L21 fill:#D6EAF8
    style L31 fill:#D6EAF8
    style L22 fill:#D5F5E3
    style L32 fill:#D5F5E3
    style L33 fill:#D5F5E3
    style U12 fill:#FADBD8
    style U13 fill:#FADBD8
    style U23 fill:#FADBD8
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the dependency map:** The blue nodes are "seeds" (column 1 of $\mathbf{L}$), the green nodes are the rest of $\mathbf{L}$, and the red nodes are the non-trivial entries of $\mathbf{U}$. Arrows show that computation must follow the arrow direction (the target depends on the source). This is precisely the order in which students should compute the values during the KTU exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** What is **LU decomposition** in the context of solving a system of linear equations? How does **Crout's method** differ from **Doolittle's method** in the choice of matrices $\mathbf{L}$ and $\mathbf{U}$?

**Model Answer:**

**LU Decomposition (Definition):** LU decomposition is a procedure of factorizing a given square matrix $\mathbf{A}$ into the product of a lower triangular matrix $\mathbf{L}$ and an upper triangular matrix $\mathbf{U}$, such that $\mathbf{A = LU}$. The original system $\mathbf{Ax = b}$ is then solved in two stages using the substitutions $\mathbf{Ly = b}$ followed by $\mathbf{Ux = y}$, both of which are computationally cheap triangular solves.

**Difference between Crout's and Doolittle's method:**

| Aspect | Doolittle's Method | Crout's Method |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------|
| $\mathbf{L}$ diagonal | $l_{ii} = 1$ (unit) | $l_{ii} \neq 1$ (general) |
| $\mathbf{U}$ diagonal | $u_{ii} \neq 1$ (general) | $u_{ii} = 1$ (unit) |
| Number of unknowns in $\mathbf{L}$ | $n(n-1)/2$ | $n(n+1)/2$ |
| Number of unknowns in $\mathbf{U}$ | $n(n+1)/2$ | $n(n-1)/2$ |

*(Award 1 mark for LU definition, 1 mark for stating the diagonals correctly, 1 mark for the count or implication.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State the **Crout's method formulas** for:
  (a) the diagonal elements $l_{ii}$ of $\mathbf{L}$
  (b) the strict upper triangular elements $u_{ij}$ of $\mathbf{U}$ (for $i < j$)
  (c) the strict lower triangular elements $l_{ij}$ of $\mathbf{L}$ (for $i > j$)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Diagonal of $\mathbf{L}$:**

$$
l_{ii} = a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} \, u_{ki}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

**(b) Strict upper triangle of $\mathbf{U}$:**

$$
u_{ij} = \frac{1}{l_{ii}} \left( a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} \, u_{kj} \right), \quad 1 \le i < j \le n
$$

**(c) Strict lower triangle of $\mathbf{L}$:**

$$
l_{ij} = a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{ik} \, u_{kj}, \quad 1 \le j < i \le n
$$

*(Award 1 mark per correct formula with proper summation limits.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Solve the following system of linear equations using **Crout's reduction method**:
$$
3 x_1 + 2 x_2 + x_3 = 10
$$
$$
2 x_1 + 3 x_2 + 2 x_3 = 14
$$
$$
x_1 + 2 x_2 + 3 x_3 = 14
$$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Perform the **LU Decomposition** using Crout's method

**Solution:**

The coefficient matrix and RHS are:
$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 14 \\ 14 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Let $\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{11} & 0 & 0 \\ l_{21} & l_{22} & 0 \\ l_{31} & l_{32} & l_{33} \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & u_{12} & u_{13} \\ 0 & 1 & u_{23} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**First column of $\mathbf{L}$** (using $l_{i1} = a_{i1}$):

$$
l_{11} = 3, \quad l_{21} = 2, \quad l_{31} = 1
$$

**First row of $\mathbf{U}$** (using $u_{1j} = a_{1j}/l_{11}$):

$$
u_{12} = \frac{2}{3}, \quad u_{13} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Diagonal $l_{22}$** (using $l_{22} = a_{22} - l_{21} u_{12}$):

$$
l_{22} = 3 - (2) \left( \frac{2}{3} \right) = 3 - \frac{4}{3} = \frac{9 - 4}{3} = \frac{5}{3}
$$

**Element $u_{23}$** (using $u_{23} = (a_{23} - l_{21} u_{13}) / l_{22}$):

$$
u_{23} = \frac{2 - (2)(1/3)}{5/3} = \frac{2 - 2/3}{5/3} = \frac{4/3}{5/3} = \frac{4}{5}
$$

**Element $l_{32}$** (using $l_{32} = a_{32} - l_{31} u_{12}$):

$$
l_{32} = 2 - (1)\left( \frac{2}{3} \right) = 2 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{4}{3}
$$

**Element $l_{33}$** (using $l_{33} = a_{33} - l_{31} u_{13} - l_{32} u_{23}$):

$$
l_{33} = 3 - (1)\left( \frac{1}{3} \right) - \left( \frac{4}{3} \right)\left( \frac{4}{5} \right) = 3 - \frac{1}{3} - \frac{16}{15}
$$

Converting to a common denominator (15):
$$
l_{33} = \frac{45}{15} - \frac{5}{15} - \frac{16}{15} = \frac{45 - 5 - 16}{15} = \frac{24}{15} = \frac{8}{5}
$$

**Assembled matrices:**

$$
\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 5/3 & 0 \\ 1 & 4/3 & 8/5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2/3 & 1/3 \\ 0 & 1 & 4/5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Solve for $\mathbf{x}$ via **Forward + Backward Substitution**

**Stage I — Forward Substitution ($\mathbf{Ly = b}$):**

**Row 1:**
$$
y_1 = \frac{b_1}{l_{11}} = \frac{10}{3}
$$

**Row 2:**
$$
y_2 = \frac{b_2 - l_{21} y_1}{l_{22}} = \frac{14 - (2)(10/3)}{5/3} = \frac{14 - 20/3}{5/3} = \frac{42/3 - 20/3}{5/3} = \frac{22/3}{5/3} = \frac{22}{5}
$$

**Row 3:**
$$
y_3 = \frac{b_3 - l_{31} y_1 - l_{32} y_2}{l_{33}} = \frac{14 - (1)(10/3) - (4/3)(22/5)}{8/5}
$$

Simplify the numerator:
$$
14 - \frac{10}{3} - \frac{88}{15} = \frac{210}{15} - \frac{50}{15} - \frac{88}{15} = \frac{72}{15} = \frac{24}{5}
$$

Therefore:
$$
y_3 = \frac{24/5}{8/5} = \frac{24}{5} \times \frac{5}{8} = 3
$$

**Stage II — Backward Substitution ($\mathbf{Ux = y}$):**

**Row 3:**
$$
x_3 = y_3 = 3
$$

**Row 2:**
$$
x_2 = y_2 - u_{23} x_3 = \frac{22}{5} - \frac{4}{5}(3) = \frac{22}{5} - \frac{12}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2
$$

**Row 1:**
$$
x_1 = y_1 - u_{12} x_2 - u_{13} x_3 = \frac{10}{3} - \frac{2}{3}(2) - \frac{1}{3}(3) = \frac{10}{3} - \frac{4}{3} - 1 = \frac{10 - 4 - 3}{3} = \frac{3}{3} = 1
$$

**Verification:**
- Eq.1: $3(1) + 2(2) + 1(3) = 3 + 4 + 3 = 10$ ✓
- Eq.2: $2(1) + 3(2) + 2(3) = 2 + 6 + 6 = 14$ ✓
- Eq.3: $1(1) + 2(2) + 3(3) = 1 + 4 + 9 = 14$ ✓

$$
\boxed{\;x_1 = 1, \quad x_2 = 2, \quad x_3 = 3\;}
$$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A heat-exchanger network in a process plant is governed by the linear system:
$$
2 x_1 + x_2 - x_3 = 8
$$
$$
3 x_1 + 2 x_2 + 2 x_3 = 17
$$
$$
x_1 - x_2 + 3 x_3 = 8
$$
Solve this system using **Crout's reduction method**, and verify the result.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Decomposition of $\mathbf{A}$ into $\mathbf{LU}$

The matrix and vector are:
$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 2 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 17 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**First column of $\mathbf{L}$:**
$$
l_{11} = 2, \quad l_{21} = 3, \quad l_{31} = 1
$$

**First row of $\mathbf{U}$:**
$$
u_{12} = \frac{1}{2}, \quad u_{13} = \frac{-1}{2}
$$

**Diagonal $l_{22}$:**
$$
l_{22} = a_{22} - l_{21} u_{12} = 2 - (3)\left( \frac{1}{2} \right) = 2 - \frac{3}{2} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Element $u_{23}$:**
$$
u_{23} = \frac{a_{23} - l_{21} u_{13}}{l_{22}} = \frac{2 - (3)(-1/2)}{1/2} = \frac{2 + 3/2}{1/2} = \frac{7/2}{1/2} = 7
$$

**Element $l_{32}$:**
$$
l_{32} = a_{32} - l_{31} u_{12} = -1 - (1)\left( \frac{1}{2} \right) = -1 - \frac{1}{2} = -\frac{3}{2}
$$

**Element $l_{33}$:**
$$
l_{33} = a_{33} - l_{31} u_{13} - l_{32} u_{23} = 3 - (1)\left( -\frac{1}{2} \right) - \left( -\frac{3}{2} \right)(7) = 3 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{21}{2} = 3 + \frac{22}{2} = 3 + 11 = 14
$$

**Assembled matrices:**

$$
\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 1/2 & 0 \\ 1 & -3/2 & 14 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1/2 & -1/2 \\ 0 & 1 & 7 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Substitution Stages

**Forward Substitution ($\mathbf{Ly = b}$):**

**Row 1:**
$$
y_1 = \frac{8}{2} = 4
$$

**Row 2:**
$$
y_2 = \frac{17 - 3(4)}{1/2} = \frac{17 - 12}{1/2} = \frac{5}{1/2} = 10
$$

**Row 3:**
$$
y_3 = \frac{8 - 1(4) - (-3/2)(10)}{14} = \frac{8 - 4 + 15}{14} = \frac{19}{14}
$$

**Backward Substitution ($\mathbf{Ux = y}$):**

**Row 3:**
$$
x_3 = \frac{19}{14}
$$

**Row 2:**
$$
x_2 = y_2 - u_{23} x_3 = 10 - 7 \cdot \frac{19}{14} = 10 - \frac{19}{2} = \frac{20 - 19}{2} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Row 1:**
$$
x_1 = y_1 - u_{12} x_2 - u_{13} x_3 = 4 - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} - \left( -\frac{1}{2} \right) \cdot \frac{19}{14} = 4 - \frac{1}{4} + \frac{19}{28}
$$

Converting to denominator 28:
$$
x_1 = \frac{112}{28} - \frac{7}{28} + \frac{19}{28} = \frac{112 - 7 + 19}{28} = \frac{124}{28} = \frac{31}{7}
$$

**Verification:**
- Eq.1: $2(31/7) + 1(1/2) - 1(19/14) = 62/7 + 1/2 - 19/14 = 124/14 + 7/14 - 19/14 = 112/14 = 8$ ✓
- Eq.2: $3(31/7) + 2(1/2) + 2(19/14) = 93/7 + 1 + 19/7 = 112/7 + 7/7 = 119/7 = 17$ ✓
- Eq.3: $1(31/7) - 1(1/2) + 3(19/14) = 62/14 - 7/14 + 57/14 = 112/14 = 8$ ✓

$$
\boxed{\;x_1 = \frac{31}{7}, \quad x_2 = \frac{1}{2}, \quad x_3 = \frac{19}{14}\;}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls ⚠️**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit-diagonal constraint of $\mathbf{U}$:** A common mistake is computing $u_{ii}$ values instead of fixing them at $1$. This shifts the equations and leads to **completely wrong $l_{ij}$ values**. Always remember: in Crout's, $u_{ii} = 1$ is a *constraint*, not a value to compute.
>
> 2. **Mixing up summation limits:** For $u_{ij}$ (with $i<j$), the sum is over $k = 1$ to $i-1$. For $l_{ij}$ (with $i>j$), the sum is over $k = 1$ to $j-1$. Students frequently use the wrong limit, losing **3–4 marks** per such error.
>
> 3. **Skipping verification:** KTU's 2024 scheme explicitly awards **2 marks for verification** (substituting $\mathbf{x}$ back into all three equations). Skipping this is a guaranteed mark loss.
>
> 4. **Arithmetic slip in subtraction of fractions:** When $l_{ii}$ values are fractions, students often make sign errors or LCM mistakes. Always show the common-denominator step explicitly.
>
> 5. **Not labeling $\mathbf{L}$ and $\mathbf{U}$ as unit/general:** Examiners deduct **1 mark** if you don't state which matrix has unit diagonal in your solution.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Crout's Method** decomposes $\mathbf{A = LU}$ where $\mathbf{U}$ has **unit diagonal** ($u_{ii} = 1$) and $\mathbf{L}$ has **general diagonal** ($l_{ii} \neq 1$).
- ✅ **The first column of $\mathbf{L}$** is a direct copy of the first column of $\mathbf{A}$: $l_{i1} = a_{i1}$.
- ✅ **The first row of $\mathbf{U}$** is the first row of $\mathbf{A}$ divided by $l_{11}$: $u_{1j} = a_{1j}/l_{11}$.
- ✅ **Diagonal of $\mathbf{L}$:** $l_{ii} = a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} u_{ki}$ — *the only non-trivial $l_{ii}$*.
- ✅ **Upper off-diagonal of $\mathbf{U}$:** $u_{ij} = (a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} u_{kj})/l_{ii}$, with $i < j$.
- ✅ **Lower off-diagonal of $\mathbf{L}$:** $l_{ij} = a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{ik} u_{kj}$, with $i > j$.
- ✅ **Two-stage solve:** $\mathbf{Ly = b}$ (forward sub) $\rightarrow$ $\mathbf{Ux = y}$ (backward sub).
- ✅ **Forward substitution formula:** $y_i = (b_i - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik} y_k)/l_{ii}$.
- ✅ **Backward substitution formula:** $x_i = y_i - \sum_{k=i+1}^{n} u_{ik} x_k$ (start from $i = n$).
- ✅ **Existence:** Method fails if any $l_{ii} = 0$ (pivoting is required in such cases).
- ✅ **Cost:** $\approx n^3/3$ operations — same as Gauss elimination, but the $\mathbf{LU}$ is reusable.
- ✅ **Verification** is **mandatory** in KTU valuation — always substitute the solution back.
- ✅ **Key difference vs Doolittle's:** In Doolittle, $\mathbf{L}$ is unit; in Crout, $\mathbf{U}$ is unit. **Memorize this swap!**
- ✅ **Industrial application:** Solving multiple load-vector systems (e.g., different feed compositions in CSTR networks) where $\mathbf{A}$ is constant but $\mathbf{b}$ varies.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Error Analysis** is the systematic study and quantification of the discrepancies (errors) that arise between the *exact mathematical answer* of a problem and the *approximate numerical value* obtained through computational/numerical methods. In the context of **PECHT526**, error analysis forms the foundational pillar upon which the reliability, accuracy, and validity of all subsequent numerical techniques (root-finding, integration, ODE solving) rest.

In numerical computation, the true value of a physical or mathematical quantity is almost never obtainable in a finite number of steps using a digital computer. Every numerical algorithm therefore produces an *approximation*, and the gap between this approximation and the truth is termed the **numerical error**.

### 1.2 Two Principal Sources of Numerical Error

| Source | Definition | When It Originates |
|---|---|---|
| **Truncation Error** | The error introduced when an infinite mathematical process is replaced by a finite one (e.g., replacing a Taylor series by a finite number of terms). | During the *mathematical modeling/derivation* phase. |
| **Round-off Error** | The error introduced because computers store real numbers using a *finite number of digits* (finite precision of floating-point arithmetic). | During the *arithmetic/computation* phase on the machine. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Carpenter and the Measuring Tape":**
> Imagine a carpenter measuring a wooden plank. The *true length* of the plank is, say, **3.1415926535... m**. However, the carpenter has two sources of error:
> 1. **Truncation Error** — He uses a tape that only has markings up to **3 decimal places** (i.e., he truncates π ≈ 3.141). This is analogous to a *Taylor series* that is cut off after a few terms.
> 2. **Round-off Error** — Even if his tape shows 3.141, his pencil is thick and he may read it as 3.142. This is analogous to a computer *rounding* the last stored digit.
>
> Error Analysis is the carpenter's discipline of *quantifying* how much these two imperfections affect the final shelf he builds. A KTU student must learn to estimate the *maximum allowable* error, just as an engineer must specify the *tolerance* on a manufactured part.

## 1.4 Why Error Analysis is Critical in Process Engineering

In chemical process engineering, virtually every calculation (heat-transfer coefficient, reaction rate, equilibrium conversion, distillation tray efficiency) involves iterative numerical methods. A small error in the iteration can cascade into a large error in plant design — potentially causing equipment failure, safety hazards, or massive economic loss. Thus, **error analysis is non-negotiable** in KTU's outcome-based curriculum (aligned with CO1: *Apply numerical methods to solve process engineering problems with awareness of error bounds*).

## 1.5 Key Definitions (KTU Board Favourites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Absolute Error** ($E_a$): The magnitude of the difference between the true value ($x_T$) and the approximate value ($x_A$). Formula: $E_a = \vert x_T - x_A \vert$.
> - **Relative Error** ($E_r$): The absolute error normalized by the true value. Formula: $E_r = \dfrac{\vert x_T - x_A \vert}{\vert x_T \vert}$.
> - **Percentage Error** ($E_p$): Relative error expressed as a percentage. Formula: $E_p = E_r \times 100\%$.
> - **True Error** ($E_t$): The error computed using the *known exact* value.
> - **Approximate Error** ($\varepsilon_a$): The error estimated using the difference between *successive approximations* in an iterative method. Formula: $\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_{i+1} - x_i}{x_{i+1}} \right\vert \times 100\%$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Errors (Hierarchical Tree)

Numerical errors are broadly classified into two super-categories. Each has sub-types that KTU examiners love to test in 3-mark questions.

### A. Inherent Errors (Input Errors)
These are errors present in the *input data* itself, before any computation begins. Sources include:
- Measurement instrument precision (thermocouple, flow meter, manometer).
- Physical constants tabulated to limited decimal places.
- Human transcription error.

### B. Numerical / Computational Errors
These arise *during* the numerical procedure.

#### 2.1.1 Truncation Error (Discretization Error)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Truncation Error** is the difference between the true (analytical/infinite) result of a mathematical operation and the approximate result obtained using a truncated (finite) version of that operation. The term is *historical* — it arose because John von Neumann & Herman Goldstine (1947) used "truncating" a Taylor series to approximate a function.

**Most common origin** — Using a *finite* Taylor series to approximate a function:

$$P_n(x) = f(x_0) + f'(x_0)(x - x_0) + \frac{f''(x_0)}{2!}(x - x_0)^2 + \dots + \frac{f^{(n)}(x_0)}{n!}(x - x_0)^n$$

The *truncation error* (also called the *remainder* $R_n$) is:

$$R_n(x) = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x - x_0)^{n+1}, \quad \xi \in [x_0, x]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The truncation error is **proportional to $(x - x_0)^{n+1}$** — meaning the *order* of the method is $(n+1)$. This dictates how fast the error shrinks as the step-size $h$ decreases.

#### 2.1.2 Round-off Error

This arises because real numbers are represented in a digital computer using a **finite number of bits**.

A normalized floating-point number in a typical IEEE 754 single-precision system is stored as:

$$x = \pm (0.d_1 d_2 d_3 \dots d_t) \times \beta^e$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = base (e.g., 2 for binary, 10 for decimal)
- $d_i$ = digits (0 to $\beta - 1$)
- $t$ = number of significant digits (mantissa precision)
- $e$ = exponent

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Epsilon (Machine Precision), $\varepsilon_m$:** The smallest positive number $x$ such that $1 + x \neq 1$ in floating-point arithmetic. For a $t$-digit base-$\beta$ system: $\varepsilon_m = \beta^{1-t}$.
> - IEEE 754 *single precision* ($t = 24$, $\beta = 2$): $\varepsilon_m \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$.
> - IEEE 754 *double precision* ($t = 53$, $\beta = 2$): $\varepsilon_m \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$.

### 2.2 Significant Digits

> [!NOTE]
> The number of **significant digits** in a number is the count of all digits from the first non-zero digit on the left to the last digit on the right (whether zero or not).
> Examples:
> - $0.004523$ → 4 significant digits.
> - $5200.0$ → 5 significant digits.
> - $4.50 \times 10^3$ → 3 significant digits.

A result computed from imprecise data is generally reliable to at most **$n$ significant digits**, where $n$ is the number of significant digits in the *least precise* input.

### 2.3 Error Propagation

When a computed result $R$ is a function $R = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ of uncertain variables $x_i$, the error in $R$ propagates from the errors in $x_i$ via the **Total Differential / Taylor Expansion**:

$$dR = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} dx_1 + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} dx_2 + \dots + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} dx_n$$

**For a single variable** $R = f(x)$:

$$dR = f'(x) \, dx$$

**For a sum/difference** $R = a + b$:

$$dR = da + db \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_R = E_a + E_b \quad \text{(absolute errors add)}$$

**For a product/quotient** $R = ab$ or $R = a/b$:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{da}{a} + \frac{db}{b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{R,rel} = E_{a,rel} + E_{b,rel} \quad \text{(relative errors add)}$$

**For a power** $R = a^n$:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = n \frac{da}{a}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $E_a = \vert x_T - x_A \vert$ | Magnitude of error (same units as $x$) |
| 2 | Relative Error | $E_r = \dfrac{\vert x_T - x_A \vert}{\vert x_T \vert}$ | Dimensionless error ratio |
| 3 | Percentage Error | $E_p = E_r \times 100\%$ | Frequently asked; report in exam |
| 4 | Approximate Error | $\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_{i+1} - x_i}{x_{i+1}} \right\vert \times 100\%$ | Stopping criterion for iteration |
| 5 | Truncation Error (Taylor) | $R_n = \dfrac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x - x_0)^{n+1}$ | Use for any Taylor-series derived formula |
| 6 | Machine Epsilon | $\varepsilon_m = \beta^{1-t}$ | Smallest distinguishable increment |
| 7 | Round-off Error (Chopping) | $E_{ro} = 0.d_{t+1} d_{t+2} \dots \times \beta^{e-t}$ | When last digits are *discarded* |
| 8 | Round-off Error (Rounding) | $E_{ro} \le 0.5 \times \beta^{e-t}$ | When last digits are *rounded* |
| 9 | Error Propagation (general) | $dR = \sum \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} dx_i$ | Use for multivariate functions |
| 10 | Error in Sum | $\Delta R = \Delta a + \Delta b$ | Absolute errors add for $+ / -$ |
| 11 | Error in Product | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta a}{a} + \dfrac{\Delta b}{b}$ | Relative errors add for $\times / \div$ |
| 12 | Error in Power | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = n \dfrac{\Delta a}{a}$ | When raising to power $n$ |
| 13 | Condition Number | $\kappa = \dfrac{x \cdot f'(x)}{f(x)}$ | Measures amplification of error |
| 14 | Stability Criterion | $\vert g'(x) \vert < 1$ for fixed-point iteration | Determines convergence |

## 2.5 Stability, Accuracy, and Conditioning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Accuracy**: How close a computed value is to the *true* value.
> - **Precision**: How close repeated computations are to *each other* (reproducibility).
> - **Stability**: A method is stable if small changes in input produce small changes in output.
> - **Conditioning** (of a problem): A problem is *well-conditioned* if the relative error in output is comparable to the relative error in input. The **Condition Number** $\kappa$ quantifies this:
>
> $$\kappa = \frac{\text{Relative change in output}}{\text{Relative change in input}} = \frac{\vert \Delta y / y \vert}{\vert \Delta x / x \vert}$$
>
> - If $\kappa$ is **small (close to 1)**: well-conditioned.
> - If $\kappa$ is **large ($> 10^k$)**: ill-conditioned. A small input error blows up.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **process engineering**, error analysis is used for:
1. **Sensor calibration**: Quantifying instrument uncertainty in a distillation column's temperature sensors.
2. **Reactor design**: Estimating error bounds on calculated reaction rates from noisy concentration data.
3. **Heat exchanger design**: Propagating errors from viscosity, density, and conductivity measurements into the overall $U$ (heat-transfer coefficient).
4. **Process control**: Tuning the dead-band in PID controllers based on sensor noise.
5. **Optimization**: Verifying whether the optimum found is a *true* optimum or a numerical artifact.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Truncation Error of the Finite-Difference Approximation of a Derivative

> **Problem:** Show that the **forward-difference** approximation $f'(x) \approx \dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ has a truncation error of **order $O(h)$** (i.e., proportional to $h$).

### Step 1: Expand $f(x+h)$ as a Taylor series about $x$.

$$f(x+h) = f(x) + f'(x) \cdot h + \frac{f''(x)}{2!} h^2 + \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^3 + \frac{f^{(4)}(x)}{4!} h^4 + \dots$$

### Step 2: Rearrange to isolate $f'(x)$.

$$f'(x) \cdot h = f(x+h) - f(x) - \frac{f''(x)}{2!} h^2 - \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^3 - \dots$$

### Step 3: Divide both sides by $h$.

$$f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} - \frac{f''(x)}{2!} h - \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^2 - \dots$$

### Step 4: Identify the approximation and the error.

The first term on the right is the **forward-difference approximation**.

$$f'(x) = \underbrace{\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}}_{\text{Approximation}} - \underbrace{\left[ \frac{f''(x)}{2!} h + \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^2 + \dots \right]}_{\text{Truncation Error } R_T}$$

### Step 5: Express in big-O notation.

$$f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} + O(h)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The truncation error is dominated by the **first neglected term**, which is $\dfrac{f''(x)}{2} h$. Hence, the error is **order $O(h)$** — it shrinks *linearly* with the step size $h$. If $h$ is halved, the truncation error is halved.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Truncation Error of the Central-Difference Approximation

> **Problem:** Show that the **central-difference** approximation $f'(x) \approx \dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h}$ has a truncation error of **order $O(h^2)$**.

### Step 1: Write Taylor expansions for $f(x+h)$ and $f(x-h)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x+h) &= f(x) + f'(x)h + \frac{f''(x)}{2!}h^2 + \frac{f'''(x)}{3!}h^3 + \frac{f^{(4)}(x)}{4!}h^4 + \dots \\
f(x-h) &= f(x) - f'(x)h + \frac{f''(x)}{2!}h^2 - \frac{f'''(x)}{3!}h^3 + \frac{f^{(4)}(x)}{4!}h^4 - \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Subtract the two expansions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x+h) - f(x-h) &= 2 f'(x) h + 2 \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^3 + 2 \frac{f^{(5)}(x)}{5!} h^5 + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $f'(x)$.

$$f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} - \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^2 - \frac{f^{(5)}(x)}{5!} h^4 - \dots$$

### Step 4: Identify the truncation error.

$$f'(x) = \underbrace{\frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h}}_{\text{Approximation}} - \underbrace{\left[ \frac{f'''(x)}{6} h^2 + \dots \right]}_{\text{Truncation Error} = O(h^2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Central difference is **$O(h^2)$** — much more accurate than forward difference $O(h)$ for the same step size. If $h$ is halved, the error in central difference reduces by a factor of **4**, while forward difference only halves.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Error Propagation in a General Multivariate Function

> **Problem:** Given $R = f(x, y, z)$ with errors $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$, derive the formula for the maximum possible error in $R$.

### Step 1: Form the total differential of $R$.

By the chain rule for multivariable functions, the total differential is:

$$dR = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} dy + \frac{\partial f}{\partial z} dz$$

### Step 2: Bound the error (worst-case scenario).

If the errors $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$ are independent and can combine *constructively* (worst case), then the **maximum possible absolute error** is:

$$\Delta R_{max} = \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right\vert \Delta x + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \right\vert \Delta y + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial z} \right\vert \Delta z$$

### Step 3: Convert to relative error.

$$\frac{\Delta R_{max}}{R} = \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{x}{f} \right\vert \frac{\Delta x}{x} + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{y}{f} \right\vert \frac{\Delta y}{y} + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{z}{f} \right\vert \frac{\Delta z}{z}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the foundation of **uncertainty analysis** in chemical engineering labs. When computing the heat-transfer coefficient $U$ from $h_i, h_o, k_{wall}$, the *partial derivatives* act as **sensitivity coefficients** — telling you which input variable most influences the result.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Round-off and Truncation Error Estimation

> **Example 1 (KTU Classic):** The number $0.5673$ is stored in a 4-significant-digit floating-point system with chopping. What is the round-off error and the relative error?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** The true number is $x = 0.5673$ (already 4 sig. figs). In a 4-digit chopping system, the machine stores it exactly as $x_m = 0.5673$.

**Step 2:** But suppose we multiply it by 7 such that an intermediate result requires more digits. The machine keeps only 4 sig. figs.

Let us redo: $0.5673 \times 7 = 3.9711$ (exact). Stored as $3.971$ after chopping. Then $3.971 \times 3 = 11.913$ (exact). Stored as $11.91$.

**Step 3:** Compute the *true* value of the chain $0.5673 \times 7 \times 3 = 11.9133$. The stored result is $11.91$.

**Step 4:** Compute absolute and relative round-off error.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_a &= \vert 11.9133 - 11.91 \vert = 0.0033 \\
E_r &= \frac{0.0033}{11.9133} = 0.000277 \approx 0.0277\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Example 2 (Truncation Error):** Use the 3-term Taylor series to approximate $e^{0.5}$. What is the truncation error bound?

The Maclaurin series is $e^x = 1 + x + \dfrac{x^2}{2!} + \dfrac{x^3}{3!} + \dots$

**Step 1:** Truncate after 3 terms: $P_2(x) = 1 + x + \dfrac{x^2}{2}$.

**Step 2:** Evaluate at $x = 0.5$:

$$P_2(0.5) = 1 + 0.5 + \frac{0.25}{2} = 1 + 0.5 + 0.125 = 1.625$$

**Step 3:** True value $e^{0.5} = 1.64872127\dots$

**Step 4:** Truncation error:

$$R_2 = e^{0.5} - 1.625 = 0.02372127$$

**Step 5:** Theoretical bound using the remainder theorem.

The next term is $\dfrac{f'''(\xi)}{3!} x^3 = \dfrac{e^{\xi}}{6} \times 0.125$ for some $\xi \in [0, 0.5]$. Maximum value: $\dfrac{e^{0.5}}{6} \times 0.125 \approx \dfrac{1.6487}{48} \approx 0.0343$. Our error (0.0237) lies within this bound — **consistent with theory**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Error Propagation in Process Engineering

> **Example 3:** The heat transfer coefficient in a double-pipe heat exchanger is given by:
>
> $$U = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\Delta x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$
>
> Given $h_i = 1500 \pm 50$ W/m²K, $h_o = 800 \pm 30$ W/m²K, $k = 50 \pm 1$ W/mK, $\Delta x = 0.005 \pm 0.0001$ m. Estimate the maximum error in $U$.

**Step 1:** Compute nominal $U$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{h_i} &= \frac{1}{1500} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \\
\frac{\Delta x}{k} &= \frac{0.005}{50} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \\
\frac{1}{h_o} &= \frac{1}{800} = 1.250 \times 10^{-3} \\
\text{Sum} &= 6.667 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.250 \times 10^{-3} = 2.0167 \times 10^{-3} \\
U &= \frac{1}{2.0167 \times 10^{-3}} = 495.87 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute partial derivatives.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial U}{\partial h_i} &= \frac{U^2}{h_i^2} = \frac{(495.87)^2}{(1500)^2} = 0.1093 \\
\frac{\partial U}{\partial h_o} &= \frac{U^2}{h_o^2} = \frac{(495.87)^2}{(800)^2} = 0.3843 \\
\frac{\partial U}{\partial k} &= \frac{U^2 \Delta x}{k^2} = \frac{(495.87)^2 \times 0.005}{2500} = 0.4919 \\
\frac{\partial U}{\partial (\Delta x)} &= -\frac{U^2}{k} = -\frac{(495.87)^2}{50} = -4918.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply error propagation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta U_{max} &= \left\vert \frac{\partial U}{\partial h_i} \right\vert \Delta h_i + \left\vert \frac{\partial U}{\partial h_o} \right\vert \Delta h_o + \left\vert \frac{\partial U}{\partial k} \right\vert \Delta k + \left\vert \frac{\partial U}{\partial (\Delta x)} \right\vert \Delta(\Delta x) \\
&= (0.1093)(50) + (0.3843)(30) + (0.4919)(1) + (4918.9)(0.0001) \\
&= 5.465 + 11.529 + 0.4919 + 0.4919 \\
&\approx 17.98 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Report.

$$U = 495.87 \pm 17.98 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Relative Error} = \frac{17.98}{495.87} \approx 3.63\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dominant contribution to error comes from $h_o$ (outer convective coefficient) and the wall thickness $\Delta x$. Engineers can therefore *prioritize* improving the accuracy of these two measurements to reduce overall uncertainty.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Numerical Errors

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete taxonomy of numerical errors covered in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NUMERICAL ERRORS] --> B[INHERENT ERRORS]
    A --> C[COMPUTATIONAL ERRORS]

    B --> B1[Measurement Error]
    B --> B2[Data Transcription Error]
    B --> B3[Model Assumption Error]

    C --> D[TRUNCATION ERROR]
    C --> E[ROUND-OFF ERROR]

    D --> D1[Taylor Series Truncation]
    D --> D2[Finite-Difference Approximation]
    D --> D3[Order O h to the power n]

    D1 --> D11[Forward Difference O h]
    D1 --> D12[Backward Difference O h]
    D1 --> D13[Central Difference O h squared]

    E --> E1[Chopping]
    E --> E2[Symmetric Rounding]
    E --> E3[Machine Epsilon]
    E --> E4[Overflow Underflow]

    E1 --> E11[Error less than beta to power e minus t]
    E2 --> E22[Error less than 0.5 times beta to power e minus t]
```

## 4.2 Error Propagation Workflow in a Numerical Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE]
        I1[Physical Measurement]
        I2[Tabulated Constants]
        I3[Model Parameters]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[COMPUTATION STAGE]
        P1[Algorithm Selection]
        P2[Discretization - Truncation Error]
        P3[Floating Point Arithmetic - Round Off Error]
        P4[Iterative Convergence - Approximate Error]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STAGE]
        O1[Numerical Result]
        O2[Error Bound Calculation]
        O3[Stability and Condition Check]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4

    P4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3

    O3 --> Q1{Is Error Within Tolerance?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Accept Result]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Refine Method or Improve Input]
    Q3 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing an Error Mitigation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> Q1{Is the problem ill conditioned?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Reformulate Problem Algebraically]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is truncation error dominant?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Reduce Step Size h or Increase Order n]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is round off error dominant?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Use Higher Precision or Compensated Summation]
    Q3 -- No --> A4[Iterative Method with Convergence Criterion]
    A1 --> END[Re Evaluate Total Error]
    A2 --> END
    A3 --> END
    A4 --> END
    END --> STOP[Report Error Bound]
```

## 4.4 Floating-Point Number Representation (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FP[IEEE 754 Single Precision 32 Bits]
        S[Sign Bit - 1 bit]
        E[Exponent - 8 bits]
        M[Mantissa - 23 bits]
    end
    S --> VAL[Normalized Value plus or minus 1 dot M times 2 to the power E minus 127]
    E --> VAL
    M --> VAL
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PECHT526]**
> **Define the following with one example each:**
> (a) Truncation error
> (b) Round-off error
> (c) Absolute error

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Truncation Error:** The error introduced when an infinite mathematical process is replaced by a finite approximation. *Example:* Using only the first three terms of the Taylor series $e^x = 1 + x + \dfrac{x^2}{2!} + \dots$ introduces a truncation error of $\dfrac{x^3}{3!} + \dots$

**(b) Round-off Error:** The error caused by representing a real number with a finite number of significant digits in a computer's floating-point arithmetic. *Example:* Storing $\pi = 3.14159265\dots$ in a 5-digit system gives $3.1416$ with a round-off error of approximately $7.35 \times 10^{-6}$.

**(c) Absolute Error:** The magnitude of the difference between the true value $x_T$ and the approximate value $x_A$, i.e., $E_a = \vert x_T - x_A \vert$. *Example:* If the true value is $3.142$ and the computed value is $3.140$, then $E_a = 0.002$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper PECHT526]**
> **Distinguish between accuracy and precision. How are they related to numerical errors?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy** refers to how *close a measured/computed value* is to the **true value** of the quantity. It is directly related to the *magnitude of the numerical error*. A highly accurate result has a small true error.
- **Precision** refers to how *close repeated measurements/computations* are to **each other**, regardless of whether they are near the true value. It relates to the *reproducibility* of the method and is constrained by the floating-point precision of the system (i.e., round-off error).
- A method can be **precise but inaccurate** (e.g., a miscalibrated sensor giving the same wrong reading 10 times) or **accurate but imprecise** (e.g., scattered readings that average out to the true value).
- In KTU valuation, *accuracy* is judged by the *truncation error* of the method, while *precision* is governed by *round-off error*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Derive the truncation error term for the **forward-difference approximation** of the first derivative of a function $f(x)$ using the Taylor series expansion. Show that the error is of order $O(h)$.
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Expand $f(x+h)$ in Taylor series about $x$:

$$f(x+h) = f(x) + f'(x) h + \frac{f''(x)}{2!} h^2 + \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^3 + \frac{f^{(4)}(x)}{4!} h^4 + \dots$$

**Step 2:** Subtract $f(x)$ from both sides and divide by $h$:

$$\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} = f'(x) + \frac{f''(x)}{2!} h + \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^2 + \dots$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} - \frac{f''(x)}{2!} h - \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^2 - \dots$$

**Step 4:** Identify the truncation error:

$$E_T = -\frac{f''(x)}{2!} h - \frac{f'''(x)}{3!} h^2 - \dots$$

The dominant term is $-\frac{f''(x)}{2} h$, so:

$$E_T = O(h) = -\frac{f''(x)}{2} h$$

**Conclusion:** The truncation error is **proportional to $h$** (first-order accurate). To halve the error, halve the step size $h$.

---

> **(b)** A student is using a floating-point number system with $t = 4$ significant digits and base $\beta = 10$ using **chopping** to store the value of $e \approx 2.718281828\dots$. Calculate:
> (i) The stored value
> (ii) The absolute round-off error
> (iii) The relative round-off error
> (iv) The machine epsilon for this system.
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stored value:** With chopping to 4 significant digits, $e = 2.718\dots$ is stored as $x_m = 2.718$.

**(ii) Absolute round-off error:**

$$E_a = \vert x_T - x_m \vert = \vert 2.718281828 - 2.718 \vert = 0.000281828$$

**(iii) Relative round-off error:**

$$E_r = \frac{0.000281828}{2.718281828} = 1.0369 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01037\%$$

**(iv) Machine epsilon:**

$$\varepsilon_m = \beta^{1-t} = 10^{1-4} = 10^{-3} = 0.001$$

This means the smallest number that, when added to 1, produces a different stored result, is $0.001$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** What is **error propagation**? Derive the formula for the propagation of absolute and relative errors in:
> (i) $R = a + b - c$
> (ii) $R = \dfrac{a \cdot b}{c^2}$
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Error propagation is the study of how uncertainties (errors) in input variables affect the uncertainty in a computed result that is a function of those variables. It uses the *total differential* of the function.

**(i) For $R = a + b - c$:**

The total differential is:

$$dR = da + db - dc$$

Worst-case absolute error (taking absolute values):

$$\Delta R = \vert \Delta a \vert + \vert \Delta b \vert + \vert \Delta c \vert$$

Relative error:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\vert \Delta a \vert + \vert \Delta b \vert + \vert \Delta c \vert}{\vert a + b - c \vert}$$

**(ii) For $R = \dfrac{a \cdot b}{c^2}$:**

Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln R = \ln a + \ln b - 2 \ln c$$

Differentiate both sides:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{da}{a} + \frac{db}{b} - 2 \frac{dc}{c}$$

Taking absolute values for worst case:

$$\left\vert \frac{\Delta R}{R} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\Delta a}{a} \right\vert + \left\vert \frac{\Delta b}{b} \right\vert + 2 \left\vert \frac{\Delta c}{c} \right\vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **General Rule:** For sums/differences, *absolute* errors add. For products/quotients/powers, *relative* errors add.

---

> **(b)** A process engineer is computing the **Reynolds number** in a pipe using $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$. The measured values are: $\rho = 1000 \pm 10$ kg/m³, $v = 2.0 \pm 0.05$ m/s, $D = 0.05 \pm 0.001$ m, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \pm 5 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Estimate the **maximum absolute error**, **relative error**, and **percentage error** in $Re$.
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the nominal $Re$:**

$$Re = \frac{(1000)(2.0)(0.05)}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{100}{10^{-3}} = 100{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute the relative error in each variable:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} &= \frac{10}{1000} = 0.01 = 1\% \\
\frac{\Delta v}{v} &= \frac{0.05}{2.0} = 0.025 = 2.5\% \\
\frac{\Delta D}{D} &= \frac{0.001}{0.05} = 0.02 = 2\% \\
\frac{\Delta \mu}{\mu} &= \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.05 = 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply error propagation (product/quotient → relative errors add):**

$$\frac{\Delta Re}{Re} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta v}{v} + \frac{\Delta D}{D} + \frac{\Delta \mu}{\mu} = 0.01 + 0.025 + 0.02 + 0.05 = 0.105$$

**Step 4: Compute maximum absolute error:**

$$\Delta Re = 0.105 \times 100{,}000 = 10{,}500$$

**Step 5: Compute percentage error:**

$$E_p = 0.105 \times 100\% = 10.5\%$$

**Final Result:**

$$Re = 100{,}000 \pm 10{,}500 \quad (\text{Percentage Error} = 10.5\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The viscosity measurement ($\mu$) contributes the **largest** relative error (5%). The engineer should invest in a more accurate viscometer to reduce overall uncertainty.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Error Analysis Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing absolute and relative errors:** Students often use the wrong type. Remember: **+ and − → absolute errors add; × and ÷ → relative errors add**.
> 2. **Forgetting to take absolute values in worst-case error:** When combining errors, always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to get the *maximum possible* error. Examiner will deduct 1 mark for missing absolute values.
> 3. **Confusing truncation and round-off errors:** Truncation = mathematical approximation (e.g., Taylor series cut-off). Round-off = computer's finite-digit storage. They are **different in origin**.
> 4. **Wrong machine epsilon formula:** It is $\varepsilon_m = \beta^{1-t}$, NOT $\beta^{-t}$. This is a 1-mark trap in KTU papers.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps in 14-mark questions:** For Part B, every algebraic step carries 1 mark. Do not write the final answer directly.
> 6. **Not reporting units:** Absolute error has the same units as the variable. Relative and percentage errors are dimensionless. Many students lose 0.5–1 mark by ignoring this.
> 7. **Using *true* error vs *approximate* error interchangeably:** True error requires knowing the exact value; approximate error uses successive iterations. KTU examiners distinguish these strictly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Error Analysis (PECHT526, Module 2):**
> - Numerical errors are of **two types**: **Truncation** (mathematical) and **Round-off** (computational).
> - **Absolute Error** = $\vert x_T - x_A \vert$. **Relative Error** = $E_a / \vert x_T \vert$. **Percentage Error** = $E_r \times 100\%$.
> - **Approximate Error** $\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_{i+1} - x_i}{x_{i+1}} \right\vert \times 100\%$ is the **stopping criterion** for iterative methods.
> - **Machine Epsilon** $\varepsilon_m = \beta^{1-t}$ where $\beta$ is the base and $t$ is the number of significant digits.
> - For a Taylor series truncated to $n$ terms, the **truncation error** is $O(h^{n+1})$.
> - **Forward-difference** derivative: error is **$O(h)$** (first order).
> - **Central-difference** derivative: error is **$O(h^2)$** (second order) — more accurate.
> - **Error propagation rule**: For $R = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, $\Delta R = \sum \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right\vert \Delta x_i$.
> - **Sum/Difference** → absolute errors add. **Product/Quotient/Power** → relative errors add.
> - **Condition Number** $\kappa = \dfrac{x f'(x)}{f(x)}$ measures how much a small input error gets amplified. $\kappa \gg 1$ ⇒ ill-conditioned.
> - **Well-conditioned problem + stable algorithm = accurate result.**
> - In process engineering applications, always identify the **dominant error source** and prioritize uncertainty reduction there (e.g., use sensitivity coefficients).
> - KTU expects you to: (1) define errors with formulas, (2) derive truncation errors from Taylor series, (3) apply error propagation to process engineering formulas, and (4) compute machine epsilon.
> - Always report **both absolute AND percentage error** in numerical answers unless the question specifies otherwise.
> - Remember the IEEE 754 single-precision $\varepsilon_m \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$ and double-precision $\varepsilon_m \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$ for MCQs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
