topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Iterative Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Iterative Methods?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Iterative methods** are numerical procedures that generate a sequence of progressively refined approximations to the solution of a mathematical equation (typically $f(x) = 0$ or $g(x) = x$) by repeatedly applying a well-defined recurrence relation, starting from an initial guess. Each successive approximation is called an **iterate**, and the process continues until a predefined **convergence criterion** (tolerance) is satisfied.

In the context of **Process Engineering**, iterative methods are the workhorse of chemical engineering computation because most nonlinear equations arising from thermodynamics (e.g., Antoine equation for vapor pressure), reaction kinetics (Arrhenius-based rate equations), and mass/energy balances **cannot be solved analytically** in closed form. Iterative methods bridge the gap between the physical model and the numerical answer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Homing Pigeon

Imagine you are blindfolded somewhere on a hilly landscape and you want to find the **bottom of a valley** (the root of an equation).

- **Bracketing methods (Bisection):** You have a friend at a higher elevation on your *left* and another on your *right*. You always know the valley bottom lies *between* them. So you walk to the midpoint. One friend is now above you, the other below — so you are closer. You keep halving your uncertainty until the bottom is precise.
- **Open methods (Newton-Raphson, Secant):** You have no friends. You just feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet and take a step **in the direction of steepest descent**, scaled by the steepness. If the slope is gentle, you take a longer step; if it's steep, a shorter one. You can leap directly toward the valley — but you might also leap *over* it and miss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Grand Families of Iterative Methods for Root Finding**
> 1. **Bracketing Methods** — Always *converge* but converge *slowly*. The interval is guaranteed to contain the root. Example: **Bisection Method**.
> 2. **Open Methods** — Converge *very fast* when they converge, but may *diverge* if the initial guess is poor. Examples: **Newton-Raphson, Secant, Fixed-Point Iteration**.

## 1.3 Why Iterative Methods are Indispensable in Process Engineering

| Engineering Problem | Equation Form | Why Iterative? |
|---|---|---|
| Vapor pressure of a component | $\ln P = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ (Antoine) | Transcendental, no closed-form for $T$ |
| CSTR steady-state | $V = \dfrac{F \cdot C_{A0} \cdot X}{k C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n}$ | Nonlinear in conversion $X$ |
| Compressibility factor | $Z = 1 + BP + CP^2 + \dots$ | Cubic / higher-order polynomial |
| Heat-exchanger LMTD correction | $F$-factor with implicit $P$ | $P$ appears inside logs |
| Bubble / dew point | $\sum y_i = 1$ or $\sum x_i = 1$ | Nonlinear in $T$ or $P$ |

The term **iterate** refers to one complete cycle of the recurrence. After $n$ iterations we obtain $x_n$, and the method is said to have **converged** when $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ for a prescribed tolerance $\varepsilon$.

## 1.4 Classification Tree of Iterative Methods for $f(x)=0$

- **Iterative Methods for Root-Finding**
  - **Bracketing**
    - Bisection Method
    - Method of False Position (Regula Falsi)
  - **Open**
    - Fixed-Point Iteration
    - Newton-Raphson Method
    - Secant Method
    - Modified Newton-Raphson (multiple roots)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bisection Method — Theoretical Breakdown

**Pre-condition (Bolzano's Theorem):** If $f(x)$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, then there exists **at least one** root $c \in (a,b)$.

**Algorithmic Logic (Step-by-step):**

1. Verify that $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ (sign change across the interval).
2. Compute the midpoint $c = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$.
3. Evaluate $f(c)$.
4. **Decision rule:**
   - If $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, the root lies in $[a,c]$ → set $b = c$.
   - Else if $f(c) \cdot f(b) < 0$, the root lies in $[c,b]$ → set $a = c$.
   - Else, $f(c) = 0$ exactly — terminate.
5. Repeat from Step 2 until $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ (interval-width test) **or** $\vert f(c) \vert < \varepsilon$ (function-value test).

**Why does it converge?** The interval is halved every iteration, so after $n$ iterations the interval length is $\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$. For a tolerance of $10^{-p}$ we need $n \geq \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{10^{-p}}\right)}{\log 2}$.

## 2.2 Fixed-Point Iteration (Successive Substitution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea**
> Rewrite $f(x) = 0$ as $x = g(x)$. Starting with $x_0$, generate the sequence $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$. If the sequence converges, its limit $L$ satisfies $L = g(L)$, i.e., $f(L) = 0$.

**Geometric interpretation:** The fixed point is the **intersection** of the curve $y = g(x)$ with the line $y = x$. If $\vert g'(x) \vert < 1$ in a neighbourhood of the fixed point, iteration converges (contraction mapping).

## 2.3 Newton-Raphson Method

**Derivation Intuition (Why does it work?):**
Near a root, $f(x)$ is approximately linear with slope $f'(x_n)$. So $f(x) \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n) = 0 \Rightarrow x = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$.

**Recurrence:**
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Convergence Criterion:** Quadratic — error squared each step near a simple root: $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \approx C \cdot e_n^2$.

**Failure Modes:**
- $f'(x_n) = 0$ → division by zero.
- Initial guess far from root → may diverge or cycle.
- Multiple roots → converges only **linearly**.

## 2.4 Secant Method

Replaces the derivative in Newton-Raphson with a **finite-difference approximation**:
$$f'(x_n) \approx \frac{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}{x_n - x_{n-1}}$$

**Recurrence:**
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{_{n-1}})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$

**Convergence Order:** Superlinear — $\approx 1.618$ (the **golden ratio**).

**Advantage over Newton-Raphson:** Does not require $f'(x)$ — useful when derivatives are messy (e.g., in process models with implicit thermodynamic relations).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Recurrence Relation | Convergence Order | Requires $f'(x)$? | Convergence Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bisection | $c = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | Linear ($1/2$) | No | $f(a)\cdot f(b) < 0$ (sign change) |
| 2 | False Position | $c = b - \dfrac{f(b)(a-b)}{f(a)-f(b)}$ | Linear (variable rate) | No | $f(a)\cdot f(b) < 0$ |
| 3 | Fixed-Point | $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ | Linear | No | $\vert g'(L) \vert < 1$ near $L$ |
| 4 | Newton-Raphson | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | **Quadratic** | Yes | $f'(x^*) \neq 0$, good $x_0$ |
| 5 | Secant | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$ | Superlinear ($\approx 1.618$) | No | Two initial guesses, $f(x_n)\neq f(x_{n-1})$ |

| # | Stopping Criterion | Formula | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Absolute error | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ | Iterations far from root |
| (ii) | Relative error | $\left\vert \dfrac{x_{n+1} - x_n}{x_{n+1}} \right\vert < \varepsilon$ | Large $x$ magnitudes |
| (iii) | Function residual | $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$ | Engineering balance checks |
| (iv) | Interval width (Bisection only) | $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ | Bracketing methods |

| # | Number of Bisection Iterations Required | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| (v) | To reach tolerance $\varepsilon$ from $[a_0, b_0]$ | $n \geq \dfrac{\ln\left(\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\ln 2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> In a typical **Process Engineering simulation package** (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, DWSIM), the inner loop that solves every flash drum, every reactor, and every distillation stage uses Newton-Raphson for its raw speed, but it is wrapped in a bisection *outer loop* that guarantees convergence if Newton diverges. This is the industry-standard "**Newton with bisection fallback**" pattern.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Newton-Raphson Recurrence

**Starting Point:** We want to find $x^*$ such that $f(x^*) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Taylor Expansion around the current iterate $x_n$:**
$$f(x) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n) + \frac{f''(x_n)}{2!}(x - x_n)^2 + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Truncate to first order (linearization):**
$$f(x) \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n)$$

**Step 3 — Set the linearized expression to zero to find the next estimate of the root:**
$$0 = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x_{n+1}$:**
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

This is the Newton-Raphson recurrence.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bisection Convergence Rate

After $n$ iterations, the interval length is:
$$L_n = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$$

To guarantee $L_n < \varepsilon$:
$$\frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n} < \varepsilon \implies 2^n > \frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon} \implies n > \log_2 \!\left(\frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

Therefore:
$$n_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\ln\left(\frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\ln 2} \right\rceil$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Bisection Method (Process Engineering Context)

**Problem (KTU-typical, 7-mark style):**
The vapor pressure of ethanol is given by the Antoine equation:
$$\log_{10} P = A - \frac{B}{T + C}$$
where $A = 8.20417$, $B = 1642.89$, $C = 230.300$, $P$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C. Find the temperature at which $P = 760$ mmHg (i.e., normal boiling point), to an accuracy of $\varepsilon = 1$ °C. Use Bisection on $[a_0, b_0] = [70, 90]$.

**Formulating $f(T)$:**
$$f(T) = 8.20417 - \frac{1642.89}{T + 230.300} - \log_{10}(760)$$

Numerically, $\log_{10}(760) = 2.88081$, so:
$$f(T) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{T + 230.300}$$

**Step 1 — Verify bracketing:**
$$f(70) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{300.300} = 5.32336 - 5.47135 = -0.14799 < 0$$
$$f(90) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{320.300} = 5.32336 - 5.12879 = +0.19457 > 0$$

Since $f(70) \cdot f(90) < 0$, a root exists in $[70, 90]$.

**Step 2 — Iteration 1:**
$$c_1 = \frac{70 + 90}{2} = 80$$
$$f(80) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{310.300} = 5.32336 - 5.29423 = +0.02913$$

Sign change between $70$ (negative) and $80$ (positive) → new interval $[70, 80]$.

**Step 3 — Iteration 2:**
$$c_2 = \frac{70 + 80}{2} = 75$$
$$f(75) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{305.300} = 5.32336 - 5.38100 = -0.05764$$

Sign change between $75$ and $80$ → new interval $[75, 80]$.

**Step 4 — Iteration 3:**
$$c_3 = \frac{75 + 80}{2} = 77.5$$
$$f(77.5) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{307.800} = 5.32336 - 5.33715 = -0.01379$$

Sign change between $77.5$ and $80$ → new interval $[77.5, 80]$.

**Step 5 — Iteration 4:**
$$c_4 = \frac{77.5 + 80}{2} = 78.75$$
$$f(78.75) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{309.050} = 5.32336 - 5.31562 = +0.00774$$

Sign change between $77.5$ and $78.75$ → new interval $[77.5, 78.75]$.

**Step 6 — Iteration 5:**
$$c_5 = \frac{77.5 + 78.75}{2} = 78.125$$
$$f(78.125) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{308.425} = 5.32336 - 5.32636 = -0.00300$$

Sign change between $78.125$ and $78.75$ → new interval $[78.125, 78.75]$.

**Step 7 — Stopping check:** Interval width $= 0.625 < 1.0$ ✓ (we already crossed the threshold but proceed for clarity). Take midpoint as the estimate: $T_{\text{bp}} \approx 78.44$ °C.

*(Reported experimental value of ethanol normal boiling point is 78.37 °C — our answer is within 0.07 °C.)*

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Newton-Raphson Method

**Same Antoine problem, starting with $T_0 = 80$ °C.**

**Step 1 — Derive $f'(T)$:**
$$f(T) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{T + 230.300}$$
$$f'(T) = \frac{1642.89}{(T + 230.300)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 1 ($T_0 = 80$):**
$$f(80) = +0.02913 \quad (\text{computed above})$$
$$f'(80) = \frac{1642.89}{(310.300)^2} = \frac{1642.89}{96{,}286.09} = 0.017062$$
$$T_1 = 80 - \frac{0.02913}{0.017062} = 80 - 1.7073 = 78.2927$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 2 ($T_1 = 78.2927$):**
$$f(78.2927) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{308.5927} = 5.32336 - 5.32333 = +0.00003$$
$$f'(78.2927) = \frac{1642.89}{(308.5927)^2} = 0.017252$$
$$T_2 = 78.2927 - \frac{0.00003}{0.017252} \approx 78.2910$$

**Step 4 — Convergence:** $\vert T_2 - T_1 \vert = 0.0017 < 10^{-2}$ ✓

**Comparison:** Newton-Raphson reached the same answer in **2 iterations** versus **5+** for Bisection — a vivid demonstration of the **quadratic vs. linear** speed advantage.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Secant Method

**Same Antoine problem, using $T_{-1} = 75$ and $T_0 = 80$.**

We already have:
$$f(75) = -0.05764, \quad f(80) = +0.02913$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$T_1 = 80 - \frac{0.02913 \cdot (80 - 75)}{0.02913 - (-0.05764)} = 80 - \frac{0.14565}{0.08677} = 80 - 1.6785 = 78.3215$$
$$f(78.3215) = 5.32336 - \frac{1642.89}{308.6215} = 5.32336 - 5.32336 \approx 0.00000$$

Already converged to 4 decimal places in **one** iteration. The Secant method is powerful because the initial bracket is used *only* to seed two starting points — no further bracketing is enforced.

## 3.6 Convergence Analysis — Fixed-Point Iteration

**Theorem:** Let $g$ be continuously differentiable on $[a,b]$ with $g(x) \in [a,b]$ for all $x \in [a,b]$. If there exists $k < 1$ such that $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k$ for all $x \in [a,b]$, then for any $x_0 \in [a,b]$, the iteration $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ converges to the unique fixed point $L$ in $[a,b]$, and:
$$\vert x_{n+1} - L \vert \leq \frac{k}{1 - k} \vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$$
$$\vert x_{n+1} - L \vert \leq k^n \vert x_0 - L \vert$$

**Key Engineering Takeaway:** The choice of $g(x)$ matters enormously. The same equation $f(x) = 0$ can be written as $x = g(x)$ in many ways, and some converge while others diverge based on the slope of $g$ at the fixed point.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bisection Method — Decision Flow Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> B0[/Input f of x, a0, b0, epsilon/]
    B0 --> C0{f at a0 times f at b0 less than zero?}
    C0 -- No --> D0[No sign change, abort or pick new interval]
    D0 --> Z1([End with error])
    C0 -- Yes --> E0[c equals a0 plus b0 over 2]
    E0 --> F0[Compute f of c]
    F0 --> G0{Absolute value of f of c less than epsilon OR b0 minus a0 less than epsilon}
    G0 -- Yes --> H0[Report c as root]
    H0 --> Z2([End with success])
    G0 -- No --> I0{f at a0 times f at c less than zero}
    I0 -- Yes --> J0[Set b0 equals c]
    I0 -- No --> K0[Set a0 equals c]
    J0 --> E0
    K0 --> E0
```

## 4.2 Newton-Raphson Method — Geometric Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0([Start]) --> N1[/Input f of x, f prime of x, x0, epsilon/]
    N1 --> N2[Compute f of x0 and f prime of x0]
    N2 --> N3{f prime of x0 equals zero}
    N3 -- Yes --> N4[Abort, derivative zero]
    N3 -- No --> N5[x1 equals x0 minus f of x0 over f prime of x0]
    N5 --> N6{Absolute value of x1 minus x0 less than epsilon}
    N6 -- No --> N7[Set x0 equals x1 and loop back to step 2]
    N7 --> N2
    N6 -- Yes --> N8[Report x1 as root]
    N8 --> NX([End with success])
```

## 4.3 Comparative Convergence Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENG[Engineering Problem f of x equals zero]
        P0[Antoine equation for T] --> P1[CSTR conversion X]
        P1 --> P2[Compressibility Z]
    end
    ENG --> CHOOSE{Initial guess available and derivative easy}
    CHOOSE -- Yes --> PATH1[Newton Raphson path, quadratic]
    CHOOSE -- Derivative hard --> PATH2[Secant path, superlinear]
    CHOOSE -- Bracket known --> PATH3[Bisection path, guaranteed slow]
    PATH1 --> SOL[Root x star]
    PATH2 --> SOL
    PATH3 --> SOL
```

## 4.4 Convergence Order Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Iteration n, error en] -- Newton Raphson --> B1[Iteration n+1, error en+1 proportional to en squared]
    A1 -- Secant --> B2[Iteration n+1, error en+1 proportional to en raised to 1.618]
    A1 -- Bisection or Fixed Point --> B3[Iteration n+1, error en+1 proportional to en linear]
    B1 --> R1[Fastest, needs f prime]
    B2 --> R2[Fast, no f prime needed]
    B3 --> R3[Slow but very robust]
```
