# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Concept of Residual `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term *residual* in the context of the relaxation method for solving a system of linear equations. How is it used as a stopping criterion?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **residual** $R_i$ associated with the $i$-th equation of the system $[A]\{x\} = \{b\}$ after an approximate solution $\{x\}^{(k)}$ has been computed is defined as:

$$
R_i^{(k)} = b_i - \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij}\,x_j^{(k)}
$$

It quantifies the **amount by which the $i$-th equation is not satisfied** by the current iterate. As a stopping criterion, the iteration is terminated when the *maximum absolute residual* falls below a prescribed tolerance $\varepsilon$:

$$
\max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \vert R_i^{(k)} \vert \leq \varepsilon
$$

Typical KTU exam tolerances are $\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$ or $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: Over-Relaxation vs Under-Relaxation `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between *over-relaxation* and *under-relaxation* in the SOR method. State the condition on the relaxation parameter $\omega$ for convergence.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Over-Relaxation ($\omega > 1$):** The correction applied at each step is *amplified* beyond the Gauss-Seidel update. This accelerates convergence when the Gauss-Seidel method is already convergent but slow.
- **Under-Relaxation ($0 < \omega < 1$):** The correction is *damped*. This is employed when the Gauss-Seidel method oscillates or diverges, and it stabilises the iteration.

The **necessary condition for convergence** of the SOR method is that the relaxation parameter must lie in the open interval $0 < \omega < 2$. The *optimal* $\omega$ is problem-dependent and is usually determined empirically. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Gauss-Seidel Iteration `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) Solve the following system of linear equations using the Gauss-Seidel method of relaxation. Perform iterations until the approximate relative error in each variable falls below 1%. Verify convergence by checking diagonal dominance first.** (7 marks)

$$
3x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 = 10
$$

$$
x_1 + 4x_2 + 2x_3 = 15
$$

$$
2x_1 + x_2 + 5x_3 = 19
$$

**(b) Calculate the residuals after the final iteration and comment on the accuracy of the solution obtained.** (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question A

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Diagonal Dominance Test**

- Row 1: $\vert 3 \vert$ vs $\vert 2 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 3 \Rightarrow 3 = 3$ (weakly dominant, *borderline* — convergence is *likely* but not guaranteed strictly)
- Row 2: $\vert 4 \vert$ vs $\vert 1 \vert + \vert 2 \vert = 3 \Rightarrow 4 > 3$ ✓
- Row 3: $\vert 5 \vert$ vs $\vert 2 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 3 \Rightarrow 5 > 3$ ✓

The system is **diagonally dominant** and the Gauss-Seidel iteration **will converge**.

**Step 2: Rearranged Iteration Formulas**

$$
x_1^{(k+1)} = \frac{10 - 2x_2^{(k)} - x_3^{(k)}}{3}
$$

$$
x_2^{(k+1)} = \frac{15 - x_1^{(k+1)} - 2x_3^{(k)}}{4}
$$

$$
x_3^{(k+1)} = \frac{19 - 2x_1^{(k+1)} - x_2^{(k+1)}}{5}
$$

**Step 3: Initial Guess** $x_1^{(0)} = x_2^{(0)} = x_3^{(0)} = 0$

#### Iteration 1

$$
x_1^{(1)} = \frac{10 - 0 - 0}{3} = 3.3333
$$

$$
x_2^{(1)} = \frac{15 - 3.3333 - 0}{4} = \frac{11.6667}{4} = 2.9167
$$

$$
x_3^{(1)} = \frac{19 - 2(3.3333) - 2.9167}{5} = \frac{9.4167}{5} = 1.8833
$$

Relative errors: All undefined since previous values were zero.

#### Iteration 2

$$
x_1^{(2)} = \frac{10 - 2(2.9167) - 1.8833}{3} = \frac{2.2833}{3} = 0.7611
$$

$$
x_2^{(2)} = \frac{15 - 0.7611 - 2(1.8833)}{4} = \frac{10.4723}{4} = 2.6181
$$

$$
x_3^{(2)} = \frac{19 - 2(0.7611) - 2.6181}{5} = \frac{14.8597}{5} = 2.9719
$$

Relative errors:

- $\vert (x_1^{(2)} - x_1^{(1)})/x_1^{(2)} \vert = \vert (0.7611 - 3.3333)/0.7611 \vert = 3.380$ (338%) — still high
- $\vert (x_2^{(2)} - x_2^{(1)})/x_2^{(2)} \vert = \vert (2.6181 - 2.9167)/2.6181 \vert = 0.114$ (11.4%)
- $\vert (x_3^{(2)} - x_3^{(1)})/x_3^{(2)} \vert = \vert (2.9719 - 1.8833)/2.9719 \vert = 0.366$ (36.6%)

#### Iteration 3

$$
x_1^{(3)} = \frac{10 - 2(2.6181) - 2.9719}{3} = \frac{1.7919}{3} = 0.5973
$$

$$
x_2^{(3)} = \frac{15 - 0.5973 - 2(2.9719)}{4} = \frac{8.4589}{4} = 2.1147
$$

$$
x_3^{(3)} = \frac{19 - 2(0.5973) - 2.1147}{5} = \frac{15.6907}{5} = 3.1381
$$

Relative errors:

- $\vert (x_1^{(3)} - x_1^{(2)})/x_1^{(3)} \vert = \vert (0.5973 - 0.7611)/0.5973 \vert = 0.275$ (27.5%)
- $\vert (x_2^{(3)} - x_2^{(2)})/x_2^{(3)} \vert = \vert (2.1147 - 2.6181)/2.1147 \vert = 0.238$ (23.8%)
- $\vert (x_3^{(3)} - x_3^{(2)})/x_3^{(3)} \vert = \vert (3.1381 - 2.9719)/3.1381 \vert = 0.053$ (5.3%)

#### Iteration 4

$$
x_1^{(4)} = \frac{10 - 2(2.1147) - 3.1381}{3} = \frac{2.6325}{3} = 0.8775
$$

$$
x_2^{(4)} = \frac{15 - 0.8775 - 2(3.1381)}{4} = \frac{7.8463}{4} = 1.9616
$$

$$
x_3^{(4)} = \frac{19 - 2(0.8775) - 1.9616}{5} = \frac{15.2834}{5} = 3.0567
$$

Relative errors:

- $\vert (x_1^{(4)} - x_1^{(3)})/x_1^{(4)} \vert = \vert (0.8775 - 0.5973)/0.8775 \vert = 0.319$ (31.9%)
- $\vert (x_2^{(4)} - x_2^{(3)})/x_2^{(4)} \vert = \vert (1.9616 - 2.1147)/1.9616 \vert = 0.078$ (7.8%)
- $\vert (x_3^{(4)} - x_3^{(3)})/x_3^{(4)} \vert = \vert (3.0567 - 3.1381)/3.0567 \vert = 0.027$ (2.7%)

#### Iteration 5

$$
x_1^{(5)} = \frac{10 - 2(1.9616) - 3.0567}{3} = \frac{3.0201}{3} = 1.0067
$$

$$
x_2^{(5)} = \frac{15 - 1.0067 - 2(3.0567)}{4} = \frac{7.8799}{4} = 1.9700
$$

$$
x_3^{(5)} = \frac{19 - 2(1.0067) - 1.9700}{5} = \frac{15.0166}{5} = 3.0033
$$

Relative errors:

- $\vert (x_1^{(5)} - x_1^{(4)})/x_1^{(5)} \vert = \vert (1.0067 - 0.8775)/1.0067 \vert = 0.128$ (12.8%)
- $\vert (x_2^{(5)} - x_2^{(4)})/x_2^{(5)} \vert = \vert (1.9700 - 1.9616)/1.9700 \vert = 0.004$ (0.4%) ✓
- $\vert (x_3^{(5)} - x_3^{(4)})/x_3^{(5)} \vert = \vert (3.0033 - 3.0567)/3.0033 \vert = 0.018$ (1.8%)

#### Iteration 6

$$
x_1^{(6)} = \frac{10 - 2(1.9700) - 3.0033}{3} = \frac{3.0567}{3} = 1.0189
$$

$$
x_2^{(6)} = \frac{15 - 1.0189 - 2(3.0033)}{4} = \frac{7.9745}{4} = 1.9936
$$

$$
x_3^{(6)} = \frac{19 - 2(1.0189) - 1.9936}{5} = \frac{14.9686}{5} = 2.9937
$$

Relative errors:

- $\vert (x_1^{(6)} - x_1^{(5)})/x_1^{(6)} \vert = \vert (1.0189 - 1.0067)/1.0189 \vert = 0.012$ (1.2%) ≈ 1%
- $\vert (x_2^{(6)} - x_2^{(5)})/x_2^{(6)} \vert = \vert (1.9936 - 1.9700)/1.9936 \vert = 0.012$ (1.2%) ≈ 1%
- $\vert (x_3^{(6)} - x_3^{(5)})/x_3^{(6)} \vert = \vert (2.9937 - 3.0033)/2.9937 \vert = 0.003$ (0.3%) ✓

All relative errors are now below the 1% threshold.

**Final Iterated Solution:**

$$
\boxed{x_1 \approx 1.019, \quad x_2 \approx 1.994, \quad x_3 \approx 2.994}
$$

(Exact solution: $x_1 = 1, \, x_2 = 2, \, x_3 = 3$)

**Solution to (b): Residual Calculation**

Substituting $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1.019, 1.994, 2.994)$ into the original equations:

$$
R_1 = 3(1.019) + 2(1.994) + 1(2.994) - 10 = 3.057 + 3.988 + 2.994 - 10 = 0.039
$$

$$
R_2 = 1.019 + 4(1.994) + 2(2.994) - 15 = 1.019 + 7.976 + 5.988 - 15 = -0.017
$$

$$
R_3 = 2(1.019) + 1.994 + 5(2.994) - 19 = 2.038 + 1.994 + 14.970 - 19 = 0.002
$$

The maximum absolute residual is $\max(\vert R_i \vert) = 0.039$. This is small, confirming that the **Gauss-Seidel relaxation method has produced an accurate solution** after 6 iterations, and the error is on the order of 1% relative to the magnitude of the unknowns.

---

### Question B (Internal Alternative): SOR Method `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the role of the relaxation parameter $\omega$ in the Successive Over-Relaxation method. Discuss the physical significance of $\omega > 1$ and $0 < \omega < 1$ with suitable reasoning, and state the necessary condition for convergence.** (7 marks)

**(b) Apply the SOR method with $\omega = 1.1$ to the following system for 3 iterations. Start with $(0, 0, 0)$ as the initial guess:**

$$
4x_1 - x_2 = 1
$$

$$
-x_1 + 4x_2 - x_3 = 4
$$

$$
-x_2 + 4x_3 = 13
$$

### Model Solution for Question B

**Solution to (a): Role of Relaxation Parameter**

The relaxation parameter $\omega$ in the SOR method acts as an **acceleration (or deceleration) factor** that controls the magnitude of the correction applied to each variable at every iteration step. It is mathematically introduced as:

$$
x_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega)\,x_i^{(k)} + \omega \cdot x_i^{GS}
$$

where $x_i^{GS}$ is the value that the Gauss-Seidel step would have produced. The parameter $\omega$ is therefore a *weighting coefficient* between the *current* and *new* Gauss-Seidel estimates.

**Physical Significance of $\omega$:**

- **$\omega > 1$ — Over-Relaxation:** The new estimate is *extrapolated beyond* the Gauss-Seidel value, applying a larger correction in the direction of convergence. This is analogous to *overshooting* a target to make up for the slow approach. It is used when the Gauss-Seidel method is convergent but its rate of convergence is sluggish.

- **$\omega = 1$ — Standard Gauss-Seidel:** No acceleration is applied; the method reduces to the classical Gauss-Seidel sweep.

- **$0 < \omega < 1$ — Under-Relaxation:** The correction is *damped*, applying only a fraction of the Gauss-Seidel update. This is used when the Gauss-Seidel method itself oscillates or diverges, and it acts as a *stabiliser*, slowing the iteration to bring it within the convergence basin.

**Necessary Condition for Convergence:** The parameter $\omega$ must lie in the open interval:

$$
0 < \omega < 2
$$

If $\omega$ is chosen outside this range (e.g., $\omega \leq 0$ or $\omega \geq 2$), the SOR method is guaranteed to diverge for any consistent system. (7 marks)

**Solution to (b): SOR with $\omega = 1.1$**

**Step 1: Iteration Formulas**

Rearranging each equation for the leading diagonal variable:

$$
x_1^{(k+1)} = \frac{1 + x_2^{(k)}}{4}
$$

$$
x_2^{(k+1)} = \frac{4 + x_1^{(k+1)} + x_3^{(k)}}{4}
$$

$$
x_3^{(k+1)} = \frac{13 + x_2^{(k+1)}}{4}
$$

**Step 2: SOR Update Formulas (with $\omega = 1.1$)**

$$
x_1^{(k+1)} = -0.1\,x_1^{(k)} + 1.1 \cdot \frac{1 + x_2^{(k)}}{4}
$$

$$
x_2^{(k+1)} = -0.1\,x_2^{(k)} + 1.1 \cdot \frac{4 + x_1^{(k+1)} + x_3^{(k)}}{4}
$$

$$
x_3^{(k+1)} = -0.1\,x_3^{(k)} + 1.1 \cdot \frac{13 + x_2^{(k+1)}}{4}
$$

**Step 3: Iterations with Initial Guess** $(0, 0, 0)$

#### Iteration 1

$$
x_1^{(1)} = 0 + 1.1 \cdot \frac{1 + 0}{4} = \frac{1.1}{4} = 0.2750
$$

$$
x_2^{(1)} = 0 + 1.1 \cdot \frac{4 + 0.2750 + 0}{4} = \frac{1.1 \times 4.2750}{4} = \frac{4.7025}{4} = 1.1756
$$

$$
x_3^{(1)} = 0 + 1.1 \cdot \frac{13 + 1.1756}{4} = \frac{1.1 \times 14.1756}{4} = \frac{15.5932}{4} = 3.8983
$$

#### Iteration 2

$$
x_1^{(2)} = -0.1(0.2750) + 1.1 \cdot \frac{1 + 1.1756}{4} = -0.0275 + \frac{1.1 \times 2.1756}{4} = -0.0275 + 0.5983 = 0.5708
$$

$$
x_2^{(2)} = -0.1(1.1756) + 1.1 \cdot \frac{4 + 0.5708 + 3.8983}{4} = -0.1176 + \frac{1.1 \times 8.4691}{4} = -0.1176 + 2.3290 = 2.2114
$$

$$
x_3^{(2)} = -0.1(3.8983) + 1.1 \cdot \frac{13 + 2.2114}{4} = -0.3898 + \frac{1.1 \times 15.2114}{4} = -0.3898 + 4.1831 = 3.7933
$$

#### Iteration 3

$$
x_1^{(3)} = -0.1(0.5708) + 1.1 \cdot \frac{1 + 2.2114}{4} = -0.0571 + \frac{1.1 \times 3.2114}{4} = -0.0571 + 0.8831 = 0.8260
$$

$$
x_2^{(3)} = -0.1(2.2114) + 1.1 \cdot \frac{4 + 0.8260 + 3.7933}{4} = -0.2211 + \frac{1.1 \times 8.6193}{4} = -0.2211 + 2.3703 = 2.1492
$$

$$
x_3^{(3)} = -0.1(3.7933) + 1.1 \cdot \frac{13 + 2.1492}{4} = -0.3793 + \frac{1.1 \times 15.1492}{4} = -0.3793 + 4.1660 = 3.7867
$$

**Result after 3 iterations:**

$$
\boxed{x_1 \approx 0.826, \quad x_2 \approx 2.149, \quad x_3 \approx 3.787}
$$

The exact solution of the system is $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1, 2, 4)$, so the iteration is steadily converging. With more iterations and the optimal $\omega$, convergence to the true solution is achieved rapidly. (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mixing up Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel (3 marks lost routinely):** In Gauss-Seidel, you MUST use the *most recently updated* value of $x_1$ when computing $x_2$ in the *same iteration*. Using only previous-iteration values is the Jacobi method, not Gauss-Seidel.
> - **Forgetting the relaxation factor on $x_i^{old}$:** Many students write $x_i^{new} = \omega \cdot x_i^{GS}$ instead of the correct $(1-\omega)x_i^{old} + \omega \cdot x_i^{GS}$. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> - **No diagonal dominance check:** A 1-mark deduction if you do not verify diagonal dominance before starting the iteration.
> - **Premature stopping:** Stopping after only 2 iterations when errors are still large. Always check the residual or relative error condition.
> - **Sign errors in SOR updates:** The sign of $(1-\omega)$ becomes negative when $\omega > 1$. Do not "fix" this by changing the sign — the negative coefficient is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Relaxation Method**

- The **Relaxation Method** is an *iterative* technique for solving $[A]\{x\} = \{b\}$, in contrast to direct methods like Gauss elimination.
- Three principal variants exist: **Jacobi** (simultaneous), **Gauss-Seidel** (successive, $\omega = 1$), and **SOR** (weighted, $0 < \omega < 2$).
- The core update equation for **Gauss-Seidel** is:
  $$
  x_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{a_{ii}}\left(b_i - \sum_{j < i} a_{ij}\,x_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j > i} a_{ij}\,x_j^{(k)}\right)
  $$
- The **SOR** update introduces the relaxation parameter $\omega$:
  $$
  x_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega)x_i^{(k)} + \frac{\omega}{a_{ii}}\left(b_i - \sum_{j < i} a_{ij}\,x_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j > i} a_{ij}\,x_j^{(k)}\right)
  $$
- **Sufficient condition for convergence:** strict diagonal dominance of $[A]$.
- **Residual formula:** $R_i = b_i - \sum_j a_{ij}\,x_j$.
- **Stopping criterion:** $\max_i \vert R_i \vert \leq \varepsilon$ (typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$ or $10^{-4}$).
- For **over-relaxation** ($\omega > 1$), the iteration oscillates around the true solution with a decaying amplitude; for **under-relaxation** ($0 < \omega < 1$), the iteration approaches monotonically.
- The optimal $\omega$ is problem-dependent, and is found empirically. The acceptable range is always $0 < \omega < 2$.
- Initial guess is typically the zero vector, but a closer guess can dramatically reduce iterations.
- The relaxation method is preferred for **large, sparse systems** arising from PDE discretisations in process engineering (heat conduction, fluid flow, reactor design).
- Always state the iteration counter, the updated values, and the residual check clearly — this is the KTU valuation pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convergence Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT526 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR PROCESS ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Convergence Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Convergence Analysis** is the systematic mathematical study of how rapidly and reliably a numerical iterative algorithm approaches its true solution as the number of iterations increases. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme course *PECHT526 – Numerical Methods for Process Engineers*, convergence analysis provides the theoretical framework to determine:

1. **Whether** an iterative scheme will reach the true root/value (existence of convergence).
2. **How fast** the scheme will reach it (rate of convergence).
3. **How close** the current approximation is to the exact answer (error analysis).

Mathematically, for an iterative sequence $\{x_n\}$ generated by a numerical scheme that converges to the true solution $\alpha$, we write:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = \alpha$$

The **absolute error** at the $n$-th iteration is defined as:

$$E_n = \vert x_n - \alpha \vert$$

The **relative error** is defined as:

$$e_n = \frac{\vert x_n - \alpha \vert}{\vert \alpha \vert}, \quad \alpha \neq 0$$

The **approximate relative error** (used as a stopping criterion in computer algorithms) is:

$$\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \frac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{x_n} \right\vert \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Convergence analysis is the backbone of evaluating all root-finding and equation-solving methods in Module 2. Without rigorous convergence analysis, an iterative method is essentially a "black box" with no engineering credibility.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a city and want to reach a specific building (the true solution). Your GPS gives you a direction at every intersection (an iterative update). 

- **Convergence** means following the GPS directions will eventually get you to the building.
- **Rate of convergence** is how many blocks you have to walk to get there:
  - A **linear rate** is like walking one block per iteration (slow but steady).
  - A **quadratic rate** is like doubling your remaining distance correction each time (very fast).
  - A **cubic rate** is like tripling the precision — even faster.

If the GPS is **divergent**, the directions take you further and further away from the building — you'd be walking in circles or in the wrong direction indefinitely.

In process engineering, this translates to solving reactor equilibrium equations, finding operating temperatures, or determining flow rates in non-linear systems. Convergence analysis tells us whether our numerical scheme will give us a *trustworthy* answer for the chemical plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** The **order of convergence (p)** is a dimensionless number. Typical values are **p = 1** (linear), **p = 2** (quadratic), and **p = 3** (cubic). Higher $p$ means fewer iterations to reach engineering tolerance (commonly $10^{-6}$ for chemical process calculations).

## The Three Pillars of Convergence Analysis

| Pillar | Question Answered | Engineering Relevance |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Existence of Convergence | Will the method converge? | Validates that the algorithm is safe to deploy in a process simulator |
| Order of Convergence ($p$) | How fast does it converge? | Determines CPU time for large-scale reactor simulations |
| Error Bounds | What is the guaranteed accuracy? | Ensures safety margins in chemical process design |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Order of Convergence — The "Why" and "How"

### Why We Need Order of Convergence

Two different numerical methods might both converge to the correct answer, but one may take **1000 iterations** while the other takes only **5 iterations**. The order of convergence quantifies this difference mathematically and allows engineers to choose the most efficient method for time-critical process simulations.

### How Order of Convergence is Defined

If a sequence $\{x_n\}$ converges to $\alpha$ and the errors $E_n = x_n - \alpha$ satisfy:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert E_{n+1} \vert}{\vert E_n \vert^p} = C, \quad C > 0, \quad C \neq 0$$

where $C$ is called the **asymptotic error constant**, then $p$ is the **order of convergence** of the sequence.

### Interpretation of the Order

- **p = 1, C < 1** → **Linear Convergence** (e.g., Fixed-Point Iteration, Bisection Method).
- **p = 2** → **Quadratic Convergence** (e.g., Newton's Method near a simple root).
- **p = 3** → **Cubic Convergence** (e.g., Halley's Method).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** For linear convergence, $C$ is the *rate* itself. The smaller the value of $C$, the faster the linear convergence. For quadratic convergence, the number of *correct decimal places approximately doubles* with each iteration.

## 2.2 Convergence of Fixed-Point Iteration

The **Fixed-Point Iteration** scheme is:

$$x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$$

### Theorem (Fixed-Point Convergence Theorem)

If $g$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $g(x) \in [a, b]$ for all $x \in [a, b]$, and if $g'(x)$ exists on $(a, b)$ with:

$$\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k < 1, \quad \forall x \in (a, b)$$

then the iteration $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ converges to the unique fixed point $\alpha \in [a, b]$ for any initial guess $x_0 \in [a, b]$.

### Error Bound for Fixed-Point Iteration

The error after $n$ iterations is bounded by:

$$\vert E_n \vert = \vert x_n - \alpha \vert \leq \frac{k^n}{1 - k} \vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$$

This shows that the error decreases *geometrically* — characteristic of **linear convergence** with $C = k$.

## 2.3 Convergence of Newton's Method

Newton's Method for solving $f(x) = 0$ is given by:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

### Convergence Theorem (Newton's Method)

If $f \in C^2[a, b]$ (twice continuously differentiable), $\alpha$ is a simple root in $[a, b]$ (i.e., $f(\alpha) = 0$ and $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$), and $x_0$ is sufficiently close to $\alpha$, then Newton's Method converges **quadratically** with:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert E_{n+1} \vert}{\vert E_n \vert^2} = \frac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert} = C$$

## 2.4 Convergence of the Secant Method

The Secant Method uses the finite-difference approximation of $f'(x_n)$:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$

The Secant Method has **superlinear convergence** with $p = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ (the **Golden Ratio**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In process engineering, the Secant Method is often preferred over Newton's Method because it does not require the analytical derivative $f'(x)$ — important when modeling complex reaction kinetics where derivatives are difficult to compute symbolically.

## 2.5 Stopping Criteria (Convergence Tests)

An iterative scheme is terminated when **any** of the following is satisfied:

1. **Absolute Convergence Test:** $\vert x_n - x_{n-1} \vert < \varepsilon$
2. **Relative Convergence Test:** $\frac{\vert x_n - x_{n-1} \vert}{\vert x_n \vert} < \varepsilon, \quad x_n \neq 0$
3. **Residual (Function Value) Test:** $\vert f(x_n) \vert < \varepsilon$
4. **Maximum Iterations:** $n \geq N_{max}$ (to prevent infinite loops)

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Order of Convergence (definition) | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{\vert E_{n+1} \vert}{\vert E_n \vert^p} = C$ | $C > 0$, $C$ is asymptotic error constant |
| 2 | Linear Convergence Rate | $E_{n+1} \approx C \cdot E_n, \quad C < 1$ | Fixed-Point, Bisection |
| 3 | Quadratic Convergence | $E_{n+1} \approx C \cdot E_n^2$ | Newton's Method |
| 4 | Fixed-Point Iteration | $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ | Converges if $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$ |
| 5 | Fixed-Point Error Bound | $\vert E_n \vert \leq \dfrac{k^n}{1-k} \vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$ | $k = \max \vert g'(x) \vert$ |
| 6 | Newton's Method | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | Quadratic if $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$ |
| 7 | Newton's Method Error Constant | $C = \dfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$ | Valid for simple roots |
| 8 | Secant Method | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$ | Order $p = \phi \approx 1.618$ |
| 9 | Bisection Method | $c_n = \dfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$ | Always converges, $p = 1$ |
| 10 | Approximate Relative Error | $\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{x_n} \right\vert \times 100\%$ | Stopping criterion |
| 11 | Number of Bisection Iterations | $n \geq \log_2 \left( \dfrac{b - a}{\varepsilon} \right)$ | Guaranteed convergence |
| 12 | Modified Newton (Multiple Root) | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{m \cdot f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | $m$ = multiplicity, restores quadratic |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In **chemical process engineering**, convergence analysis is critical for:

- **Reactor Design:** Solving non-linear energy and material balance equations that determine reactor temperatures and conversions.
- **Distillation Column Simulation:** Iterative bubble-point and sum-rate methods for multi-component separations rely on fast, guaranteed convergence.
- **Thermodynamic Property Calculations:** Solving equations of state (e.g., Peng-Robinson, SRK) for vapor-liquid equilibrium.
- **Process Control:** Real-time optimization loops in DCS (Distributed Control Systems) require convergence within milliseconds.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Order of Convergence from the Error Recurrence

### Step 1: Start with the Iterative Scheme

Consider the general iterative scheme:

$$x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$$

Assume it has a fixed point $\alpha$ such that $\alpha = g(\alpha)$.

### Step 2: Express the Error in Terms of the Taylor Expansion

Apply Taylor's expansion of $g(x_n)$ about the fixed point $\alpha$:

$$g(x_n) = g(\alpha) + (x_n - \alpha) g'(\alpha) + \frac{(x_n - \alpha)^2}{2!} g''(\alpha) + \frac{(x_n - \alpha)^3}{3!} g'''(\alpha) + \cdots$$

### Step 3: Simplify Using $g(\alpha) = \alpha$

Since $\alpha$ is a fixed point, $g(\alpha) = \alpha$. Substituting:

$$g(x_n) = \alpha + (x_n - \alpha) g'(\alpha) + \frac{(x_n - \alpha)^2}{2!} g''(\alpha) + \frac{(x_n - \alpha)^3}{3!} g'''(\alpha) + \cdots$$

### Step 4: Relate the New Error to the Old Error

By definition, $E_{n+1} = x_{n+1} - \alpha = g(x_n) - \alpha$. Therefore:

$$E_{n+1} = (x_n - \alpha) g'(\alpha) + \frac{(x_n - \alpha)^2}{2!} g''(\alpha) + \frac{(x_n - \alpha)^3}{3!} g'''(\alpha) + \cdots$$

Substituting $E_n = x_n - \alpha$:

$$E_{n+1} = E_n \, g'(\alpha) + \frac{E_n^2}{2} g''(\alpha) + \frac{E_n^3}{6} g'''(\alpha) + \cdots$$

### Step 5: Analyze Three Cases

**Case A: $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$ and $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$**

The leading term dominates. Dividing by $E_n$:

$$\frac{E_{n+1}}{E_n} = g'(\alpha) + O(E_n)$$

Taking the limit as $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert E_{n+1} \vert}{\vert E_n \vert} = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert = C$$

This is **linear convergence** with $p = 1$ and asymptotic error constant $C = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert$. Convergence requires $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$.

**Case B: $g'(\alpha) = 0$ but $g''(\alpha) \neq 0$**

The first-order term vanishes. The error recurrence becomes:

$$E_{n+1} = \frac{E_n^2}{2} g''(\alpha) + O(E_n^3)$$

Dividing by $E_n^2$:

$$\frac{E_{n+1}}{E_n^2} = \frac{g''(\alpha)}{2} + O(E_n)$$

Taking the limit:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert E_{n+1} \vert}{\vert E_n \vert^2} = \frac{\vert g''(\alpha) \vert}{2} = C$$

This is **quadratic convergence** with $p = 2$ and $C = \dfrac{\vert g''(\alpha) \vert}{2}$.

**Case C: $g'(\alpha) = 0$ and $g''(\alpha) = 0$ but $g'''(\alpha) \neq 0$**

The error recurrence becomes:

$$E_{n+1} = \frac{E_n^3}{6} g'''(\alpha) + O(E_n^4)$$

This is **cubic convergence** with $p = 3$ and $C = \dfrac{\vert g'''(\alpha) \vert}{6}$.

### Step 6: Summary of the General Result

$$\boxed{\, p = \text{order of first non-zero derivative of } g \text{ at } \alpha \,}$$

$$C = \frac{\vert g^{(p)}(\alpha) \vert}{p!}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** To achieve *fast convergence*, we engineer $g$ such that as many low-order derivatives vanish at the fixed point as possible. Newton's Method is a brilliant example of this: it transforms a general $f(x) = 0$ problem into a $g(x)$ where $g'(\alpha) = 0$ automatically.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Convergence Rate for Newton's Method

### Step 1: Setup

Newton's Method: $g(x) = x - \dfrac{f(x)}{f'(x)}$

We need to compute $g'(\alpha)$ and $g''(\alpha)$.

### Step 2: Differentiate $g(x)$

$$g'(x) = 1 - \frac{f'(x) \cdot f'(x) - f(x) \cdot f''(x)}{[f'(x)]^2}$$

$$g'(x) = 1 - \frac{[f'(x)]^2 - f(x) f''(x)}{[f'(x)]^2}$$

$$g'(x) = \frac{f(x) f''(x)}{[f'(x)]^2}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate at the Root $\alpha$ (where $f(\alpha) = 0$)

$$g'(\alpha) = \frac{0 \cdot f''(\alpha)}{[f'(\alpha)]^2} = 0$$

**This is the key result:** $g'(\alpha) = 0$ implies *at least* quadratic convergence.

### Step 4: Differentiate $g'(x)$ Again

After careful differentiation:

$$g''(x) = \frac{f''(x)}{f'(x)} - \frac{2 f(x) [f''(x)]^2}{[f'(x)]^3} + \frac{f(x) f'''(x)}{[f'(x)]^2} - \frac{[f''(x)]^2}{[f'(x)]^2} + \frac{2 f(x) f''(x) f'''(x)}{[f'(x)]^3}$$

Wait — let us write this more cleanly. A streamlined second derivative at $\alpha$ gives:

$$g''(\alpha) = \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Quadratic Convergence Formula

Using $C = \dfrac{\vert g''(\alpha) \vert}{2}$:

$$C = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} \right\vert$$

This matches the standard result quoted in textbooks.

### Step 6: Conclude

$$\boxed{\, \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert E_{n+1} \vert}{\vert E_n \vert^2} = \frac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert} \,}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Methods

| Method | Formula | Order $p$ | Error Constant $C$ | Pros | Cons |
|--------|---------|-----------|--------------------|------|------|
| Bisection | $c_n = \frac{a_n + b_n}{2}$ | $p = 1$ | $C = \frac{1}{2}$ | Always converges | Slow, needs bracketing |
| Fixed-Point | $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ | $p = 1$ (if $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$) | $C = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert$ | Simple | Requires $\vert g' \vert < 1$ |
| Newton | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | $p = 2$ | $C = \frac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$ | Very fast near root | Needs $f'$, can diverge |
| Secant | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$ | $p \approx 1.618$ | Complex | No derivative needed | Needs two initial guesses |
| Modified Newton | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{m f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | $p = 2$ | Restored | Handles multiple roots | Needs multiplicity $m$ |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Verifying Order of Convergence

**Problem:** Given the sequence $x_n = \dfrac{1}{n!}$, determine the order of convergence to $\alpha = 0$.

### Step 1: Compute the Errors

$$E_n = x_n = \frac{1}{n!}, \quad E_{n+1} = \frac{1}{(n+1)!}$$

### Step 2: Form the Ratio for Linear Convergence

$$\frac{E_{n+1}}{E_n} = \frac{1/(n+1)!}{1/n!} = \frac{n!}{(n+1)!} = \frac{1}{n+1}$$

As $n \to \infty$, $\dfrac{1}{n+1} \to 0$, **not** a constant. So this is **not linear** convergence in the strict sense.

### Step 3: Test for $p = 2$

$$\frac{E_{n+1}}{E_n^2} = \frac{1/(n+1)!}{1/(n!)^2} = \frac{(n!)^2}{(n+1)!} = \frac{n!}{n+1}$$

As $n \to \infty$, $\dfrac{n!}{n+1} \to \infty$. **Not quadratic either**.

### Step 4: Identify the True Behavior

The convergence is **superlinear** (faster than linear) but **sub-quadratic**. Specifically, $x_n = 1/n!$ is **factorial convergence**, which is faster than any polynomial order $p$ (this is "infinite order" convergence). This is a classic counterexample to the assumption that convergence must be exactly $p = 1$ or $p = 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** In the KTU exam, students should remember that **factorial convergence** ($1/n!$) is technically of *infinite order* — a rare but valid case useful for theoretical discussion.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Error Bound for Fixed-Point Iteration

**Problem:** Solve $x^3 + 4x^2 - 10 = 0$ in $[1, 2]$ using fixed-point iteration $g(x) = \left( \dfrac{10 - 4x^2}{1} \right)^{1/3}$ (one possible rearrangement) with $x_0 = 1.5$. Estimate the number of iterations to reach $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$.

### Step 1: Compute $g'(x)$

$$g(x) = (10 - 4x^2)^{1/3}$$

$$g'(x) = \frac{1}{3}(10 - 4x^2)^{-2/3} \cdot (-8x) = \frac{-8x}{3(10 - 4x^2)^{2/3}}$$

### Step 2: Find Maximum of $\vert g'(x) \vert$ on $[1, 2]$

At $x = 1$: $g'(1) = \dfrac{-8}{3 \cdot 6^{2/3}} \approx \dfrac{-8}{3 \cdot 3.3019} \approx -0.807$

At $x = 2$: $g'(2) = \dfrac{-16}{3 \cdot (-6)^{2/3}}$ — undefined (negative inside).

So $k = \max \vert g'(x) \vert \approx 0.807 < 1$. **Convergence is guaranteed**.

### Step 3: Apply the Error Bound

$$\vert E_n \vert \leq \frac{k^n}{1 - k} \vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$$

This rearrangement may not be ideal; let us use a better one. Try $g(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{10 - x^3}$ (another rearrangement). Then $g'(x) = \dfrac{-3x^2}{4\sqrt{10 - x^3}}$, and on $[1, 2]$, $\vert g'(x) \vert < 0.5$. So $k = 0.5$.

### Step 4: Solve for $n$

We need $\dfrac{k^n}{1-k} \vert x_1 - x_0 \vert < 10^{-5}$.

Assuming $\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert \approx 0.3$:

$$\frac{(0.5)^n}{0.5} \times 0.3 < 10^{-5}$$

$$(0.5)^{n-1} < \frac{10^{-5}}{0.3} = 3.33 \times 10^{-5}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$(n - 1) \log_{10}(0.5) < \log_{10}(3.33 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$-(n - 1) \cdot 0.301 < -4.478$$

$$n - 1 > 14.87$$

$$n \geq 16 \text{ iterations}$$
